topics.title: Chapter 1: Introduction To Short Exam
topics.content_html:
It is the one form among clinical oral exams (i.e: long cases, short cases and viva). The short-exam section in pediatrics covers a number of systems (most commonly HEENT, respiratory, cardiovascular, abdomen, lower motor) and many miscellaneous cases such as: dehydration, malnutrition, rickets, anemia, Down syndrome, burn, edema, shock, skin lesion and so on.
In most of medical and health science schools in Ethiopia, the candidate will be examined mostly after long case examination often in the morning time. There will be one or more short cases based on the examiner. The candidate is examined by two examiners (one senior and one resident). Each short exam usually takes 15 minutes.
Short cases tend to have a lead-in that is more directed, so a more focused examination is in order; the examiner may say: do chest examination, assess for signs of dehydration (DHN), assess for signs of edema …. Proficiency in short cases is dependent upon how much you practice preferably on a daily basis on real patients i.e “practice, practice, practice… makes perfect!” Several examination problems require a great deal of clinical material to be covered in 15 minutes; so a well prepared routine is essential. This book gives just an outline of an approach to most of the commonly seen short-case topics.
Often the examiners will instruct that you may either talk as you proceed or examine in silence and summarise at the completion of the examination. Most candidates prefer the former method as it allows the examiners to see and hear how you think and should a questionable finding be noted; the examiners may guide you at that point. The examiner wants to see how you get the finding and also how you analyze it not on what you get.
Do not wait for order from the examiner for each step of an examination and do not say “I would now like to look at … or now I am going to do …” just<boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout>do it! Be confident but don’t be argumentative. If you become angry with an uncooperative child, you may fail; if you inadvertently hurt a child, such as in a joint examination, you may well fail and also inappropriate techniques of abdominal examination may lead to life threatening conditions like splenic rupture. If you do not follow the instructions of the provided introduction, you will fail; so keep your boundary of level of confidence and answer what you are asked.
At the completion of your examination, you may mention further areas that you would like to examine next and ask if time permits and then present a succinct summary of your findings with the most likely diagnosis, followed by a logically presented differential diagnosis. The differential diagnosis need not be exhaustive. In particular, it should never be a rote-learnt list for a certain type of case, but it should be relevant only to the particular child you have just examined. Be careful that the first diseases you mention are not inappropriate suggestions. Beware of ‘automatic list’ responses.
Avoid terms like “I think perhaps…” you need to consciously tighten up your description of the patient’s finding you get. The examiner judges your: level of confidence, the way of approach to the patient, technique and steps of examination, timing, ability to interpret physical findings and case based discussion of investigations.
Remember, before starting to examine: communicate to the patient and attendant. Give a greeting and introduce yourself (act as a physician). Explain for the patient and attendant what you are going to do and ask for permission to proceed.

topics.title: Chapter 2: Vital Signs
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Pulse Rate <boldout>
  • The radial pulse is used commonly to count the heart rate
  • With the pads of your index and middle fingers, compress the radial artery until you detect a maximal pulsation
  • In neonates and young infants counting apical heart beat is possible
  • The rate, rhythm and volume of the pulse should be noted
  • Normal values of the pulse rate depend on sex and age of the patient
  • The rate should be counted for a full one minute
  • While sleeping, infant heart rates may drop significantly
  • Normal heart rate is usually closes to 2-3 times normal respiratory rate for age
<img:t1.png>
<boldin><italicin>Table <italicout><italicin><italicout><italicin><italicout><italicin><italicout><italicin>1<italicout><italicin><italicout><italicin>: <italicout>Pulse Rates at Rest<boldout>

Taken from Nelson text book of pediatrics 21st edition, chapter 449, table 449.4
<boldin>Respiratory rate<boldout>
  • Try to make the patient calm as much as possible
  • Sleeping respiratory rates are more reproducible in infants than those obtained during feeding or activity
  • The rate should be counted for a full one minute
<boldin>• Pattern of breathing should be noted<boldout>
  • Normal respiratory rate varies with age of the patient
<img:t2.png>
Where: PR – pulse rate in beats/min, RR – respiratory rates in breaths/min
Taken from Nelson text book of pediatrics 21st edition, chapter 81, table 81.1
<boldin>Temperature<boldout>
<boldin>• Temperature can be measured through orally, rectally, axillary and tympanic membrane:<boldout>

<boldin><italicin>Oral temperature<italicout><boldout>
  • Should not be taken in infants and young children
<boldin>• Choose either glass or electronic thermometer<boldout>
  • Insert the thermometer under the tongue and order the patient to clos both lips
  • It should not be taken if the patient took hot or cold drinks or foods a short while ago
  • Average oral temperature is 37 °c (98.6 °F) which fluctuates considerably from the early morning to the late afternoon

<boldin><italicin>Rectal temperature<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Preferable in infants<boldout>
  • A common practice in unresponsive patients or go those at risk of biting down on the thermometer
  • Select a thermometer with stubby tip, lubricate it and insert 2 cm in to<boldin> <boldout>the anal canal in a direction pointing to the umbilicus and remove it after 3 minutes
  • Rectal temperature is higher than oral temperature by about 0.4-0.5 °c

<boldin><italicin>Axillary temperature<italicout><boldout>
  • Axillary temperature is lower than oral temperature by 0.5-1°c
<boldin>• Less accurate than other measurements<boldout>
  • The most employed method of measurement due to its easiness to employ

<boldin><italicin>Tympanic membrane temperature <italicout><boldout>
  • More variable than oral or rectal temperature
  • Make sure the external auditory canal is free of cerumen
  • Position the probe in the canal and wait for 2-3 seconds until the digital reading appears
<boldin>• It measures core body temperature<boldout>
  • Its reading is higher than oral temperature by 0.8°c
<boldin><italicin>Remember<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>In rectal and oral temperature measurement, thermometers should not be shared to prevent transmission of infection<italicout>
  • <italicin>Since thermometers read in °C or °F, better to know how to covert °C to °F and the reverse; i.e<italicout>
  • <italicin>°C = (°F – 32) x <italicout>
  • <italicin>°F = (°C x <italicout><italicin>) + 32<italicout>
<boldin><italicin>Blood Pressure (BP)<italicout><boldout>
  • Use a cuff that fits the patient’s arm
  • Blood pressure cuff should cover approximately two thirds of the arm
  • Too small a cuff yields spuriously high pressure readings and too large a cuff yields spuriously low pressure reading

<boldin>Steps in measuring blood pressure<boldout>
  • The patient should avoid smoking or drinking caffeinated beverages for 30 minutes before the blood pressure is taken and rest for at least 5 minutes
  • the examining room should be quiet and comfortably warm
  • Make sure the arm selected is free of clothing
  • Palpate the brachial artery to confirm that it has a viable pulse
  • Position the arm so that the brachial artery, at the antecubital crease, is the heart level
  • Center the inflatable bladder over the brachial artery
  • The lower border of the cuff should be 2.5cm above the antecubital crease
  • Position the patient’s arm so that it is slightly flexed at the elbow
  • To determine how high to the cuff pressure; feel the radial artery with the fingers of one hand and rapidly inflate the cuff until the radial pulse disappears. Read this pressure on the manometer and add 30 mmHg
  • Place the bell of stethoscope lightly over the brachial artery, taking care to make an air seal with its full rim. Because the sounds to be heard (Korotkoff sounds) are relatively low in pitch, they are heard better with the bell
  • Inflate the cuff rapidly to the level just determined and then deflate it slowly, at a rate of about 2 to 3 mmHg per second. Note the level at which you hear the sounds of at least two consecutive beats. This is the systolic pressure.
  • Continue to lower the pressure slowly. The disappearance point, usually only a few mmHg below the muffling point, is the best estimate of diastolic pressure


  • Blood pressure can also be measured at lower extremities (popliteal artery), in cases of when we have too large cuff size that unfit to the upper arm and do not have another appropriate cuff size. After the age of infancy, systolic and diastolic blood pressure in the legs are 20 and 10 mmHg higher than the arms respectively.
  • Four extremity blood pressure measurement as an initial screening is recommended to pick some abnormalities like coarctation of aorta and takayasu syndrome
  • During inspiration, both the systolic and diastolic blood pressure decreased due to pooling of blood in the pulmonary bed
  • Measuring blood pressure while the child is crying can increase the blood pressure falsely by 40-50mmHg from the baseline
<boldin>• Blood pressure interpretation according to the 2017 AHA definition updates:<boldout>
  • Normal blood pressure 	- BP between  5thand 90thcentile for age and sex
  • Elevated blood pressure - BP ≥ 90th  to /< 95thcentile for age and sex
  • Stage I hypertension  - BP ≥ 95th to (95thcentile<boldin> +<boldout> 11 mmHg)
  • Stage II hypertension 	- BP ≥ (95thcentile  <boldin>+<boldout> 12mmHg)
  • If the systolic and diastolic blood pressure interpretations fall in different stages, take the severe one to stage. E.g. if systolic blood pressure is classified as elevated blood pressure and  diastolic blood pressure classified as stage II hypertension  , our final interpretation is the patient has stage II hypertension (since we take the severe one).

topics.title: Chapter 3: General Appearance
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner<boldout>
<boldin>• What do you see?<boldout>
<boldin>• Possible differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Evidence for your differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>Introduction <boldout>
  • It is a gross analysis of the patient’s physique, posture, complexion, nutritional status, and emotional status, which constitute the individual’s appearance.
  • Observation has a crucial role than manipulating the child unnecessarily
  • It includes: Mentation, health status, Cardio-respiratory status, nutritional status, skin color, gross dysmorphic feature, posture and movement
  • For the question “What do you see?”, asses all the above parameters as fast as possible and report only the positive finding you get
  • If you asked to assess “general appearance”, report both positive and negative findings


<boldin>Mental status<boldout>
<boldin>• Playful, Irritable, Sleepy<boldout>
<boldin>• Conscious, lethargic, obtunded, stupor or comatose<boldout>
  • Conscious  - Responds for normal tone of voice
  • Lethargic   - Responds for loud voice but not for normal tone
  • Obtunded   - Responds for a gentle shake of a patient but not for loud voice
  • Stupor        - Responds for painful stimuli but not for gentle shake
  • Comatose   - No response for repeated painful stimuli
<boldin>Health status<boldout>
<boldin>• Healthy looking<boldout>
<boldin>• Acute sick looking<boldout>
<boldin>• Chronic sick looking<boldout>
  • Sick looking – when it is difficult to say acute or chronic sick looking
<boldin>• Acute sick looking on chronic base<boldout>
<img:t3.png>
<boldin>Cardio-pulmonary status<boldout><boldin>; <boldout>in:<boldin> <boldout>
<boldin>Cardiac distress:<boldout>
<boldin>• Distended neck vein<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiac position (orthopnea)<boldout>
<boldin>• Head nodding<boldout>
<boldin>Respiratory distress:<boldout>
<boldin>• Stridor, Grunting<boldout>
<boldin>• Central cyanosis<boldout>
<boldin>Nutritional status; <boldout>looks:
<boldin>• Well-nourished<boldout>
<boldin>• Undernourished<boldout>
<boldin>• Overnourished<boldout>
<boldin>Malnutrition ~ Under nutrition<boldout>
<boldin>• Emaciated<boldout>
<boldin>• Bony /& skinny appearance<boldout>
<boldin>• Visible zygomatic bone<boldout>
<boldin>• Hair color change<boldout>
<boldin>• Edema of nutritional origin<boldout>
<boldin>Over nutrition<boldout>
<boldin>• Looks obese<boldout>
<boldin>Skin color<boldout>
<boldin>• Pink – Normal<boldout>
<boldin>• Pallor – Anemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Ruddy or plethora – Polycythemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Yellow – Jaundice<boldout>
<boldin>• Greenish – Meconium<boldout>
<boldin>Gross dysmorphic feature<boldout>
<boldin>• Cleft lip<boldout>
<boldin>• Big tongue<boldout>
<boldin>• Retrognathia<boldout>
<boldin>• Microphthalmia<boldout>
<boldin>• Low seated ears<boldout>
<boldin>• Flat nasal bridge ……<boldout>
<boldin>Posture and movement<boldout>
  • Especially used for neurological disease and in neonatal evaluation

topics.title: Chapter 4
topics.content_html:
<boldin>HEENT<boldout> = <boldin>H<boldout>ead, <boldin>E<boldout>ar, <boldin>E<boldout>ye, <boldin>N<boldout>ose, Mouth and <boldin>T<boldout>hroat
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner<boldout>
<boldin>• Do HEENT examination?<boldout>
<boldin>• Differential diagnosis for your findings<boldout>
<boldin>• Evidence for your differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>Head<boldout>
<boldin>Look for:<boldout>
  • Hair – amount, color, texture, distribution, pluckability
  • Shape – caput quadratum, frontal bossing, …
<boldin>• Size – normal, big or small<boldout>
<boldin>• Masses<boldout>
<boldin>• Depression (Craniotabes)<boldout>
<boldin>• Tenderness of scalp<boldout>
<boldin>• Fontanel<boldout>
<boldin>• Size /& closure<boldout>
  • Surface (Flat – normal, Depressed – dehydration or Bulged)
<boldin>• Facial puffiness or periorbital edema<boldout>
<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout><boldin> <boldout>
<boldin>Macrocephaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Rickets<boldout>
<boldin>• Hydrocephalus (congenital or acquired)<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypothyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>• Achondroplasia<boldout>
<boldin>• Storage disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Intracranial hemorrhage<boldout>
<boldin>• Gigantism<boldout>
<boldin>• Familial- autosomal dominant<boldout>
<boldin>Microcephaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Severe malnutrition<boldout>
<boldin>• AIDS<boldout>
<boldin>• TORCH infection<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperthyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>• Craniosynostosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Dawn, Patau or Edward syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>Frontal bossing<boldout>
<boldin>• Rickets<boldout>
<boldin>• Thalasemia major<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital syphilis<boldout>
<boldin>• Achondroplasia<boldout>
<boldin>• Hurler’s syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Cleidocranial syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Ectodermal dysplasia<boldout>
<boldin>• Pyknodysostosis<boldout>
<boldin>Bulged fontanel<boldout>
<boldin>• Crying infant<boldout>
<boldin>• Meningitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Intracranial bleeding<boldout>
<boldin>• Hydrocephalus<boldout>
<boldin>• Tumor<boldout>
<boldin>• Pseudotumurcerebri<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperparathyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>Wide fontanel<boldout>
<boldin>• Rickets<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypothyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>• Hydrocephalus<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital rubella syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Osteogenesis imperfect<boldout>
<boldin>• Prematurity<boldout>
<boldin>• Down syndrome, Patau syndrome, Edward syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>Craniotabes<boldout>
<boldin>• Physiological<boldout>
<boldin>• Rickets<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital syphilis<boldout>
<boldin>• Hydrocephalus<boldout>
<boldin>• Osteogenesis imperfect<boldout>
<boldin>• Premature infants<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Posterior fontanel is (1x1 cm) closed at the age of 2<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>months<italicout>
  • <italicin>Anterior fontanel is (2 x2±1 cm), maximally enlarged and closed at the age of 6 months <italicout><italicin>and 9-18 <italicout><italicin>months<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>respectively<italicout>
<boldin>Ear <boldout>
<boldin>Look for<boldout>
<boldin>• Shape of the ear<boldout>
  • Position of the ear – normal, low or high seated ear
<boldin>• Discharge – pus, blood or othorrhea<boldout>
<boldin>• Skin color change<boldout>
<boldin>• Swelling<boldout>
<boldin>• Mastoid /& tragus tenderness<boldout>
  • By using otoscopy – look at tympanic membrane: hyperemia, bulging, perforations, whitish exudates

<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>Low set ear<boldout>
<boldin>• Dawn syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Turner syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Trisomy 17-18, 13-15<boldout>
<boldin>• Renal agenesis (potter facies)<boldout>
<boldin>• Treacher-Collins syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Cri-du-chat syndrome<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin>: Low set ear<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Draw an imaginary line between inner and outer canthi which bisect the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>ears<italicout>
  • <italicin>Normally divides into upper 1/3<italicout><italicin>rd <italicout><italicin>and lower 2/3<italicout><italicin>rd<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>portions<italicout>
  • <italicin>When less than 20% comes above this line, it is low set<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>ear<italicout>

<boldin>Eye<boldout>
<boldin>Look for<boldout>
  • Excessive discharge   →<boldin> <boldout><italicin>Conjunctivitis, keratitis,<italicout><italicin> foreign body<italicout>
<boldin>• Periorbital edema         <boldin>→ <boldout><italicin>Nephrotic syndrome, kwashiorkor<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Orbital mass                <boldin>→ <boldout><italicin>Retinoblastoma…<italicout><boldout>
  • Sunkening of eyeball   <boldin>→ <boldout><italicin>Dehydration, marasmus,<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>…<italicout>
  • Palpebral fissure          <boldin>→ <boldout><italicin>Normal, slant (down<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>syndrome)<italicout>
<boldin>• Sclera                           <boldin>→ <boldout><italicin>Icteric, non-icteric, bitot’s<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>spot<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Conjunctiva                 <boldin>→ <boldout><italicin>Pink, pale, injection,<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>hemorrhage<italicout><boldout>
  • Lid lag, lid retraction   <boldin>→ <boldout><italicin>Graves’<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>disease<italicout>
<boldin>• Xanthelasma                <boldin>→ <boldout><italicin>Dyslipidemia<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>Lid lag<boldout>
  • Steady the patient’s head with one hand
  • Ask the patient to look at your finger
  • Ask the patient to look up and down following your finger
  • The lid may lag while the eyeball move downward and the upper sclera become visible
<boldin>• Thyrotoxicosis, Graves’ disease<boldout>
<boldin>Lid retraction<boldout>
<boldin>• Visibility of upper sclera at rest<boldout>
<boldin>• Due to spasm of upper eyelid<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypoglycemia, thyroid disease<boldout>
<boldin>Xanthelasma<boldout>
<boldin>• Subcutaneous lipid deposit at periorbital area<boldout>
<boldin>• Signify presence of lipid disorder (dyslipidemia)<boldout>
<boldin>• Not common in children<boldout>
<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>Orbital mass<boldout>
<boldin>• Rhabdomyosarcoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Neuroblastoma (metastasis)<boldout>
<boldin>• Orbital abscess<boldout>
<boldin>• Retinoblastoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemangioma<boldout>
<boldin>• Chloroma<boldout>
<boldin>• Orbital hematoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Orbital/Preseptal cellulitis<boldout>
<boldin>Nose <boldout>
<boldin>Look for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Nasal bridge<boldout>
<boldin>• Septum<boldout>
<boldin>• Polyp<boldout>
<boldin>• Patency<boldout>
<boldin>• Discharge<boldout>
<boldin>• Active bleeding<boldout>
<boldin>• Sinus tenderness<boldout>
<boldin>Mouth /& Throat<boldout>
<boldin>Look for<boldout>
  • Lip - ulcer, fissures and cracks, cyanosis, dryness
  • Mouth – open, odor (haluthosis, acetone breath…)
  • Mucous membrane – wetness or dryness, color (pink or pale), koplik’s spots, thrush
<boldin>• Gum - bleeding, ulcers<boldout>
  • Teeth – number of teeth, caries, extractions and dentures
  • Tongue – protruding, coating, fissures, atrophy, color, deviation, dryness
  • Pharynx - central uvula, tonsillar swelling and color change
  • Length of the neck – short, long or normal
<boldin>• Neck mass<boldout>
<boldin>• Neck stiffness<boldout>
  • Any sinus tracts or fistula over the neck
<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>Macroglossia (big tongue)<boldout>
<boldin>• Cretinism<boldout>
<boldin>• Glycogen storage disease (Pompe disease)<boldout>
<boldin>• Hurler’s disease<boldout>
  • Beckwith-Wiedeman syndrome: An overgrowth disorder characterized by macrosomia, macroglossia, organomegaly and developmental abnormalities (in particular abdominal wall defect (Omphalocele))
  • Down syndrome: Tongue is normal in size but oral cavity is small and the child usually protrudes the tongue
<boldin>• Mass lesion growth from the tongue<boldout>
<boldin>• Rhabdomyosarcoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Neurofibromatosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemangioma<boldout>
<boldin>Delayed dentition<boldout>
<boldin>• Rickets<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypothyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypopituitarism<boldout>
<boldin>• Down syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Cleidocranial dysplasia<boldout>
<boldin>• Constitutional delay<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note:<italicout><boldout>
<italicin>As a principle while examining the baby from head to toe, ear and throat examinations are the last to be done among the systems to avoid disturbance of the baby.<italicout>

topics.title: Chapter 5: Respiratory System Examination
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner:<boldout>
  • Do respiratory system examination i.e. both anterior /& posterior chest
  • Do anterior or posterior chest examination i.e. only the one you ordered
<boldin>• Assess for signs of respiratory distress<boldout>
  • What is the pathology for your findings? Consolidation, effusion…
  • What is the likely diagnosis for your particular pathology?
<boldin>• Evidences for your differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Investigations for your differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>Introduction to lung anatomy<boldout>
  • Anteriorly, the apex of each lung rises about 2-4cm above the inner third of the clavicle
  • The lower border of the lung crosses the
  • 6th rib at the mid-clavicular line
  • 8th rib at the mid-axillary line and
  • 10th rib at the posterior axillary line
  • The right lung has 3 lobes; upper, middle and lower lobes
  • The left lung has 2 lobes; upper and lower lobes
  • The trachea bifurcates into main bronchi at the level of sternal angle anteriorly and the 4ththoracic spinous process posteriorly

<boldin>Approaches to examine<boldout>
<boldin>• Inspection<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpation<boldout>
<boldin>• Percussion<boldout>
<boldin>• Auscultation<boldout>
<boldin>INSPECTION<boldout>
<boldin>Look for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Breathing pattern<boldout>
<boldin>• Quality of voice<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest shape<boldout>
<boldin>• Signs of respiratory distress<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest expansion – symmetrical or not<boldout>
<boldin>• Cyanosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Clubbing<boldout>
• <boldin>Breathing<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>pattern<boldout>
  • Slow breathing - Occurs in drug-induced respiratory depression (E.g. Barbiturate poisoning)
  • Kussmaul‘s breathing - Fast, deep and labored breathing usually occurs in metabolic acidosis
• Rapid shallow breathing<boldin> <boldout>
<boldin>• Due to hypoxia in respiratory diseases<boldout>
<boldin>• Sign of alveolar or parenchymal disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Restrictive disorders like pneumonia, pulmonary edema<boldout>
<boldin>• Slow and deep breathing<boldout>
<boldin>• Sign of airway obstruction<boldout>
  • Obstructive disorders like croup, foreign body aspiration
<boldin>• Paradoxical respiration<boldout>
  • The abdomen sucks inwards with inspiration due to diaphragmatic paralysis
  • Normally it pouches outwards due to diaphragmatic descent
<boldin>• Apnea<boldout>
  • Cessation of breathing for over 20 seconds or any duration if accompied by cyanosis, pallor, hypotonia, desaturation on spo2 and bradycardia
  • Classified as central (occurs when inspiratory effort is absent), obstructive (occurs when inspiratory effort persists but airway obstruction present) and mixed apnea

• <boldin>Quality of voice<boldout>
<boldin>• Normal<boldout>
<boldin>• Wheeze<boldout>
<boldin>• Stridor<boldout>
<boldin>• Hoarseness of voice<boldout>
<boldin>• Grunting<boldout>
<boldin>• Snoring<boldout>
• <boldin>Chest<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>shape<boldout>
<boldin>• Barrel chest<boldout>
  • Increased anterio-posterior diameter of the chest in comparison to lateral diameter of the chest.
  • Most often associated with obstructive lesions like emphysema
<boldin>• Funnel chest (Pectus excavatum)<boldout>
  • Depressed sternum (caved-in or sunken appearance of the chest).
  • It is the most common congenital deformity of the anterior chest wall.
  • Causes can be rickets, Noonan syndrome or Marfan syndrome
  • Pigeon chest (Pectus carinatum): Anteriorly displaced sternum with depressed costal cartilage
<boldin>• Harrison‘s sulcus<boldout>
  • A horizontal groove along the lower border of the thorax corresponding to the costal insertion of the diaphragm
  • Usually caused by rickets, chronic asthma or obstructive respiratory disease
<boldin>• Kyphosis<boldout>
  • Excessive convex curvature of spine occurring in the thoracic and sacral regions
  • Can result from degenerative diseases such as arthritis, osteoporosis or trauma.
<boldin>• Lordosis<boldout>
  • Refers to the normal inward lordotic curvature of the lumbar and cervical regions of spine
  • Excessive curvature of the lower back is known as lumbar hyperlordosis, commonly called saddle back
<boldin>• Scoliosis<boldout>
  • A spinal curvature of more than 10 degrees to the right or left as the examiner faces the child, i.e. the coronal plane.
  • Spinal x-ray helps to decide the degree
  • Causes can be idiopathic, congenital (cerebral palsy, spinal bifida), secondary to neuromuscular disease (spinal muscular atrophy, physical trauma, spinal bifida, cerebral palsy) or caused by the environment (limb length inequality, tumors).
<boldin>• Kypho-scoliosis<boldout>
  • Describes an abnormal curvature of the spine in both a coronal and sagittal plane
  • It is a combination of kyphosis and scoliosis

• <boldin>Cyanosis<boldout>
  • It is bluish discoloration of the skin mainly on digits of the feet and hand, tongue and mucus membrane resulting from an increased quantity of deoxygenated hemoglobin
  • Becomes evident when the absolute concentration of deoxygenated hemoglobin is ≥ 5gm/dl of capillary blood
  • Cyanosis is classified as Central cyanosis and peripheral cyanosis.
  • Peripheral cyanosis (acrocyanosis) is bluish discoloration of the distal parts of extremities due to vasoconstriction
  • Central cyanosis is bluish discoloration of the circum-oral area (lips ,tongue and buccal mucosa ) due to hypoxia and it is always pathologic

• <boldin>Clubbing<boldout>
  • Is selective bulbous enlargement of the distal segments of fingers and toes due to proliferation of connective tissue
<boldin>• Grading:<boldout>
  • Grade I 	– Spongy feeling on pressing the nail bed
  • Grade II 	– Obliteration of the hyponychial angle
  • Grade III 	– Drum stick appearance of the fingers
  • Grade IV	– Drum stick appearance with hypertrophic osteoarthropathy

<boldin>Differential diagnosis of clubbing<boldout>

<boldin>PALPATION<boldout>
<boldin>Approach = (3T + C/S)<boldout>
<boldin>• Tracheal position/ Tracheal tug<boldout>
<boldin>• Tenderness of chest wall<boldout>
<boldin>• Subcutaneous emphysema<boldout>
<boldin>• Tactile fremitus<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest expansion<boldout>
<boldin>Technique<boldout>
<boldin>• Tracheal position<boldout>
  • Feel for the trachea by putting the second and fourth fingers on each edge of sternal notch and use the third finger to assess the trachea is central or deviated to one side
  • A slight deviation of the trachea to the right side may be found in healthy individuals
<boldin>• Causes of tracheal displacement:<boldout>
  • Tracheal deviation away from side of the lung lesion
<boldin>• Unilateral massive pleural effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Unilateral pneumothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Mediastinal mass<boldout>
  • Tracheal deviation towards the side of the lung lesion
<boldin>• Upper lobe collapse<boldout>
<boldin>• Upper lobe fibrosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Pneumonectomy<boldout>
  • Chest wall tenderness - Look at his face while palpating
<boldin>• Check for costochondral beading<boldout>
<boldin>• Subcutaneous emphysema- Crackling sensation<boldout>
<boldin>• Tactile fremitus (TF)<boldout>
  • For cooperative patients (age <boldin>≥ <boldout>6 year) or while crying for non-cooperative child
  • Ask the patient to say “ninety-nine” or “arba-arat” repeatedly while the palm of hand over the chest wall
<boldin>• Compare bilaterally in symmetrical fashion<boldout>
  • Reduced TF on affected lung occurs in
<boldin>• Pneumothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Pleural effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Fibrotic lung disease<boldout>
  • Increased TF on affected lung occurs in lung consolidation
<boldin>• Chest expansion<boldout>
  • For cooperative patients (age ≥ 6 year) a measuring tape is put around the mid-thorax perpendicular to vertebrae and patient is asked to breath- in maximally and the difference between full inspiration and expiration is recorded, normally it is two cm
<boldin>• Posterior chest<boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout><italicin>(to check symmetry)<italicout><boldout>
  • Place your thumbs at the level of and parallel to the tenth rib, your hands grasping the lateral ribcage
  • Slide your thumbs medially in order to raise loose skin folds between your thumbs and the spine
<boldin>• Ask the patient to inhale deeply<boldout>
  • Watch divergence of your thumbs during inhalation
  • Observe for symmetry and degree of chest expansion
<boldin>• Anterior chest<boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout><italicin>(to check symmetry)<italicout><boldout>
  • Place your thumbs along each costal margin, your hands holding the lateral rib cage
  • Slide your thumbs medially to raise skin folds
  • Reduced chest wall movement on affected side
<boldin>• Consolidation<boldout>
<boldin>• Lung collapse<boldout>
<boldin>• Atelectasis<boldout>
<boldin>• Pleural effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Pneumothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Localized pulmonary fibrosis, …<boldout>
<boldin>• Symmetrically reduced chest expansion<boldout>
<boldin>• Bilateral pleural effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Bilateral lung collapse<boldout>
<boldin>• Emphysema<boldout>
<boldin>• Bilateral fibrotic lung disease, …<boldout>
<boldin>PERCUSSION<boldout>
  • Percussion of the chest sets the chest wall /& underlying tissues into motion, producing audible sounds /& palpable vibration
  • It helps to determine whether the underlying tissues are air-filled, fluid-filled or solid-filled

<boldin>Approach:<boldout>
<boldin>• Percussion notes<boldout>
<boldin>• Diaphragmatic excursion<boldout>
<boldin>• Percussion notes<boldout>
<boldin>• Resonance             – Normal<boldout>
  • Relative dullness    – Fluid filled alveoli or consolidation
  • Stony dullness        – Fluid in the pleural space
  • Hyper resonance     – Air in the pleural space
<boldin>• Diaphragmatic excursion<boldout>
  • For cooperative patients (age ≥ 6 year)
  • The distance between the level of dullness on full expiration and full inspiration
  • Normal range of diaphragmatic excursion is 4-6 cm but it is age dependent

<boldin>Technique<boldout>
  • Percuss posterior chest from apices to lung base
  • Determine the level of dullness at full expiration and then at full inspiration
<boldin>• Determine the difference in centimeter<boldout>
<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout>
Reduced diaphragmatic excursion on the affected side of the lung
<boldin>• Pleural effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary edema<boldout>
<boldin>• Consolidation<boldout>
<boldin>• Lung collapse<boldout>
<boldin>• Atelectatic<boldout>
<boldin>• Fibrosis of the lung<boldout>
<boldin>Reduced diaphragmatic excursion bilaterally<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperinflation<boldout>
<boldin>• Bilateral pleural effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Bilateral lung collapse …<boldout>
<boldin>AUSCULTATION<boldout>
<boldin>Approach:<boldout>
<boldin>• Breath sounds<boldout>
<boldin>• Adventitious sounds<boldout>
<boldin>• Pleural friction rub<boldout>
<boldin>• Transmitted sounds<boldout>
• <boldin>Breath<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>sounds<boldout>
Normal breath sounds are classified according to their intensity, pitch and duration of their inspiratory and expiratory phases
<img:t4.png>
• <boldin>Adventitious<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>sounds<boldout>
<boldin>Crepitation<boldout>
  • Produced by sudden changes in gas pressure related to the sudden opening of previously closed small airways (i.e. due to the presence of fluid in the alveoli)
<boldin>• Heard during inspiration<boldout>
<boldin>• Intermittent /& non-musical brief, high-pitched sounds<boldout>
<boldin>• Cannot be cleared by coughing<boldout>
  • Can be coarse or fine /& unilateral or bilateral
<boldin>Rhonchi<boldout>
<boldin>• Low-pitched sounds with snoring quality<boldout>
  • Due to partially obstructed bronchi by inflamed mucosa or secretion
<boldin>• Disappear while coughing<boldout>
<boldin>Wheeze<boldout>
  • High pitched sounds with hissing or shrill quality
  • Usually maximal during expiration and is accompanied by prolonged expiration
  • Can be diffused, scattered or localized and unilateral or bilateral
<boldin>Stridor<boldout>
  • A noisy breathing due to turbulent air flow
  • Cardinal sign of URT obstruction in children
<boldin>• Can be:<boldout>
<boldin>• Inspiratory<boldout>
<boldin>• At or above the vocal cords<boldout>
  • Due to the collapse of the soft tissue with negative pressure during inspiration
<boldin>• Expiratory<boldout>
<boldin>• Due decreased airway caliber with expiration<boldout>
<boldin>• Emanates from intra-thoracic trachea and bronchi<boldout>
<boldin>• Biphasic<boldout>
  • Indicates unchanging airway caliber due to a fixed lesion
<boldin>• Characteristics of mid-tracheal lesions<boldout>
• <boldin>Pleural friction rub<boldout>
  • Occur when the pleural layers are inflamed and lost their lubrication with creaking or rubbing quality
<boldin>• Appears during inspiration /& expiration movement<boldout>
  • Can be described as a sound you heard through your stethoscope while you rubbing the skin by the stethoscope diaphragm or a sound made by treading on fresh snow
  • Common in patients with pneumonia, pulmonary embolism and pleurisy

• <boldin>Transmitted<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>sounds<boldout>
<boldin>• Common in consolidation<boldout>
<boldin>• Done in cooperative patients<boldout>
  • Look for: Bronchophony, Aegophony and Whispering pectoriloquy
<boldin><italicin>Bronchophony<italicout><boldout>
  • Ask the patient to say repeatedly “ninety-nine”; louder and clearer sounds are heard on the chest wall
<boldin>• Normally muffled and indistinct<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Aegophony<italicout><boldout>
  • Ask the patient to say “ee-ee-ee”; E-to-A change with nasal or bleating quality is heard
  • Normally hear a muffled long “E” sound
<boldin><italicin>Whispering pectoriloquy<italicout><boldout>
  • Ask the patient to whisper “one-two-three”; louder and clearer whispered sounds are heard
<boldin>• Normally heard faintly and indistinctly<boldout>

<boldin>SOME COMMON LUNG PATHOLOGYS<boldout>
<boldin>CONSOLIDATION<boldout>
<boldin>Signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Reduced chest expansion<boldout>
<boldin>• Increased tactile fremitus<boldout>
<boldin>• Relative dullness<boldout>
<boldin>• Bronchial breath sounds<boldout>
<boldin>• Crepitation<boldout>
<boldin>• Transmitted sounds are positive<boldout>
<boldin>Causes<boldout>
<boldin>• Pneumonia (commonest cause)<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary edema or hemorrhage<boldout>
<boldin>• Aspiration<boldout>
<boldin>Investigation<boldout>
<boldin>• CBC – normal or leukocytosis<boldout>
<boldin>• CXR<boldout>
<boldin>• Air bronchogram<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyper dense (homogeneous opacity)<boldout>
<boldin>• Silhouette sign – if lobar pneumonia<boldout>
<boldin>• CT scan<boldout>
<boldin>PLEURAL EFFUSION<boldout>
<boldin>Signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachypnea<boldout>
  • Trachea - deviated away from a massive effusion
<boldin>• Reduced chest expansion<boldout>
<boldin>• Stony dullness<boldout>
<boldin>• Absent air entry<boldout>
<boldin>• May be crepitation above the effusion<boldout>
<boldin>Pleural effusion<boldout>
  • Collection of fluid in the pleural space
<boldin>• Simple fluid = Hydrothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Blood = Hemothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Lymph = Chylothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Pus = Empyema<boldout>
<boldin>• Can be Exudative or Transudative<boldout>
  • Exudatives are likely be due to inflammation, malignant or traumatic processes
  • To differentiate the two, we use “Lights criteria”
  • Exudative – If full fill one of the following Lights criteria
<boldin>• Pleural protein-to-serum protein ratio /> 0.5<boldout>
  • Pleural LDH-to-serum LDH ratio /> 0.6 or
  • Pleural LDH is above 2/3 of upper normal serum level
  • Transudative – if not full fill any of the above parameters
<img:t5.png>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout>
<italicin>Why pleural effusion is more common in the right pleural space?<italicout>
<italicin>List differential diagnosis for unilateral vs bilateral crepitation<italicout>
<boldin>Bilateral pleural effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Congestive heart disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Nephrotic syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary infarction<boldout>
<boldin>• Lupus<boldout>
<boldin>• Rheumatoid Arthritis<boldout>
<boldin>• Malignancy<boldout>
<boldin>• Tuberculosis<boldout>

<boldin>• Complete blood count<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest x-ray (CXR)<boldout>
<boldin>• Expected to be seen<boldout>
<boldin>• Blunted/ obliterated costophrenic angle<boldout>
<boldin>• Radio-opaque density extending from the base<boldout>
  • Meniscus sign at upper surface of the fluid
<boldin>• Mediastinal shift away from the effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Pleural fluid analysis<boldout>
<boldin>• Appearance<boldout>
<boldin>• Clear or straw: Transudative effusion, TB…<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemorrhagic<boldout>
<boldin>• Trauma<boldout>
<boldin>• Malignancy<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary infarction<boldout>
<boldin>• Tuberculosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Spontaneous pneumothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Chylous<boldout>
<boldin>• Malignancy<boldout>
<boldin>• Trauma to lymphatic vessels<boldout>
<boldin>• Tuberculosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Thrombosis of the left subclavian vein<boldout>
<boldin>• Cloudy – bacterial infection, tuberculosis…<boldout>
<boldin>• Turbidity, Cell count<boldout>
<boldin>• LDH level, Protein level, Glucose level<boldout>
<boldin>• Gram stain, AFB<boldout>
<boldin>• Culture<boldout>
<boldin>• Cytology study<boldout>
<boldin>• Blood culture<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest ultrasound<boldout>
<boldin>• Aids in identification of loculated effusion<boldout>
  • Aids in differentiation of fluid from fibrosis
<boldin>• Aids in identification of thoracentesis site<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest CT scan<boldout>
<boldin>• Aids in differentiation of<boldout>
<boldin>• Consolidation Vs effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Cystic vs solid lesions<boldout>
<boldin>• Peripheral lung abscess Vs loculated empyema<boldout>
<boldin>• Aids in identification of<boldout>
<boldin>• Necrotic areas<boldout>
<boldin>• Pleural thickening, nodules, masses<boldout>
<boldin>• Extent of tumour<boldout>
<boldin>• Pleural biopsy and cytology<boldout>
<boldin>• Closed pleural biopsy<boldout>
<boldin>• CT guided cutting needle biopsy<boldout>
<boldin>• Video assisted thoracoscopic (VATS) pleural biopsy<boldout>
<boldin>Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>• Management differs according to the cause<boldout>
<boldin>• Therapeutic tap with indication<boldout>
  • Chest tube insertion <italicin>(see at common pediatrics procedures)<italicout>
<img:t6.png>
<boldin>PNEUMOTHORAX<boldout>
  • Accumulation of air in the pleural cavity due to leakage of air from the lung or chest wall punctures into the pleural space
<boldin>Signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachypnea<boldout>
<boldin>• Trachea deviates away from<boldout>
<boldin>• Subcutaneous crepitus<boldout>
<boldin>• Reduced chest expansion<boldout>
<boldin>• Reduced tactile fremitus<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperresonance<boldout>
<boldin>• Greatly reduced or absent air entry<boldout>
<boldin>• Distended neck vein<boldout>
<boldin>Types of pneumothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Spontaneous pneumothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Can be primary or secondary<boldout>
  • Visceral pleural ruptures without an external traumatic or iatrogenic cause
<boldin>• Open pneumothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Also known as “sucking chest wound”<boldout>
  • Defects in the chest wall large enough to exceed the laryngeal cross- sectional area allows air to enter from the exterior into the pleural cavity and results in lung collapse
<boldin>• Tension pneumothorax<boldout>
  • A clinical diagnosis defined as “any pneumothorax with cardiorespiratory compromise or collapse”
  • Air continues to get into the pleural space but cannot exit = the operation of one-way valve system
<boldin>• Subcutaneous emphysema on the affected side<boldout>
  • Distended neck veins due impedance of venous return but it can be flat because of concurrent systemic hypovolemia
<boldin>• Usually patients are hypotensive<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
<boldin>• CXR - expected to be seen<boldout>
  • Hypo dense (extremely dark) on the affected side
<boldin>• Visceral pleural edge is visible<boldout>
<boldin>• Absent bronchovascular markings peripherally<boldout>
  • Loss of lung volume on the affected side
<boldin>• Mediastinal shift to opposite side<boldout>
<boldin>• Tracheal shift to opposite side<boldout>
<boldin>• Transillumination test<boldout>
  • For infants age less than 6 month
<boldin>• It uses a fiberoptic light probe<boldout>
  • Free air in the pleural space often results in an unusually large halo (≥ 2cm from the center) of light in the skin surrounding the probe
  • Comparison with the contralateral chest is often very helpful in interpreting findings
  • This test is unreliable in older patients and patients with subcutaneous emphysema or atelectasis
<boldin>• Chest CT scan<boldout>
<boldin>Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>Open pneumothorax<boldout>
  • Occlusive dressing that is tapped on three sides
  • Closure of the defect and tube thoracostomy done remote from the wound
<boldin>Tension pneumothorax<boldout>
  • Large bore needle thoracostomy at second intercostal space over the mid clavicular line
  • Tube thoracostomy at fourth or fifth intercostal space over the mid axillary line

<boldin>LUNG COLLAPSE<boldout>
  • Partial or complete loss of lung volume
<boldin>Signs<boldout>
  • Trachea - deviates towards the collapsed lung
<boldin>• Reduced chest expansion<boldout>
<boldin>• Relative dullness<boldout>
<boldin>• Decreased air entry / absent<boldout>
  • Signs of hyperinflation on the opposite side
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest X-ray; Depends on:<boldout>
<boldin>• Mechanism of collapse<boldout>
<boldin>• Degree of collapse<boldout>
<boldin>• Presence or absence of consolidation and<boldout>
<boldin>• Preexisting state of the pleura<boldout>
  • Signs of collapse can be direct or indirect. Indirect signs are as a result of compensatory changes in response to volume loss.
<boldin>• Direct signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Displacement of interlobular fissure<boldout>
<boldin>• Loss of aeration of the lung<boldout>
<boldin>• Vascular and bronchial overcrowding<boldout>
<boldin>• Indirect signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Elevation of the hemidiaphragm<boldout>
<boldin>• Mediastinal displacement<boldout>
<boldin>• Hilar displacement<boldout>
<boldin>• Compensatory hyperinflation<boldout>
  • Chest CT scan – <boldin>(<boldout><boldin><italicin>see at p.<italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin>effusion)<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>Differential diagnosis for lung collapse<boldout>
<boldin>• Cystic fibrosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Foreign body<boldout>
<boldin>• Hilar lymphadenopathy<boldout>
<boldin>• Bronchogenic carcinoma<boldout>
<boldin>WHEEZE<boldout>
<boldin>Wheezing can be polyphonic or monophonic<boldout>
  • Polyphonic: When there is wide spread narrowing of the airways, causing various pitches or levels of obstruction to air flow as seen in asthma or pediatric reactive airway disease (RAD)
  • Monophonic: Wheezing referring to a single pitch sound that is produced in the larger airways during expiration, as in foreign body aspiration, distal tracheomalacia or bronchomalacia

<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Bronchial asthma/Reactive airway disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Clinical features<boldout>
<boldin>• Diffused, bilateral wheeze<boldout>
<boldin>• Afebrile unless the precipitant is infection/pneumonia<boldout>
<boldin>• Recurrent symptoms<boldout>
<boldin>• Response to bronchodilators<boldout>
  • May have family history (Assess for Asthma Predictive Index)
  • Global bronchoconstriction and likely of polyphonic in nature
  • Types of wheezers (Tucson Respiratory children’s study)
  • Transient wheezer → Wheeze at 3 year of age but no after 6 year of age
  • Persistent wheezer → Wheeze at /< 3 year of age and at 6 year of age
  • Late onset wheezer → No wheeze at /< 3 year of age but wheeze at 6 year
  • Never -→ No wheeze by the first 6 years of age
<boldin>• Foreign body<boldout>
  • Chocking episode history (Sudden onset cough + vomiting ± cyanosis)
  • Localized, most commonly unilateral but could be bilateral wheeze
<boldin>• Afebrile ( depends on the phase)<boldout>
  • Peak age is 7 months - 3 years
<boldin>• Congenital anomalies<boldout>
  • Malecia of the larynx, trachea and/or bronchi
  • Symptoms start usually by two to four weeks of birth
<boldin>• Relief of symptoms in prone position<boldout>
<boldin>• Tracheoesophageal fistula (H – type)<boldout>
<boldin>• Recurrent pneumonia<boldout>
<boldin>• Risk factors during pregnancy like polyhydramnios<boldout>
<boldin>• Vascular ring<boldout>
<boldin>• Symptoms since birth<boldout>
<boldin>• Noisy breathing<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachypnea<boldout>
<boldin>• Infections<boldout>
<boldin>• Bronchiolitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Diffused or scattered, bilateral wheeze<boldout>
<boldin>• Febrile (usually ≤ 38.2°c)<boldout>
<boldin>• First episode of wheeze during infancy<boldout>
<boldin>• Poor /no response to bronchodilators<boldout>
<boldin>• Pneumonia<boldout>
<boldin>• Sign of consolidation<boldout>
<boldin>• Scattered, unilateral wheeze<boldout>
<boldin>• Coarse crepitation<boldout>
<boldin>• Endobronchial tuberculosis<boldout>
  • Contact history to a known tuberculosis patient
<boldin>• Hilary adenopathy on chest x-ray<boldout>
<boldin>• Miscellaneous<boldout>
<boldin>• Congested heart failure (Pulmonary edema)<boldout>
<boldin>• Bilateral or unilateral wheeze<boldout>
<boldin>• Signs of congested heart failure<boldout>
<boldin>• Auscultatory cardiac findings<boldout>
<boldin>• Bilateral postero-basal rales<boldout>
<boldin>• Gastro-esophageal reflux disease (GERD)<boldout>
<boldin>• Vomiting since early infancy<boldout>
<boldin>• Failure to thrive<boldout>
<boldin>• Mediastinal mass/tumor<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest X-ray<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperinflation (asthma)<boldout>
<boldin>• Foreign body<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Mediastinal widening<boldout>
<boldin>• Consolidation / pulmonary edema<boldout>
<boldin>• CBC with differentials<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary function test<boldout>
<boldin>• PPD test – tuberculosis screening test<boldout>
<boldin>• Esophagogram - Vascular ring, GERD<boldout>
<boldin>STRIDOR<boldout>
<img:t7.png>
<img:t8.png>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
<boldin>• CBC with differentials<boldout>
<boldin>• Lateral neck x-ray<boldout>
  • Neck x-ray (anterio-posterior view as well as lateral)
<boldin>• Chest x-ray<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout>
<italicin>For febrile stridor always the patient should be stabilized or started on management before the imaging (x-ray) even always may not be mandatory.<italicout>

topics.title: Chapter 6: Cardiovascular System Examination
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner:<boldout>
  • Do cardiovascular system examination: General exam + arterial exam + venous exam + precordial exam
<boldin>• Do precordial examination (i.e. Precordium only)<boldout>
  • What is the conclusion of your findings?
<boldin>• Differential diagnosis for your findings<boldout>
<boldin>• Evidences for your differential diagnoses<boldout>
<boldin>• Investigations for your differentials<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>• Asses signs of congested heart failure<boldout>
<boldin>Cardiovascular System Examination<boldout>
<boldin>Approach<boldout>
<boldin>• General examination<boldout>
<boldin>• Arterial examination<boldout>
<boldin>• Venous examination<boldout>
<boldin>• Precordial examination<boldout>
<boldin>General Examination<boldout>
<boldin>• Cyanosis, Signs of distress, Edema, Clubbing…<boldout>
<boldin>Arterial Examination<boldout>
  • Feel for all peripheral arteries (Carotid, Brachial Radial, Femoral, Popliteal, Posterior tibial and Dorsalispedis arteries) bilaterally at the same time except carotid artery. If asymmetric, think of ↔ Arteritis, arterial obstruction…
  • Evaluate for: Rate, Rhythm, Character, Volume, Radio-femoral delay and Pulse deficit

<boldin>Rate and<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>rhythm<boldout>
<boldin>• Radial artery is commonly used<boldout>
  • Rate – count pulse rate for full one minute
<boldin>• Rhythm<boldout>
<boldin>• Regular<boldout>
<boldin>• Regularly irregular – ectopic beats<boldout>
<boldin>• Irregularly irregular – atrial fibrillation<boldout>
<boldin>Pulse character <boldout>(amplitude /& counter)
  • Is best assessed in the carotid artery, except in collapsing pulse where radial artery is preferred, but the carotid artery should be palpated one side at a time.
  • Collapsing pulse is a high volume pulse with abrupt collapse. When you palpating it, it hammers your finger tips and abruptly collapsing. Occurs in patients with aortic insufficiency and PDA

<boldin>Pulse<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>volume<boldout>
<boldin>• Provides crude indications of stroke volume<boldout>
<boldin>• Small in systolic heart failure<boldout>
  • Large in hyper kinetic heart disease (bounding pulse) like: Aortic regurgitation (AR), Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), Anemia, Pregnancy, Thyrotoxicosis and Fever.

<boldin>Pulse<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>deficit<boldout>
  • Difference of heart beat rate and peripheral arterial rate
  • When the difference is ≥10 beats per minute
  • Often occurs in atrial fibrillation due to failure in conducting all central beats to peripheral arteries


<boldin>Radio – femoral<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>delay<boldout>
  • Press both radial and femoral artery at the same time
  • Notice for arterial pulse delay at femoral artery as compared to radial artery
  • Usually observed in coarctation of the aorta /interrupted aortic arch
  • Normally, femoral pulse is slightly faster than the radial pulse because of femoral artery is a direct branch of abdomen aorta
  • Pulse volume/blood pressure assessment is more reliable than radio-femoral delay in infants and non-older child as collaterals are not yet developed

<boldin><italicin>Note <italicout><boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Based on pulse strength, pulse is graded in to five:<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Grade o   = Absent<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>pulse<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Grade 1+ = Decreased pulse <italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Grade 2+ = Normal pulse <italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Grade 3+ = Full pulse <italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Grade 4+ = Bounding pulse <italicout><boldout>
<italicin>Bounding pulse is a full, exaggerated arterial pulsation due vasodilation and increased cardiac out put <italicout>

<boldin>Venous Examination <boldout>
<boldin>Distended<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>neck<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>vein<boldout>
  • When a jugular vein is engorged and pulsatile the most likely cause is raised right atrial pressure
  • If the jugular vein is engorged and non-pulsatile the most likely cause is venous obstruction like in case of superior vena-caval syndrome (SVS)/ superior mediastinal syndrome (SMS) in lymphoma

<boldin>Jugular Venous pressure<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>(<boldout><boldin>JVP<boldout><boldin>)<boldout>
  • The normal upper limit is 3-4cm vertically above sternal angle. This is about 8-9 cm above right atrium which corresponding to a JVP of 8-9 mmHg.
  • JVP />3-4cm vertically above sternal angle is considered elevated
  • If the internal jugular vein pulsation is not visible, measure the vertical distance of the point above which the external jugular veins appear to be collapsed from the sternal angle
  • It is difficult to measure JVP for infants and young children (≤2 years) due to the short neck they have

<boldin>Kussmaul‘s<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>sign<boldout>
  • Increase rather than the normal decrease in the JVP during inspiration, which is observed in: Constrictive pericarditis, Right ventricular infarction and Severe right ventricular failure.

<boldin>Hepato-jugular reflex test<boldout>
  • Positive if there is; An increase in JVP during firm compression below the right subcostal margin for ten seconds followed by a rapid drop in JVP on release of the compression
<img:t9.png>
<boldin>PRECORDIAL EXAMINATION<boldout>
<boldin>Approach<boldout>
<boldin>• Inspection<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpation<boldout>
  • Percussion: It has little significance. Do it when you suspect dextro-cardia, pericardial effusion or huge cardiomegaly
<boldin>• Auscultation<boldout>
<boldin>INSPECTION<boldout>
<boldin>Look for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Bulging<boldout>
<boldin>• Precordial activity<boldout>
<boldin>• Apical impulse<boldout>
<boldin>• Surgical scar<boldout>
<boldin>Bulged Precordium<boldout>
<boldin>• Indicates chronicity or cardiomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>Precordial Activity<boldout>
  • Active precordium: when only one pulsation is visible
<boldin>• Hyperactive precordium<boldout>
  • More than one visible pulsations or a pulse involving /> 2.5 cm
<boldin>• Shows hyper dynamicity<boldout>
<boldin>• Signs of volume /pressure overload<boldout>
<boldin>• Quiet precordium / invisible apical impulse<boldout>
<boldin>• No visible pulsation<boldout>
<boldin>• Differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Thick chest wall<boldout>
  • Apical impulse exactly at the rib i.e. overlap with a rib
<boldin>• Massive pericardial effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Dextrocardia/ dextroposition<boldout>
<boldin>• Dilated cardiomyopathy with no heart failure<boldout>
<boldin>Apical Impulse<boldout>
  • Is the apical impulse visible or not?
  • If visible - determine the location in inter costal space in relation to left mid-clavicular line
  • If not visible– thick chest wall, pericardial effusion…
  • The apex beat is defined as the lowest and most lateral point at which the cardiac impulse can be palpated
  • Apical impulse is due to the recoil of heart as blood is ejected reflecting the left ventricle
  • The normal left ventricular impulse is located at or medial to the left mid-clavicular line at the fourth or fifth inter costal space
  • Down ward and laterally displaced apical impulse suggests left ventricular enlargement
  • Volume overload – displaced lateral /& downward
<boldin>• Pressure overload – displaced down ward<boldout>
<boldin>• Right-sided apical impulses signify:<boldout>
<boldin>• Dextrocardia<boldout>
  • Dextroposition like in case of tension pneumothorax
  • Left sided thoracic space occupying lesions (e.g., diaphragmatic hernia)

<boldin>Surgical scar<boldout>
  • May tell us previous cardiothoracic surgeries or procedures like: valviectomy, pericardieoctomy ….

<boldin>PALPATION<boldout>
<boldin>Look for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpable heart sound<boldout>
<boldin>• Point of maximal impulse<boldout>
<boldin>• Thrill<boldout>
<boldin>• Heave<boldout>
<boldin>Palpable heart sound<boldout>
<boldin>Feel for palpable heart sounds at<boldout>
<boldin>• Apex<boldout>
<boldin>• Left upper and lower sternal border<boldout>
<boldin>• Right upper sternal border<boldout>
<boldin>Point of maximal impulse/intensity (PMI)<boldout>
<boldin>Look for<boldout>
<boldin>• Location, amplitude, duration<boldout>
<boldin>• Localized or diffused; Diffuse PMI is when:<boldout>
<boldin>• Occupies /> 2.5 cm in diameter<boldout>
  • Palpable between second and fourth finger tips while off the third finger tip from the precordial area or
<boldin>• Occupies more than one intercostal space<boldout>
  • Sustained or non-sustained; Sustained PMI is when it occupies more than two-third of cardiac cycle
<boldin>• Thrusting or tapping<boldout>
<boldin>Thrill<boldout>
<boldin>• It is palpable heart murmurs<boldout>
  • Palpable, low frequency, vibrations associated with heart murmurs
  • Thrill is the finding of at least grade 4 and above systolic murmurs
  • The presence of thrill is a sign of no more innocent murmur
<boldin>• Look for<boldout>
<boldin>• Location (site)<boldout>
<boldin>• Timing of the thrill<boldout>
<boldin>• Location (site)<boldout>
  • Palpate the apex, left upper /& lower sternal border and right upper sternal border with palm of examining hand, and feel for thrill as purring of a cat
  • Timing of the thrill – Systolic or diastolic
  • If the thrill coincides with the carotid pulse – Systolic thrill
  • If the thrill comes after the carotid pulse – Diastolic thrill
<boldin>Heave (lift)<boldout>
<boldin>• Can be apical or parasternal heave<boldout>
  • Put ulnar border of the hand over apical or left sternal area and look for lift of your hand
  • Left parasternal lift suggests, right ventricular hypertrophy or severe TR with giant right atrium
  • Apical heave suggests, left ventricular hypertrophy or severe MR

<boldin>AUSCULTATION<boldout>
<boldin>Look for<boldout>
<boldin>• Heart sounds: S1 /& S2<boldout>
  • Added heart sounds: S3 /& S4 (gallop)
<boldin>• Friction rub<boldout>
<boldin>• Murmur (innocent vs pathologic)<boldout>
<boldin>Heart Sounds: <boldout><boldin><italicin>S1 (Lub) and S2 (Dub)<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>First heart sound (S1)<boldout>
  • Corresponds to mitral and tricuspid valve closure at the onset of systole
  • It is maximally heard at apical area
<boldin>Second heart sound (S2)<boldout>
  • Corresponds to aortic and pulmonary valve closure following ventricular ejection
  • It is maximally heard at pulmonic area
  • The louder the sound as you go down is S1 and the louder the sound as you go up is S2
  • Increased intensity of S2 at pulmonic area occurs in pulmonary hypertension

<boldin>Added Heart Sounds: <boldout><boldin><italicin>S3 and S4<italicout><boldout>
Use the bell of stethoscope to listen third and fourth heart sounds
<boldin>Third heart sound (S3)<boldout>
<boldin>• A low frequency sound<boldout>
<boldin>• Best heard at the apex<boldout>
  • Occurs due to rapid ventricular filling in mid diastole, causing sudden stretching of chordae tendinae and papillary muscles
  • Physiologic S3 occurs in <italicin>adolescents and<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>pregnancy<italicout>
<boldin>• Pathologic S3 often noticed in patients with:<boldout>
<boldin>• Anemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Thyrotoxicosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Severe MR<boldout>
<boldin>• CHF – fluid overload<boldout>
<boldin>Fourth heart sound (S4)<boldout>
<boldin>• A low frequency sound<boldout>
  • Occurs due to vigorous atrial contraction against a non-compliant ventricle in late diastole
  • May be heard just before the S1 in late diastole
  • S4 is usually pathological and occurs in stiff non-compliant hypertrophied ventricle due to;
<boldin>• Hypertensive heart disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Aortic stenosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy<boldout>
  • In a patient with the clinical signs of heart failure and tachycardia, S3 may be heard as a gallop rhythm and may merge with an S4, a finding known as a <italicin>summation gallop<italicout>

<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout><italicin>:<italicout><italicin> Opening of any normal valve is not audible<italicout>
<boldin>Pericardial Friction Rub<boldout>
  • High-pitched scratching sound audible at any part of cardiac cycle
  • Heard best at left lower sternal border
<boldin>• The intensity increases during leaning forward<boldout>
<boldin>• Has no relation with respiratory cycle<boldout>
<boldin>• Observed in acute pericarditis<boldout>
<boldin>HEART MURMURS<boldout>
  • Result from vibrations set up in the blood stream and the surrounding heart and great vessels as a result of turbulent blood flow

<boldin>Characterizing a murmur<boldout>
<boldin>• Location of maximal intensity<boldout>
<boldin>• Timing<boldout>
<boldin>• Intensity/grading of the murmur<boldout>
<boldin>• Shape (configuration)<boldout>
<boldin>• Pitch<boldout>
<boldin>• Quality<boldout>
<boldin>• Radiation<boldout>
<boldin>• Maneuvers<boldout>
<boldin>Location of maximal intensity<boldout>
<boldin>• Signifies origin of murmur<boldout>
<boldin>Timing<boldout>
  • Identify whether the murmur is <boldin><italicin>systolic, diastolic or continuous <italicout><boldout>in comparison to carotid pulse
  • Systolic murmurs coincide with carotid pulse, while the diastolic is not

• <boldin>Systolic<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>murmurs<boldout>
  • Mid-systolic murmur (MSM) or ejection systolic murmur
  • Begin after S1 and stops before S2
<boldin>• Murmur of AS, PS<boldout>
<boldin>• Pansystolic murmur (PSM) or holosystolic murmur<boldout>
  • Starts with S1 and stops at S2
<boldin>• Murmur of MR, VSD, TR<boldout>
<boldin>• Late systolic murmur (LSM)<boldout>
  • Starts in mid or late systole and persists up to S2
<boldin>• Murmur of Mitral valve prolapse (<italicin>MVP<italicout>)<boldout>
• <boldin>Diastolic<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>murmur<boldout>
<boldin>• Early diastolic murmur (EDM)<boldout>
  • Starts after S2 and fades into silence before next S1
<boldin>• Murmur of AR, PR<boldout>
<boldin>• Mid diastolic murmur (MDM)<boldout>
  • Starts after S2 and fade away or merge into a late diastolic murmur
<boldin>• Murmur of MS, TS, ASD<boldout>
<boldin>• Late diastolic (presystolic) murmur (LDM)<boldout>
  • Starts late in diastole and continuous up to S1
  • Murmur of <italicin>MS or TS<italicout><boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout>in sinus rhythm
• <boldin>Continuous murmur<boldout>
  • Begin in systole, peak at S2, and continue into all or part of diastole
  • Murmur of <italicin>PDA<italicout><boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout>(patent ductus arteriosus), aortopulmonary window defect

<boldin>Grading of intensity<boldout>
<boldin>• Systolic murmurs (Six grades)<boldout>
Grade I	- Faint murmur, heard only with special efforts
Grade II	- Quiet but heard murmur
Grade III	- Moderately loud murmur but no thrill
Grade IV	- Loud murmur accompanied by a thrill
Grade V	- Very loud, heard with a stethoscope partly off
Grade VI	- Heard with the stethoscope entirely off the chest
  • In patients who have undergone prior heart surgery, murmur of grade IV or greater may be heard in the absence of a thrill
<boldin>• Diastolic murmurs have only four grades.<boldout>
<boldin>Shape (configuration)<boldout>
<boldin>• Crescendo<boldout>
<boldin>• Murmur grows louder<boldout>
<boldin>• Pre-systolic murmur of MS<boldout>
<boldin>• Crescendo-decrescendo<boldout>
  • Murmur that grows louder and then fall
<boldin>• Mid-systolic murmur of AS<boldout>
<boldin>• Decrescendo<boldout>
<boldin>• Murmur grows softer and slowly falls<boldout>
<boldin>• Early diastolic murmur of AR<boldout>
<boldin>• Plateau<boldout>
<boldin>• Murmur has same intensity throughout<boldout>
<boldin>• Pansystolic murmur of MR<boldout>
<boldin>Pitch: <boldout>High, Medium or Low pitched sound
<boldin>Quality <boldout>
<boldin>• Blowing<boldout>
<boldin>• Harsh<boldout>
<boldin>• Rumbling<boldout>
<boldin>• Musical<boldout>
<boldin>Radiation<boldout>: Signifies direction of blood flow
<boldin>Maneuvers<boldout>
<boldin>• Respiration (RILE)<boldout>
  • <boldin><italicin>Right <italicout><boldout>- side murmurs increase with <boldin><italicin>inspiration<italicout><boldout>
  • <boldin><italicin>Left<italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout>- side murmurs increase with <boldin><italicin>expiration<italicout><boldout>
  • Leaning forward: Increases the intensity of AR
  • Leaning to left lateral position: Increases the intensity of MR
  • Hand grip exercise: Increases the intensity of MR and AR
<boldin>Note<boldout><boldin>: Innocent Murmurs<boldout>
<boldin>• Are functional, normal or insignificant murmurs<boldout>
  • May be heard in up to 30% of patients at some time during childhood
  • Usually accentuated by fever, infection, anxiety and first noticed during a visit for an intercurrent illness
<boldin>• No thrill in innocent murmurs<boldout>
  • The most common innocent<boldin> <boldout>murmur<boldin> <boldout>is a medium-pitched, vibratory or “musical,” relatively short systolic ejection murmur
  • Best heard along the left lower and mid-sternal border and has no significant radiation to the apex, base or back
  • Heard most frequently in children between 3 and 7 year of age
  • The intensity of the murmur often changes with respiration and position and may be attenuated in the sitting or prone position
  • A venous hum<boldin> <boldout>is another example of a common innocent murmur
<boldin>• Heard during childhood<boldout>
  • Produced by turbulence of blood in the jugular venous system
<boldin>• No pathologic significance<boldout>
  • May be heard in the neck or anterior portion of the upper part of the chest

<boldin>COMMON VALVULAR LESIONS<boldout>
<boldin>Mitral regurgitation<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>(MR)<boldout>
  • Laterally and down ward displaced apical impulse
<boldin>• High pitched<boldout>
<boldin>• Blowing type<boldout>
<boldin>• S1 is diminished (Muffled)<boldout>
<boldin>• Plateau configuration<boldout>
<boldin>• Soft to loud pansystolic murmur<boldout>
<boldin>• Best heard at apical area<boldout>
<boldin>• Radiating to axilla<boldout>
<boldin>• Accentuated by left lateral decubitus position<boldout>
<boldin>Differential diagnosis of MR<boldout>
<boldin>Acute MR<boldout>
  • Infective endocarditis: In our set up infective endocarditis is more precipitant than a primary cause
<boldin>• Papillary muscle rupture<boldout>
<boldin>• Trauma – due to ruptured chordae<boldout>
<boldin>Chronic MR<boldout>
<boldin>• Rheumatic fever<boldout>
<boldin>• Rheumatic heart disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Dilated cardiomyopathy<boldout>
  • Mitral valve prolapse also called Degenerative or Myxomatous mitral valve
<boldin>• Mitral annular calcification<boldout>
<boldin>• Infective endocarditis (healed)<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenial: due to valve defect<boldout>
<boldin>• Drugs - ergotamine, pergolide and cabergoline<boldout>
<boldin>• Functional mitral regurgitation<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy<boldout>
<boldin>Aortic regurgitation<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>(AR)<boldout>
  • Laterally and down ward displaced apical impulse
<boldin>• Wide pulse pressure<boldout>
<boldin>• Absent S2<boldout>
<boldin>• High-pitched<boldout>
<boldin>• Soft to loud blowing<boldout>
<boldin>• Decrescendo configuration<boldout>
<boldin>• Early diastolic<boldout>
  • Best heard at the second to fourth left interspace (Erb‘s point)
<boldin>• Accentuated by expiration and leaning forward<boldout>
  • Austin flint murmur at the apex – due to back regurgitation flow

<boldin>Peripheral signs of AR:<boldout>
  • Demusset/'s sign – Head bob occurring with each heart beat
  • Quincke/'s pulses – Capillary pulsations in the fingertips or lips
  • Mueller/'s sign – Systolic pulsations of the uvula
  • Becker/'s sign– Visible pulsations of z retinal arteries /& pupils
  • Hill/'s sign – Popliteal cuff systolic pressure exceeding brachial pressure by more than 60 mmHg
  • Mayne/'s sign – More than a 15 mmHg decrease in diastolic blood pressure with arm elevation from the value obtained with the arm in the standard position
  • Traube/'s sign – A pistol shot pulse (systolic and diastolic sounds) heard over the femoral arteries
  • Duroziez/'s sign – A systolic and diastolic bruit heard when the femoral artery is partially compressed
  • Rosenbach/'s sign – Systolic pulsations of the liver
  • Gerhard/'s sign – Systolic pulsations of the spleen
<boldin>Differential diagnosis of AR<boldout>
<boldin>Leaflet abnormalities<boldout>
<boldin>• Rheumatic fever<boldout>
<boldin>• Endocarditis<boldout>
<boldin>• Trauma<boldout>
<boldin>• Bicuspid aortic valve<boldout>
<boldin>• Rheumatoid arthritis<boldout>
<boldin>• Myxomatous degeneration<boldout>
<boldin>• Ankylosing spondylitis<boldout>
<boldin>Aortic root or ascending aorta problems<boldout>
<boldin>• Systemic hypertension<boldout>
<boldin>• Aortities (e.g. Syphilis)<boldout>
<boldin>• Trauma<boldout>
<boldin>• Marfan syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Dissecting aneurysm<boldout>
<boldin>• Reiter’s syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Ankylosing spondylitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Ehler’s – Danlos syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>Tricuspid regurgitation<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>(TR)<boldout>
<boldin>• Low to high-pitched<boldout>
<boldin>• Blowing type<boldout>
<boldin>• Pansystolic murmur<boldout>
  • Best heard at the left lower sternal border
<boldin>• Radiating to the epigastric area<boldout>
  • Accentuated by deep inspiration (positive caravello‘s sign)
<boldin>Mitral stenosis (MS)<boldout>
<boldin>• Accentuated S1<boldout>
<boldin>• Opening snap following S2<boldout>
<boldin>• Low-pitched<boldout>
<boldin>• Rumbling type<boldout>
<boldin>• Crescendo<boldout>
<boldin>• Mid-diastolic murmur<boldout>
<boldin>• Limited to apex<boldout>
  • Increased intensity of murmur with exercise and left lateral positioning
<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Rheumatic fever<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital mitral valve stenosis<boldout>
  • Mitral annular calcification with extension onto the leaflets (degenerative)
<boldin>• Left atrial myxoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Infective endocarditis with large vegetation<boldout>
<boldin>• Carcinoid heart disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Endomyocardial fibrosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Systemic lupus erythematous<boldout>
<boldin>• Rheumatoid arthritis<boldout>
<boldin>Aortic stenosis<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>(AS)<boldout>
<boldin>• Anacrotic arterial pulse<boldout>
  • Laterally displaced thrusting and sustained apical impulse
<boldin>• Low- pitched<boldout>
<boldin>• Rasping(rough) type<boldout>
<boldin>• Crescendo – decrescendo<boldout>
<boldin>• Mid-systolic<boldout>
<boldin>• Best heard at aortic area<boldout>
<boldin>• Radiating to the carotid arteries<boldout>
<boldin>Differential diagnosis:<boldout>
<boldin>• Rheumatic heart disease,<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital (bicuspid or unicuspid aortic valve)<boldout>
<boldin>Ventricular Septal Defect (VSD)<boldout>
  • The most common among congenital heart diseases
<boldin>• If defect is restrictive<boldout>
  • Loud harsh holosystolic murmur best heard at left lower sternal border
<boldin>• Frequently accompanied by a thrill<boldout>
<boldin>• If the defect is non restrictive<boldout>
<boldin>• May have precordial bulge<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpable parasternal heave<boldout>
<boldin>• Laterally displaced apical impulse<boldout>
<boldin>• Apical thrust and a systolic thrill<boldout>
  • Less harsh but more blowing holosystolic murmur
<boldin>• Early systolic murmur at pulmonic area<boldout>
<boldin>• Accentuated second heart sound<boldout>
<boldin>Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA)<boldout>
<boldin>• Asymptomatic in small PDA<boldout>
<boldin>• If the PDA is large<boldout>
<boldin>• Wide pulse pressure<boldout>
<boldin>• Bounding pulse<boldout>
  • Continuous machinery murmur at second left intercostal space
<boldin>• May radiate to:<boldout>
<boldin>• Down the left sternal border<boldout>
<boldin>• Left clavicle or toward the apex<boldout>
  • Often well heard over the left back
<boldin>Atrial Septal Defect (ASD)<boldout>
<boldin>• Most are asymptomatic in children<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperactive precordium<boldout>
  • Left parasternal heave at left lower sternal border
<boldin>• Loud first heart sound<boldout>
  • Widely split and fixed second heart sound
  • Mid systolic murmur best heard at the pulmonic area
<boldin>• Rumbling mid-diastolic murmur at tricuspid area<boldout>
  • Holosystolic murmur at mitral area in OP (ECD)
<boldin>SIGNS OF CONGESTED HEART FAILURE<boldout>
Consider generalized body swelling due to heart failure
<boldin>General appearance<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiopulmonary distress<boldout>
<boldin>• Edematous<boldout>
<boldin>HEENT<boldout>
<boldin>• Facial puffiness<boldout>
<boldin>Vital signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachycardia<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachypnea<boldout>
<boldin>Respiratory system<boldout>
<boldin>• Basal rales<boldout>
<boldin>• Crepitation<boldout>
<boldin>• Absent air entry<boldout>
<boldin>• Stony dullness<boldout>
<boldin>Cardiovascular system<boldout>
<boldin>• Distended neck vein<boldout>
<boldin>• Raised JVP<boldout>
<boldin>• S3 gallop<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>Abdomen<boldout>
<boldin>• Tender hepatomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Positive Hepatojugular reflex<boldout>
<boldin>• Ascites<boldout>
<boldin>Musculoskeletal system<boldout>
<boldin>• Peripheral edema or generalized body swelling<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
<boldin>• Complete blood count<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest x-ray<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiomegaly<boldout>
  • Cardiothoracic ratio /> 60% for neonates and infants
  • Cardiothoracic ratio /> 50% for older children
<boldin>• Heart Shape evaluation<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary vascular bed<boldout>
<boldin>• Increased pulmonary vascularity<boldout>
<boldin>• Truncus arteriosus<boldout>
<boldin>• Total anomalous pulmonary venous drainage (TAPVD)<boldout>
  • Large VSD with left to right shunt
<boldin>• Large PDA<boldout>
  • Transposition of the great arteries (TGA) in neonates
<boldin>• Decreased pulmonary vascularity<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary atresia with MAPCs<boldout>
<boldin>• Tricuspid atresia<boldout>
<boldin>• Ebstein’s anomaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Tetralogy of Fallot<boldout>
  • Critical pulmonary stenosis associated with a right-to-left shunt in the neonatal period
<boldin>• Pulmonary edema<boldout>
<boldin>• Pleural effusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Signs of pneumonia/PTE as a precipitant<boldout>
<boldin>• Lung parenchymal evaluation for corpulmonale<boldout>
<boldin>• ECG<boldout>
<boldin>• Chamber hypertrophy<boldout>
<boldin>• Rhythm disorders<boldout>
<boldin>• Myocardial infarction or ischemia<boldout>
  • Shows findings that suggest specific etiologies: Eg. Low-voltage QRS morphologic characteristics with ST-T wave abnormalities may suggest myocardial inflammatory disease or<boldin> <boldout>pericarditis

<boldin>• Echocardiography<boldout>
  • Gives information about ventricular size, function and valvular abnormalities
<boldin>• Size of all four chambers<boldout>
<boldin>• Ventricular function<boldout>
<boldin>• Fractional shortening (ESD-EDD/EDD): Normally 28-42%<boldout>
<boldin>• Reduced ejection fraction: Normally 55-65%<boldout>
  • Pre-ejection: ejection period ratio: Normally /< 40%
<boldin>• Calculation of COP<boldout>
  • Structural valve abnormalities – regurgitation and stenosis
<boldin>• Congenital defects – VSD, ASD, …<boldout>
<boldin>• Pericardial effusion<boldout>
  • Other investigations are based on the precipitant factors and for management purpose

<boldin>Management Principle<boldout>
<boldin>Supportive measures<boldout>
<boldin>• Bed rest<boldout>
<boldin>• Semi upright position<boldout>
<boldin>• Fluid restriction<boldout>
<boldin>• O2 administration<boldout>
  • No added salt diets– (it doesn’t mean total absence of salt)
<boldin>• Increase daily calories<boldout>
  • Restriction to competitive and strenuous sport activities
<boldin>Treat congestive state<boldout>
<boldin>• Diuretics– Lasix, spironolactone, …<boldout>
  • After load reducing agents – ACE inhibitors, …
<boldin>Treat the precipitant factor<boldout>
  • Pneumonia, infective endocarditis, anemia, rheumatic recurrence …
<boldin>Treat the underlying cause<boldout>
<boldin>• Pericardiocentesis – for tamponade<boldout>
  • Surgery for valvular heart disease (congenital or acquired)
<boldin>• Implantation of cardiac prosthesis<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiac transplantation<boldout>
  • Transfuse if severe anemia is the cause of heart failure
<boldin>Halt the progression of heart failure<boldout>
<boldin>• ACE inhibitors<boldout>
<boldin>• Beta blockers<boldout>
<boldin>• Digoxin – give only with indication<boldout>
  • Benzathine penicillin prophylaxis– monthly as secondary prevention of rheumatic heart disease

topics.title: Chapter 7: Abdominal Examination
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner:<boldout>
<boldin>• Do abdominal examination<boldout>
<boldin>• Differential diagnosis for your findings<boldout>
<boldin>• Evidences for your differential diagnoses<boldout>
<boldin>• Investigations for your differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>Approaches<boldout>
<boldin>• Inspection<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpation<boldout>
<boldin>• Percussion<boldout>
<boldin>• Auscultation<boldout>
<boldin>• Digital rectal examination<boldout>
<boldin>Abdomen has:<boldout>
<boldin>• Four quadrants (URQ, LRQ, ULQ, LLQ)<boldout>
  • Nine regions: Right hypochondrium, Left hypochondrium, Epigastrium, Right lumbar, Left lumbar, Umbilical, Right iliac, Left iliac and Hypogastrium or suprapubic

<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout><italicin>: Before starting your examination:<italicout>
  • <italicin>Check for good lightening of the room<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Communicate with the patient<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>The patient should have an empty bladder<italicout>
  • <italicin>Make the patient lying in supine position with his/her arms and hands in anatomical position<italicout>
  • <italicin>Expose the abdomen from the nipple line to the mid-thigh<italicout>
