<boldin>INSPECTION<boldout>
<boldin>Look for<boldout>
<boldin>• Symmetry of the abdomen<boldout>
<boldin>• Movement with respiration<boldout>
<boldin>• Shape of the abdomen<boldout>
<boldin>• Flank fullness<boldout>
<boldin>• Umbilicus<boldout>
<boldin>• Visible peristalsis<boldout>
<boldin>• Visible abdominal vein<boldout>
<boldin>• Skin pigmentation<boldout>
<boldin>• Scars and striae<boldout>
<boldin>• Hernia sites<boldout>
<boldin>• Diastasis recti<boldout>
<boldin>Symmetry of the abdomen<boldout>
  • Symmetric – normal, food, ascites, flatus or faces
  • Asymmetric – mass or fetus in adolescents
<boldin>Movement with respiration<boldout>
  • Markedly diminished or absent abdominal movement: - generalized peritonitis

<boldin>Shape of the abdomen<boldout>
<boldin>• Scaphoid→ SAM (cachexic), hernia<boldout>
<boldin>• Flat→ normal<boldout>
<boldin>• Full→ normal or 5f’s<boldout>
  • Distended→ 5f’s (food, fluid, flatus, faces or fetus in adolescents)
<boldin>• Protuberant → mass or mechanical obstruction<boldout>
<boldin>Flank fullness<boldout>
  • Full flanks with full or distended abdomen
<boldin>• Ascites<boldout>
<boldin>• Intestinal obstruction<boldout>
  • Psoas abscess, Hydronephrosis, Perinephric abscess, Wilms tumor… may cause full flanks without abdominal distension or asymmetric flank fullness.
<boldin>Umbilicus<boldout>
  • Normally inverted with circular slit, slightly horizontal in adults
  • Everted umbilicus – huge ascites or mass
  • Horizontal slit – ascites or bilateral flank mass
<boldin>• Vertical slit – mass<boldout>
<boldin>• Discharge, swelling or color change<boldout>
<boldin>Visible peristalsis<boldout>
  • Vigorous visible peristalsis – early intestinal obstruction, IHPS, diarrhea

<boldin>Visible abdominal vein<boldout>
  • Due to portal hypertension or Inferior vena-caval obstruction
  • Determine direction of flow in the veins during palpation by milking the distended veins above the umbilicus using the second and third fingers
  • Draining away from umbilicus – IVC obstruction
<boldin>• Draining towards umbilicus – portal hypertension<boldout>
<boldin>Skin pigmentation<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypo or hyper pigmentation<boldout>
<boldin>Scars and striae<boldout>
<boldin>• Scar – previous surgery or trauma<boldout>
  • Striae– are wrinkled linear marks due to gross stretching of the skin or rupture of the elastic fibers of abdominal wall
<boldin>• Striae alba or atrophica – Ascites<boldout>
<boldin>• Purple striae - Cushing ‘s syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Striaegravidarum - Pregnancy<boldout>
<boldin>Hernia sites<boldout>
  • Support and lift the patient’s head with the shoulder with your left arm and let him/her cough repeatedly while observing sites of hernia (for cooperative patients)
  • For uncooperative patients: observe sites of hernia while crying
  • If there is hernia, it will budge out at the hernial sites
  • The bulging mass is soft and can be pushed back in to the cavity
  • The edge of the opening can be felt during palpation
<boldin>• Common sites of hernia<boldout>
  • Inguinal and femoral hernia - at groin region
  • Periumbilical hernia  - at or around the umbilicus
<boldin>• Epigastric hernia      - at epigastric area<boldout>
  • Incisional hernia       - at surgical scar site
<boldin>Diastasis recti<boldout>
  • Separation of the two rectus abdominis muscles, through which abdominal contents bulge to form a midline ridge when the patient raises head and shoulders.
<boldin>• Prune belly syndrome<boldout>

<boldin><italicin>Note:<italicout><boldout><italicin> <italicout>
<italicin>First stand in front (on the leg side) and then on the right side of the patient to see the listed parameters<italicout><italicin>.<italicout>

<boldin>PALPATION<boldout>
<boldin>• Classified as superficial and deep palpation<boldout>
  • Be on the right side of the patient, unless you are left sided
  • Before you starting to palpate, warm your hand and explain to the patient or care giver what you are going to do

<boldin>Superficial<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>Palpation<boldout>
<boldin>Check for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Abdominal tenderness<boldout>
<boldin>• Superficially palpable abdominal masses/organs<boldout>
<boldin>• Abdominal resistance<boldout>
  • Consistency of the abdomen- soft, firm, hard…
  • Ask if there is abdominal area which is painful to touch
  • If there is an area which is painful, start far away from the tender site /& palpate the tender site at last
  • If there is no an area which is painful to touch, start at right lower quadrant and move quadrant–by- quadrant in clock-wise or anti-clockwise rotation and at last palpate at the epigastric and periumblical area
  • Look at the patient ‘s face while you palpate
  • If there is superficially palpable mass, ask the patient to raise head /& shoulder or ask the patient to rise up the lower extremities in an extension position to see if the mass disappear or protrude; Then if the mass is;
  • Protrude – it is likely in the abdominal wall such as: Lipoma, abdominal muscle abscess, Dermoid cyst
<boldin>• Disappear – it is likely intraabdominal<boldout>
<boldin>Deep<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>Palpation<boldout>
<boldin>Check for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Guarding<boldout>
<boldin>• Rigidity<boldout>
<boldin>• Rebound tenderness<boldout>
<boldin>• Organomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Abdominal mass<boldout>
<boldin>• Ballotibility (if huge ascites)<boldout>
<boldin>Guarding<boldout>
  • Involuntary reflex contraction of abdominal wall muscles overlying an inflamed viscous classically seen in uncomplicated acute appendicitis

<boldin>Rigidity<boldout>
  • Involuntary reflex rigidity of abdominal wall muscles
  • Board-like rigidity is seen in diffuse peritonitis
<boldin>Rebound tenderness<boldout>
  • Deeply and slowly palpate the abdomen, and check for the presence of sudden pain while the examiner releases his hand suddenly from the abdomen

<boldin>Abdominal Organ Palpation<boldout>
<boldin>Spleen<boldout>
  • The spleen weighs 11gram at birth, 55 gram at six years of age, and 125 gram at puberty
  • Spleen to be palpable below the left costal margin has to be enlarged 2-3x of its normal size but can be palpable in nearly one-third of neonates and in case of “Wandering spleen syndrome” ...needs ultrasound mostly
  • Enlargement of the spleen takes place in a superior and posterior direction before it becomes palpable
  • Once palpable, the direction of growth is down wards and towards the umbilicus (along the splenic growth line)
  • Start palpating from the right iliac fossa and move to the left hypochondrium
  • Palpate while the patient breathes in and move your fingers while he/she breath out
  • If not palpable, turn the patient to half on to the right side (with right leg extended and left leg flexed at hip and knee joint) /& repeat the examination as above
  • All palpable spleen is not to mean splenomegaly
  • The upper limits of normal splenic length on ultrasound measurements
  • 6 cm at 3 months
  • 7 cm at 12 months
  • 5 cm at 6 years
  • 5 cm at 12 years
  • 12 cm for girls ≥15 years
  • 13 cm for boys ≥15 years of age
<boldin>• Splenomegaly<boldout>
  • Tipped /< 3 cm below the left coastal margin
  • Moderate – between 3 and 7 cm below the left coastal margin
  • Massive />7 cm below the left coastal margin
<boldin>• If there is splenomegaly, characterize:<boldout>
<boldin>• Size along splenic growth line<boldout>
<boldin>• Tenderness<boldout>
<boldin>• Consistency - firm or hard<boldout>
<boldin>• Surface – smooth or nodular<boldout>
<boldin>• Border – regular or irregular<boldout>
<boldin>• Moves with respiration<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpable medial notch<boldout>
  • Unable to go beneath the left costal margin
<boldin>• Not bimanually palpable<boldout>
  • How to report for a splenomegaly? E.g.
  • There is a 9 cm non tender palpable mass over the left upper quadrant along the splenic growth line with palpable medial notch, regular border, smooth surface and firm in consistency which moves with respiration, not bimanually palpable, unable to go beneath the left costal margin and dull to percussion.
<boldin>• Most likely Splenomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>Investigation for splenomegaly<boldout>
Based on your differential diagnosis (E.g.; VL)
<boldin>• Complete blood count<boldout>
  • rk39
<boldin>• Splenic aspiration; Contraindication of splenic aspiration:<boldout>
<boldin>• Tipped splenomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Huge splenomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Huge ascites<boldout>
<boldin>• Tender spleen<boldout>
<boldin>• Site infection<boldout>
<boldin>• Thrombocytopenia; PLT /< 40,000<boldout>
<boldin>• Bone marrow aspiration<boldout>
<boldin>• Lymph node aspiration<boldout>
<img:t10<img:t11.png>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout><italicin>: M<italicout><italicin>assive enlargement of spleen can be palpated bimanually<italicout>
<boldin>Liver<boldout>
  • Ask the patient to breathe in deeply and start palpation from the right iliac fossa towards the right hypochondrium with the right hand below and parallel to the right costal margin
  • In children, the normal liver edge can be felt up to 2 cm below the right coastal margin
  • In newborn infant, extension of liver edge more than 3.5 cm below the right coastal margin along the right midclavicular line suggests hepatic enlargement
  • To say hepatomegaly always depend on age specific measures of total vertical liver span
<boldin>• Characterize the enlarged liver<boldout>
<boldin>• Size below the right costal margin<boldout>
<boldin>• Tenderness<boldout>
<boldin>• Consistency - soft, firm or hard<boldout>
<boldin>• Surface – smooth or nodular<boldout>
<boldin>• Edge – sharp or blunt<boldout>
<boldin>• Border – regular or irregular<boldout>
<boldin>• Movement with respiration<boldout>
<boldin>• How to report for hepatomegaly? E.g.<boldout>
  • There is a 7 cm tender palpable mass below the right costal margin along the right midclavicular line with blunted edge, regular boarder, smooth surface, firm in consistency which moves with respiration with a total vertical liver span of 14 cm.
<boldin>• Most likely congested hepatomegaly<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout>
<italicin>Never say hepatomegaly by palpation, unless you found a size below right costal margin is larger than the upper limit of normal total vertical liver span. Causes that will push down the liver: Right hyper inflated lung and Huge right pleural effusion.<italicout>
<boldin>Investigations for hepatomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Abdominal ultrasonography<boldout>
<boldin>• Complete blood count<boldout>
<boldin>• Liver function test<boldout>
<boldin>• Viral markers<boldout>
<boldin>• Computed tomography scan<boldout>
<boldin>• Magnetic resonance imaging<boldout>
<img:t12.png>
<boldin>Right Kidney<boldout>
Bimanual palpation
  • Stand on the right side of the patient
  • Place the right hand horizontally in the right lumbar region anteriorly with the left hand placed posteriorly in the right loin
  • Push forwards with the left hand, ask the patient to take a deep breath in, and press the right hand inwards and upwards
  • The lower pole of the right kidney is commonly palpable in thin patients and is felt as a smooth, rounded swelling which descends on inspiration
  • It helps to differentiate<italicin> enlarged right kidney <italicout>from <italicin>enlarged gallbladder<italicout>

<boldin>Left<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>Kidney<boldout>
<boldin>Bimanual palpation<boldout>
  • Stand on the right side of the patient
  • Place the left hand posteriorly in the left loin and right hand anteriorly in the lumbar region
  • Ask the patient to take deep breath in and press the left hand forwards and the right hand backwards, upwards and inwards
  • Not usually palpable unless it is either low in position or enlarged

<boldin>Urinary bladder<boldout>
<boldin>• Normally not palpable<boldout>
  • When it is full and the patient cannot empty it (retention), a smooth firm regular oval-shaped swelling will be palpated in the suprapubic region and its upper border may reach as far as the umbilicus
  • The lateral and upper borders can be readily made out, but it is not possible to feel its lower border (i.e. The swelling is /'arising out of the pelvis/')
  • The fact that this swelling is symmetrically placed in the suprapubic region beneath the umbilicus, that it is dull to percussion, and that pressure on it gives the patient a desire to micturate
  • In women, think of other differential diagnoses: Gravid uterus, Ovarian cyst …
<boldin>Abdominal Mass Palpation<boldout>
<boldin>Look for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Site/ location<boldout>
<boldin>• Size and shape<boldout>
<boldin>• Surface, edge, boarder and consistency<boldout>
<boldin>• Mobility and Attachment<boldout>
<boldin>• Bimanually palpable or pulsatile<boldout>
  • Ballotibility test – if there is huge ascites
<boldin>Site and location<boldout>
<boldin>• Specify the quadrant<boldout>
  • Is it abdominal wall or intra-abdominal mass?Feel the mass while the patient lifts her/his head and shoulders off the pillow to tense the abdominal wall
  • Prominently protruded mass   - abdominal wall mass
  • If the mass disappears            - intra-abdominal mass
<boldin>• Identify whether abdominal or pelvic origin<boldout>
<boldin>Surface, edge, boarder /& consistency<boldout>
<boldin>• Surface – smooth or nodular<boldout>
<boldin>• Edge – sharp or blunt<boldout>
<boldin>• Boarder – regular, irregular or ill-defined<boldout>
<boldin>• Consistency – soft, firm or hard<boldout>
<boldin>• A swelling that is<boldout>
  • Hard, irregular in outline and nodular is likely to be <italicin>malignant<italicout>
  • Regular, round, smooth, tense swelling is likely to be <italicin>cystic<italicout>
  • Solid, ill-defined and tender mass suggests an inflammatory lesion
<boldin>Mobility<boldout>
  • Mass arising from the liver, spleen, kidneys, gall bladder and stomach moves down ward during inspiration
  • Mass arising from the small bowel, transverse colon, mesentery and greater omentum are not usually influenced by respiratory movement
  • Side-to-side movable lower abdominal mass favors swelling of uterine origin but not from urinary bladder arising mass

<boldin>Attachment<boldout>
<boldin>When the mass is completely fixed it usually signifies one of the three things:<boldout>
  • A mass of retroperitoneal origin (e.g. pancreas)
  • Part of an advanced tumour with extensive spread to the anterior or posterior abdominal walls or abdominal organs
  • A mass resulting from severe chronic inflammation involving other organs (e.g. diverticulitis of the sigmoid colon or a tuberculous ileocecal mass)

<boldin>Ballotibility test<boldout>
  • It will be difficult to palpate organ in the presence of huge ascites
  • It will be better if we use Ballotibility test
<boldin>• Done by the two hands<boldout>
  • The palm of one hand is placed on the expected site of enlarged organ and the other palm of the hand is placed on the dorsum of the first hand
  • Flex the hands at the carpal metacarpal joint slightly pressing downward and then release the pressure as quick as possible but keep the hands at the site with little pressure
  • There will be a feeling of tap by the enlarged organ to your palm while the organ moves up and down due the pressure you applied and free movement in the fluid.

<boldin>Pulsatile mass<boldout>
  • Decide whether the pulsation comes from the mass or is transmitted
  • Pulsatile and expansible abdominal mass favors abdominal aneurysm
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout>
<italicin>All retroperitoneal organs except kidney and adrenal gland, have no movement with respiration. Why kidney and adrenal gland moves with respiration?<italicout>

<boldin>PERCUSSION<boldout>
Used to detect organomegaly, mass, ascites or intestinal obstruction
<boldin>Look for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Percussion notes<boldout>
<boldin>• Total vertical liver span (TVLS)<boldout>
<boldin>• Splenic dullness<boldout>
<boldin>• Shifting dullness<boldout>
<boldin>• Fluid thrill<boldout>
<boldin>Percussion Notes<boldout>
<boldin>• Tympanic – Normal<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypertympanic – Intestinal obstruction<boldout>
  • Dull – Organomegaly, mass, ascites or cystic mass
<boldin>• Colonic resonance – Enlarged kidney (Nephromegaly)<boldout>
<boldin>Total Vertical Liver Span <boldout>
  • Start percussion at the right second intercostal space over the midclavicular line down ward till you get relative dullness
  • And then, start percussion at right lower quadrant (RLQ) upward till you get dullness
  • Measure the distance between the two points (TVLS)
  • Becarefull about the lung pathologies when you report total vertical liver span. E.g. Right side pleural effusion/consolidation
  • Total vertical liver span increases linearly with body weight and age in both sexes
<boldin>• Normal total vertical liver span of ages:<boldout>
  • At 1 week of age: 4.5 – 5 cm
  • At 12 yr of age: 7 - 8cm for boys and 6.0 – 6.5cm for girls
<boldin>• At adult pediatric age group:<boldout>
  • 10 cm ±2   ↔ Normal
  • <boldin>/><boldout>12 cm     ↔ Hepatomegaly
  • <boldin>/<<boldout><boldin> <boldout>8 cm      ↔ Liver is shrinked
<boldin>Splenic dullness <boldout>
<boldin>• If not palpable and not found in the abdomen by percussion, we use maneuvers:<boldout>

<boldin>Nixon‘s method<boldout>
  • Ask the patient to turn to the right side
  • Begin percussing at the lower level of pulmonary resonance along the left posterior axillary line and proceed diagonally along a perpendicular line towards the lower mid-anterior costal margin
  • The upper border of dullness is normally 6-8 cm above the left costal margin
<boldin>• Dullness /> 8cm suggests splenomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>Castell‘s method<boldout>
  • Ask the patient to be on supine position
  • Percuss the left intercostal space at 8th or 9th interspace along left anterior axillary line during full inspiration and expiration
<boldin>• Dullness on full inspiration suggests splenomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>Traube‘s method<boldout>
  • Ask the patient to be on supine position with the left arm slightly abducted
  • Percuss from medial to lateral margins in normal breathing. It usually produces normal resonant sound at Traube’s space
<boldin>• Borders of Traube’s space:<boldout>
<boldin>• Superiorly sixth rib<boldout>
<boldin>• Laterally left mid axillary line<boldout>
<boldin>• Inferiorly left costal margin<boldout>
  • Dullness to percussion at Traube’s space suggests splenomegaly

<boldin>Ascites<boldout>
  • Ascites is a condition of pathologic fluid accumulation within the peritoneal cavity
  • There are three theories of ascitic formation
• <boldin><italicin>Underfilling theory <italicout><boldout>
This theory suggests that the primary abnormality is inappropriate sequestration of fluid with in the splanchnic vascular bed due to portal hypertension and a consequent decrease in effective circulating blood volume. This activates the plasma renin, aldosterone, and sympathetic nervous system resulting in renal sodium and water retention.

• <boldin><italicin>Overflow theory<italicout><boldout>
This theory suggests that the primary abnormality is inappropriate renal retention of sodium and water in the absence of volume depletion.

• <boldin><italicin>Peripheral arterial vasodilation theory <italicout><boldout>
It is the most recent theory which includes components of the above two theories. It suggests that portal hypertension leads to vasodilation which causes decreased effective arterial blood volume. As the natural history of the disease progresses, neurohormonal excitation increases, more renal sodium is retained and plasma volume expands. This leads to overflow of fluid into the peritoneal cavity.

<boldin>Detection of Ascites<boldout>
<boldin>Shifting Dullness<boldout>
  • Ask the patient to be on supine position
  • Start percussion on the midline near the umbilicus and move your fingers laterally towards the flank
  • When dullness is detected, keep your fingers in that position and ask the patient to turn towards the opposite side
  • Wait for ten seconds till peritoneal fluid redistributes
  • Confirmed when the percussion note changed from dullness to tympanic at site of first dullness detected
  • If the ascitic fluid is small in amount, put the patient in knee chest position and percuss starting from the flanks towards the umbilicus. Dullness will be elicited at the umbilical area if there is fluid and this is called <boldin>puddle sign<boldout>.

<boldin>Fluid Thrill<boldout>
  • Place one hand flat over the lumbar region of one side
  • Ask an assistant to put ulnar side of his hand longitudinally and firmly in the midline of the abdomen to damp transmission of impulse via abdominal wall fat,
  • Then tap the lumbar region on your side with the middle finger and feel for the wave
<img:t13.png>
<boldin>Ascites Grading<boldout>
Based on physical signs <italicin>(SN Chugh bed side medicine; page 58)<italicout>
<boldin>• Grade0 (minimal)  = Positive puddle’s sign<boldout>
  • Grade 1 (mild)  = Shifting dullness present, fluid thrill absent
  • Grade 2 (moderate) = Shifting dullness and fluid thrill present
  • Grade 3 (massive) = Fluid thrill present, shifting dullness absent
A grading system proposed by the International Ascites Club
  • Grade 1 = Mild ascites detectable only by ultrasound examination
  • Grade 2 = Moderate ascites manifested by moderate symmetrical distension
  • Grade 3 = Large or gross ascites with marked abdominal distension
  • However, the validity of the grading system has not yet been established
  • In particular, the natural history of /"grade 1/" ascites is unclear
An older system that grades ascites from 1+ to 4+ is also used
  • Grade 1<boldin>+ <boldout>= Minimal and barely detectable
<boldin>• Grade 2<boldin>+ <boldout>= Moderate<boldout>
  • Grade 3<boldin>+ <boldout>= Massive but not tense
<boldin>• Grade 4<boldin>+ <boldout>= Massive and tense<boldout>
<img:t14.png>
<boldin>Portal Hypertension<boldout>
  • Normal portal pressure is between 3 and 6 mmHg
  • Portal hypertension is defined as a portal pressure greater than 10 mmHg
  • Clinically significant portal hypertension exists when pressure exceeds a threshold of 12 mmHg or greater

<boldin>Causes<boldout>
  • Increased splanchnic blood flow secondary to vasodilatation within splanchnic vascular bed or Increased portal resistance
  • Causes can be classified as: Pre-hepatic, Hepatic and Post hepatic
<boldin>Pre-hepatic<boldout>
<boldin>• Portal vein thrombosis or cavernous transformation<boldout>
<boldin>• Portal vein agenesis, atresia, stenosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Splenic vein thrombosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Increased portal flow<boldout>
<boldin>• Arteriovenous fistula<boldout>
<boldin>Hepatic<boldout>
<boldin>• Pre-sinusoidal: Schistosomiasis, congenital hepatic fibrosis<boldout>
  • Sinusoidal: Cirrhosis, alcoholic hepatitis (uncommon in pediatrics patients)
<boldin>• Post-sinusoidal: Hepatic sinusoidal obstruction (veno-occlusive syndrome)<boldout>
<boldin>Post-hepatic<boldout>
<boldin>• Budd chiari syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• IVC obstruction<boldout>
<boldin>• Restrictive cardiomyopathy<boldout>
<boldin>• Constrictive pericarditis<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiac cirrhosis<boldout>
<boldin>Complications of portal hypertension <boldout>
<boldin>• Gastro-esophageal varieces<boldout>
<boldin>• Ascites<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperspleenism<boldout>
<boldin>• Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations for ascites<boldout>
  • Depends on the diagnosis that we have considered
  • Determine serum ascites albumin gradient (SAAG) level based on serum albumin and ascitic fluid albumin gradient and put the differential diagnosis based on SAAG
<boldin>• Random blood sugar<boldout>
<boldin>• Complete blood count<boldout>
<boldin>• Abdominal ultrasonography<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum albumin level<boldout>
<boldin>• Liver function test<boldout>
<boldin>• Viral markers<boldout>
<boldin>• Renal function test<boldout>
<boldin>• Urine analysis<boldout>
<boldin>• Ascitic fluid analysis:<boldout>
<boldin>• Appearance<boldout>
<boldin>• Clear or straw: Transudative causes, TB<boldout>
  • Hemorrhagic: Trauma, Malignancy, Tuberculosis, Bleeding diathesis, Acute hemorrhagic pancreatitis
  • Chylous: Malignancy, Trauma to lymphatic vessels, Tuberculosis
  • Cloudy: Pyogenic peritonitis, Ruptured amoebic liver abscess, Septicemia
<boldin>• Turbidity<boldout>
<boldin>• Cell count<boldout>
<boldin>• Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH) level<boldout>
<boldin>• Protein level<boldout>
<boldin>• Albumin level<boldout>
<boldin>• Glucose level<boldout>
<boldin>• Gram stain<boldout>
<boldin>• Acid-Fast Bacillus (AFB) test<boldout>
<boldin>• GeneXpert<boldout>
<boldin>• Culture<boldout>
<boldin>• Cytology study<boldout>
<boldin>• Blood culture<boldout>
<boldin>• Peritoneal biopsy and cytology<boldout>
<boldin>• Echocardiography (Echo)<boldout>
<boldin>• Electrocardiography (ECG)<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest X-ray (CXR)<boldout>
<boldin>• Thyroid function test (TFT)<boldout>
<boldin>• Pancreatic enzymes<boldout>
<boldin>• Computed tomography (CT) scan of abdomen<boldout>
<boldin>• Stool examination<boldout>
<boldin>• Plain abdominal x-ray<boldout>
<boldin>Complications of ascites <boldout>
  • Right sided pleural effusion due to leakage of ascitic fluid through lymphatic channels in the diaphragm and commonly encountered in patients with nephrotic syndrome and Portal hypertension secondary to sinusoidal causes.
<boldin>• Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis<boldout>
  • Abdominal hernia (umbilical, inguinal, epigastric…) and divarication of recti due to tense ascites as a result of increased intraabdominal pressure
<boldin>• Functional renal failure<boldout>
<boldin>• Mesenteric venous thrombosis<boldout>
<boldin>AUSCULTATION<boldout>
<boldin>Look for<boldout>
<boldin>• Bowel Sounds<boldout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Succession<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>Splash<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Vascular Bruits<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>Bowel Sounds<boldout>
  • Place the stethoscope just to the right of the umbilicus, at the site of ileocecal valve
  • Normal sounds consist of clicks and gurgles with a frequency of 6-36 per minute
  • Increased frequency of bowel sounds occur in patients with Diarrhea or Mechanical intestinal obstruction
  • Reduced or absent bowel sounds occur in patients with Paralytic ileus, Generalized peritonitis, Severe sepsis or Shock
<boldin>Vascular Bruits<boldout>
<boldin>Listen for bruits by applying the stethoscope lightly:<boldout>
  • Above and to the left of the umbilicus – aorta
  • Above the umbilicus and 2 cm from the midline - renal bruit
<boldin>• At iliac fossae - iliac arteries<boldout>
  • At epigastrium - coeliac or superior mesenteric arteries
  • Over the enlarged liver - increased blood flow in liver tumors
  • Over the enlarged spleen for friction rub - splenic infarction
<boldin>Succession Splash<boldout>
  • Place the patient in supine position and place the diaphragm of the stethoscope over the epigastrium
  • Place your hands on the lumbar region of the abdomen, and roll the patient briskly from side to side
  • A positive test can be considered when a splashing noise is heard, either with the naked ear or with the aid of a stethoscope.
  • Splashing sound is heard if the stomach is distended with fluid
  • Positive test is confirmed if there is splashing sound after four hours of meal intake
  • Succession splash is positive in patients with Gastric outlet obstruction (pyloric stenosis), Paralytic ileus or Intestinal obstruction with distended bowel loops

<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout>
<italicin>Better if you auscultate before palpation and percussion for “bowel sounds” to avoid bowel obstruction/ disturbance during palpation or percussion.<italicout>

<boldin>Digital Rectal Examination<boldout>
<boldin>• Purposes:<boldout>
  • To check for patency of the anal canal in neonates
  • To look for localized diseases like anal fissure…
  • To assess the tone of anal sphincter
<boldin>• To look for evidences of intussusception<boldout>
  • To differentiate partial /& complete obstruction in case of intestinal obstruction
  • It is better to do digital rectal examination after you finished all examinations what you want to do to avoid patient disturbance or discomfort
  • Explain for the patient and attendant what you are going to do and ask for permission to proceed
  • Try to make the environment aesthetically pleasing to the patient
  • Ask the patient to wear out his or her trouser and underwear
  • Position the patient to lie on left lateral side with buttocks at the edge of the coach and knees drawn up to the chest
  • Put on gloves and spread the buttocks apart
<boldin>• Inspect perianal areas for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Active bleeding or discharge
<img:ch07_t01_01.png><boldout>
<boldin>• Protruded rectal mucosa<boldout>
<boldin>• Excoriations<boldout>
<boldin>• Fissure<boldout>
<boldin>• Signs of inflammation<boldout>
<boldin>• Ulcers<boldout>
<boldin>• Lumps<boldout>
<boldin>• Lubricate your gloved little finger or index finger (for older children) and gently insert your little or index finger tip into the anal canal in a direction pointing toward the umbilicus and notice for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Patency of the anal canal<boldout>
<boldin>• Gush of air<boldout>
<boldin>• Sphincter tone of the anus<boldout>
<boldin>• Tenderness<boldout>
<boldin>• Induration<boldout>
<boldin>• Irregularities or nodules<boldout>
<boldin>• Circumferential mass for intussusception<boldout>
  • Insert your finger into the rectum as far as possible
  • Rotate your finger to palpate the rectal surface for tenderness, nodules, irregularities or induration
  • Gently withdraw your finger and wipe the patient’s anus
  • Look for fecal matter or blood staining on your gloved finger
Differential diagnosis for intra-abdominal mass (differs based on your physical findings)
<boldin>• Hepatomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Splenomegaly<boldout>
<boldin>• Hepatoblastoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Non Hodgkin Lymphoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Neuroblastoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Wilms tumor<boldout>
<boldin>• Rhabdomyosarcoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Teratoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Ovarian cyst<boldout>
<boldin>• Appendicial mass<boldout>
<boldin>• Appendicial abscess<boldout>
<boldin>• Abdominal TB Lymphadenopathy<boldout>
<boldin>• Polycystic kidney disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Aortic aneurism<boldout>
<boldin>• Psoas abscess<boldout>
<boldin>• Perinephric abscess<boldout>
<boldin>• Hydronephrosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Ileocecal TB<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 8: Musculoskeletal System Examination
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner<boldout>
<boldin>• Do musculoskeletal system examination or<boldout>
<boldin>• What do you see?<boldout>
<boldin>• Differential diagnosis for your finding<boldout>
<boldin>• Investigations for your differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principles<boldout>
<boldin>Approaches<boldout>
<boldin>• Look (Inspection)<boldout>
<boldin>• Feel (Palpation)<boldout>
<boldin>• Move (Range of motion)<boldout>
<boldin>• Measure (Measurement)<boldout>
<boldin>Note <boldout>
  • Before starting to examine, communicate to the patient and attendant
  • Explain for the patient and attendant what you are going to do and ask for permission to proceed
<boldin>• Expose both sides<boldout>
<boldin>• Check for adequate light exposure<boldout>
<boldin>• Always compare bilaterally<boldout>
<boldin>• Start on the normal side<boldout>
<boldin>Look<boldout>
<boldin>Look for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Symmetry/asymmetry between sides<boldout>
  • Skin color change – erythematous change, darkness of the skin…
<boldin>• Skin dryness/wetness<boldout>
<boldin>• Hair distribution over the skin<boldout>
<boldin>• Swelling<boldout>
<boldin>• Dilated veins<boldout>
<boldin>• Scar<boldout>
  • Wound or surgical dressing – look at wound examination in detail
<boldin>• Ulceration<boldout>
<boldin>• Deformity<boldout>
<boldin>• Atrophy<boldout>
<boldin>Feel<boldout>
  • Temperature – with your back of the hand
<boldin>• Tenderness<boldout>
<boldin>• Dryness or wetness<boldout>
<boldin>• Lump or mass<boldout>
<boldin>• Muscle bulk – for adult pediatrics age groups, measure by tape meter at:<boldout>
  • 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity – for lower leg
  • 20 cm above the tibial tuberosity – for thigh
  • Pulse – check bilaterally at the same time
<boldin>• Lower extremity<boldout>
<boldin>• Dorsalispedis arteries<boldout>
<boldin>• Posterior tibialis arteries<boldout>
<boldin>• Popliteal arteries<boldout>
<boldin>• Femoral arteries<boldout>
<boldin>• Upper extremity<boldout>
<boldin>• Radial arteries<boldout>
<boldin>• Brachial arteries<boldout>
<boldin>• Edema – presence/absence, pitting/non pitting<boldout>
<boldin>Move (Range of motion)<boldout>
<boldin>Active movement<boldout>
  • Done by only the patient, no need of applying external force
  • Ask the patient to move the normal one first and then the affected site
<boldin>• Watch for:<boldout>
  • Decreased or increased movement of the joint compared to the normal one
<boldin>• Pain with movement<boldout>
<boldin>• Abnormal movement<boldout>
• Listen for crepitus or “popping<boldin>”<boldout>
<boldin>Passive movement<boldout>
  • Move the joints passively (by external force) and comparing the end points to the end point of active movement
<boldin>• Look for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Any decreased or increased movement<boldout>
<boldin>• Pain with the movement<boldout>
<boldin>• Crepitus or “popping” sound while moving<boldout>
<boldin>Measure<boldout>
  • Apparent length: From xiphisternum or umbilicus to medial malleolus
  • Real length: From greater trochanter of the femur up to the medial malleolus
  • True length: Between two bony prominences of a single long bone

topics.title: Chapter 9: Wound Examination
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible Questions by the examiner:<boldout>
<boldin>• What do you see?<boldout>
<boldin>• Characterize this finding<boldout>
<boldin>• Differential diagnosis for your findings<boldout>
<boldin>• Investigation for your differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>Approach<boldout>
<boldin>• Inspection<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpation<boldout>
<boldin>Inspection<boldout>
<boldin>• Site/location, Size (estimate)<boldout>
<boldin>• Number – single, multiple<boldout>
<boldin>• Discharge / bleeding<boldout>
  • Visible dressing: Clean, Bloody, Any color change …
  • Floor / surface of the wound/ ulcer: Discharge, Granulation, Slough…
<boldin>• Surrounding skin: Redness, Pigmentation, Swelling<boldout>
<boldin>• Margin: Regular, Irregular, Round or oval<boldout>
<boldin>• EdgeTypes of edge:<boldout>
  • Sloping edge: it is sign of healing and has 3 parts
  • Outer → White - due to scar or fibrous formation
  • Middle  → Blue  - due to epithelial formation
  • Inner    →  Red - due to a red healthy granulation
<boldin>• Undermined edge<boldout>
<boldin>• Seen in a tuberculous ulcer<boldout>
  • Disease process advances in deeper plane whereas skin proliferates inwards
<boldin>• Punched out edge<boldout>
<boldin>• Seen in a gummatous (syphilitic) ulcer<boldout>
<boldin>• Due to endarteritis<boldout>
<boldin>• Raised and beaded edge<boldout>
<boldin>• Seen in a rodent ulcer (BCC)<boldout>
  • Beads are due to proliferating active cells
<boldin>• Everted edge<boldout>
<boldin>• Seen in a carcinomatous ulcer(SCC)<boldout>
  • Due to spill of the proliferating malignant tissues over the normal skin
<boldin>• Inflamed edge<boldout>
<boldin>• Red, irregular with inflamed surrounding skin<boldout>
<boldin>Palpation<boldout>
<boldin>• Temperature<boldout>
<boldin>• Tenderness<boldout>
<boldin>• Size (measured value)<boldout>
  • Floor – see if bleeds/discharge on touch
<boldin>• Check the involvement of underlying structures<boldout>
<boldin>• Related examinations<boldout>
<boldin>• Related lymph nodes<boldout>
  • Related arteries, veins and nerves: Pulse and capillary refill
  • Movement in neighboring joints: Restriction to movement indicates muscle involvement or painful inflammation…

<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Ulcer has four parts<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Margin – a thin and most outer part of the ulcer<italicout>
  • <italicin>Edge – connects the margin and floor<italicout>
  • <italicin>Floor – it is the one visible (the visible wound)<italicout>
  • <italicin>Base – it is the one the ulcer rests, can be bone or soft tissue<italicout>

topics.title: Chapter 10: Mass Examination
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible Questions by the examiner:<boldout>
<boldin>• What do you see?<boldout>
<boldin>• Differential diagnosis for your findings<boldout>
<boldin>• Investigation for your differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>Approach<boldout>
<boldin>• Inspection<boldout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Palpation<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Auscultation <italicout>– <italicin>for bruit (not for all<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>masses)<italicout>
<boldin>Inspection<boldout>
<boldin>• Number – single or multiple<boldout>
<boldin>• Site/location<boldout>
<boldin>• Overlying skin color change<boldout>
<boldin>• Discharge or ulceration<boldout>
<boldin>• Size (estimate)<boldout>
<boldin>• Shape<boldout>
  • Movement with respiration, swallowing (especially for neck swelling) or joints

<boldin>Palpation<boldout>
<boldin>Mass<boldout>
  • Temperature – compare with the surrounding skin
<boldin>• Tenderness<boldout>
<boldin>• Surface – smooth, nodular<boldout>
<boldin>• Border – regular, irregular or ill-defined<boldout>
<boldin>• Consistency – soft, firm, hard<boldout>
  • Fixity – to overlying or under lying tissue
<boldin>• Pulsatility<boldout>
<boldin>• Size (measured value)<boldout>
<boldin>Soft tissue<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>Swelling<boldout>
<boldin>• Temperature (warmness or coldness)<boldout>
<boldin>• Tenderness<boldout>
<boldin>• Fluctuation<boldout>
<boldin>• Transillumunation<boldout>
<boldin>• Reducibility<boldout>
<boldin>• Compressibility<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulsatility<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note:<italicout><boldout>
<boldin><italicin>When you asked:<italicout><boldout>
<boldin><italicin>What do you see?<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Better if you report after you finished your examination<italicout>
  • <italicin>After you finished your examination start to report your findings<italicout>
  • <italicin>Try to avoid interruptions (terms like on inspection, on palpation and the like), report the whole at once in orderly fashion so that your report will have a flow and make easy to recall your differential diagnosis<italicout>

<boldin><italicin>Differential diagnosis<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>List from most likely to less likely<italicout>
  • <italicin>When you list your differential diagnosis for a mass; think of the structures that are found under/overlying the mass (i.e. skin, connective tissue, adipose tissue, muscles, vessels, nerves, bones, and other surrounding structures) …then list the benign and malignant disease forms of each structure and do NOT forget the age of the patient<italicout>
  • <italicin>Differential diagnosis should always be in line with the finding of your examination. Therefore, be pertinent to your finding.<italicout>

<boldin>Example<boldout>
<boldin>Neck mass<boldout>
<boldin>• Teratoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Fibroma<boldout>
<boldin>• Fibro sarcoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Lipoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Rhabdomyosarcoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Sternocleidomastoid (SCM) tumor<boldout>
<boldin>• Hematoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemangioma<boldout>
<boldin>• Lymphadenopathy<boldout>
<boldin>• Lymphadenitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Cystic hygroma<boldout>
<boldin>• Castlman disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Lymphoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Neuroblastoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Goiter<boldout>
<boldin>• Thyroglosal duct cyst<boldout>
<boldin>• Brachial cleft cyst<boldout>
<boldin>• Nasopharyngeal cancer<boldout>
<boldin>• Osteosarcoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Ewing sarcoma<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
  • Based on the location and physical findings of the mass
<boldin>• Complete blood count<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemoglobin<boldout>
<boldin>• Fine needle aspiration cytology<boldout>
<boldin>• Biopsy<boldout>
<boldin>• X – ray<boldout>
<boldin>• Ultrasonography<boldout>
<boldin>• Computed tomography scan<boldout>
<boldin>• Magnetic resonance imaging …<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 11: Lower Motor Examination
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible Questions by the examiner:<boldout>
<boldin>• Do lower motor examination<boldout>
  • What did you find from your examination?
<boldin>• Differential diagnosis for your findings<boldout>
<boldin>• Investigation for your differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>Approaches<boldout>
<boldin>• Inspection<boldout>
<boldin>• Muscle tone<boldout>
<boldin>• Muscle strength<boldout>
<boldin>• Reflexes (deep and superficial)<boldout>
<boldin>Inspection<boldout>
  • Position of the extremities after repositioning in anatomical position and comment as normal anatomical position, rotated medially or laterally
<boldin>• Spontaneous or induced fasciculation<boldout>
  • Muscle bulk (Atrophy, symmetry or asymmetry) by inspection and measuring with tape meter
<boldin>• Compare bilaterally<boldout>
<boldin>Muscle tone<boldout>
  • Muscle tone is referred as resistance of a muscle to passive stretch
<boldin>• Ask the patient to relax<boldout>
  • Flex and extend the patient/'s fingers, wrist, and elbow
  • Flex and extend patient/'s ankle and knee
  • There is normally a small, continuous resistance to passive movement
<boldin>• Observe for flaccid or spastic tone<boldout>
<boldin>Spastic<boldout>
<boldin>• Increased muscle tone<boldout>
<boldin>• Upper motor neuron lesion<boldout>
  • Types of spasticity are Clasp–knife spasticity and Lead pipe rigidity
<boldin>• Clasp–knife spasticity<boldout>
<boldin>• Initiate and finish with knife-resistance-released effect<boldout>
  • Characterized by initially resistance to passive movement of the limb and a sudden release after reaching a maximum resistance
  • Because spasticity results from upper motor neuron dysfunction, it disproportionately affects the upper-extremity flexors and lower-extremity extensors
  • It occurs in conjunction with disuse atrophy, hyperactive deep tendon reflexes and extensor plantar reflexes (Babinski sign)
<boldin>• Commonly seen in pyramidal tract lesions<boldout>
<boldin>• Lead pipe rigidity<boldout>
  • Characterized by resistance to passive movement that is equal in the flexors and extensors regardless of the velocity of movement
  • Increased tone present throughout the passive movement
<boldin>• Commonly seen in basal ganglia lesions<boldout>
<boldin>Flaccid<boldout>
<boldin>• Decreased muscle tone<boldout>
<boldin>• Lower motor neuron lesion<boldout>
  • Loss muscle tone causing the limb to be loose or floppy
  • The affected limb may be hyper extensible or flail like
<boldin>Muscle strength / power <boldout>
  • Test strength by having the patient move against your resistance
  • Always compare one side to the other
  • Has 6 grades (0 – 5) and scored out of 5
<boldin>• Grading:<boldout>
0/5 = No muscle movement
1/5 = Visible flicker muscle movement but no movement at the joints
2/5 = Movement at joints but not against gravity
3/5 = Movement against gravity but not against added resistance
4/5 = Movement against resistance but less than normal
5/5 = Normal strength
<boldin>Reflexes<boldout>
<boldin>Deep tendon reflex<boldout>
  • The patient must be relaxed and positioned properly before starting
  • Use no more force than you need to provoke a definite response
  • Reflexes can be reinforced by having the patient perform isometric contraction of other muscles (clenched teeth, hooking up fingers of both hands), and named as Jendrasik maneuver
  • Grading scores ranges from 0 to 4
<boldin>• Grade:<boldout>
0 		= Absent (by Jendrasik maneuver)
1+ or + 	= Hypoactive (less brisk)
2+ or ++ 	= Normal (brisk)
3+ or +++ 	= Hyperactive without clonus (very brisk)
4+ or ++++ 	= Hyperactive with clonus
<boldin>Technique<boldout>
<boldin>Knee<boldout>
  • Have the patient sit or lie down with the knee flexed
<boldin>• In sitting position:<boldout>
  • Place your left pronated arm below the patient ‘s right knee and left hand over the patient ‘s left knee, and strike the right patellar tendon just below the patella
  • Place your left supinated arm below the patient ‘s right and left knees, and strike the left patellar tendon just below the patella
  • Note contraction of the quadriceps and extension of the knee
<boldin>Ankle<boldout>
<boldin>• Dorsiflex the foot at the ankle<boldout>
<boldin>• Strike the Achilles tendon<boldout>
  • Watch for contraction of gastrocnemius muscle and plantar flexion at the ankle
<boldin>Clonus: <boldout>
  • When there are sustained rhythmic oscillations (≥3 rhythmic oscillations)
<boldin>Ankle clonus<boldout>
  • If the reflexes seem hyperactive, test for ankle clonus
  • Support the knee in a partly flexed position
  • With the patient relaxed, quickly dorsiflex the foot
<boldin>• Observe for sustained rhythmic oscillations<boldout>
<boldin>Knee clonus<boldout>
<boldin>• Partially flex the knee<boldout>
  • Hold the patella and quickly push downwards
<boldin>• Observe for sustained rhythmic oscillations<boldout>
<boldin>Superficial reflex<boldout>
<boldin>Plantar reflex<boldout>
  • Stroke the lateral aspect of the sole of each foot up to the second toe with the end of a reflex hammer or key
  • Note movement of the toes, normally flexion
<boldin>• Remember:<boldout>
  • Extension of the big toe with fanning of the other toes is abnormal, referred to as a positive <boldin><italicin>Babinski sign,<italicout><boldout><boldin> <boldout>sign of upper motor neuron lesion (UMNL)
  • Flexion of the big toe is referred to as plantar reflex down going
  • If the big toe is neither extending nor flex is referred to as plantar reflex has no response
  • Never say either Babinski sign is negative or equivocal
  • Too vigorous stimulation may produce withdrawal, which may be misinterpreted as a Babinski sign
  • Plantar responses have limited diagnostic utility in neonates, depending on how the foot is positioned
  • Asymmetry of the reflexes or plantar response is a useful lateralizing sign in infants and children
<boldin>• Alternative methods of eliciting Babinski sign:<boldout>
  • <italicin>Oppenheim‘s sign<italicout>: Extension of big toe with dorsiflexion of the foot is also obtained by pressing heavily along the medial border of the tibia
  • <italicin>Gordon ‘s sign<italicout>: Squeezing the calf or Achilles tendon
  • <italicin>Chadok ‘s sign<italicout>: Stroking the lateral border of foot
  • <italicin>Bing sign<italicout>: Pressing the dorsum of big toe
  • No response to plantar reflex indicates lower motor neuron lesion
<img:t15.png>
<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>Flaccid paraplegia<boldout>
<boldin>• Guillain-Barré syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Poliomyelitis (usually asymmetric)<boldout>
<boldin>• Tabes dorsalis (tertiary neurosyphilis)<boldout>
<boldin>• Spinal shock<boldout>
<boldin>• Cauda equina syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Polyradiculoneuropathy<boldout>
<boldin>• Peripheral neuropathies<boldout>
  • Periodic paralysis due to hyperkalemia or hypokalemia
<boldin>• Freidreich/'s ataxia<boldout>
<boldin>Spastic paralysis<boldout>
<boldin>Compressive causes<boldout>
<boldin>• Pott’s disease (TB spondylitis)<boldout>
<boldin>• Vertebral neoplasms<boldout>
<boldin>• Disc prolapse<boldout>
<boldin>• Epidural abscess or haemorrhage<boldout>
<boldin>• Syringomyelia<boldout>
<boldin>• Haematomyelia<boldout>
<boldin>• Meningioma<boldout>
<boldin>• Neurofibroma<boldout>
<boldin>Non compressive causes<boldout>
<boldin>• Acute transverse myelitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Lathyrism<boldout>
<boldin>• Vacuolar myelopathy (AIDS myelopathy)<boldout>
  • Subacute combined degeneration (SCD) of spinal cord (Vit B12 deficiency)
<boldin>• Hypocupric myelopathy (copper deficiency)<boldout>
<boldin>• Hereditory spastic paraplegia<boldout>
<boldin>• Tropical spastic paraplegia<boldout>
<boldin>• Multiple sclerosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Postvaccinal myelitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Schistosomiasis<boldout>
<boldin>• Sarcoidosis<boldout>
  • Transverse myelitis 2° to hepB, CMV, HSV, HIV…
<boldin>• SLE…<boldout>
<boldin>Cerebral paraplegia<boldout>
<boldin>• Cerebral diplegia<boldout>
<boldin>• Superior sagittal sinus thrombosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Parasagittal meningioma (usually falx meningioma)<boldout>
<boldin>• Thrombosis of unpaired anterior cerebral artery<boldout>
<boldin>• Hydrocephalus<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
Depends on your findings and differential diagnosis
<boldin>• Lumbar puncture – Guillain-Barre syndrome (GBS)<boldout>
<boldin>• X-ray of chest, vertebrae<boldout>
  • Computed tomography (CT) scan of the spine
  • CT myelography - to look for parenchymal lesion of the spinal cord
  • Spinal Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) – lytic lesion, destructive lesion…
<boldin>• Abdominal ultrasonography – malignancy<boldout>
  • Complete blood count (CBC) – malignancy, epidural abscess, TB spondylitis
  • Provider-initiated HIV testing and counselling (PITC) - AIDS myelopathy
  • Serum Vitamin B12 level - Subacute combined degeneration (SCD) of spinal cord
<boldin>• Copper study - Hypocupric myelopathy<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum electrolyte – hypokalemia or hyperkalemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Nerve conduction test<boldout>
<boldin>• Antinuclear antibody (ANA) - autoimmune diseases<boldout>
<boldin>• Stool examination – Schistosomiasis<boldout>
  • Venereal disease research laboratory (VDRL) test – Tabes dorsalis
<boldin>• Viral markers – transverse myelitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Blood culture<boldout>
<boldin>• Organ function test (OFT)<boldout>
<boldin>Key words<boldout>
<boldin>-Paresis<boldout>
  • A condition typified by a weakness of voluntary movement or partial loss of voluntary movement or by impaired movement
<boldin>• Power of 3/5 or 4/5<boldout>
<boldin>-Plegia<boldout>
  • Used to describe paralysis in which all voluntary movement is lost or a weakness with complete or nearly complete
<boldin>• Power of 0/5, 1/5 or 2/5<boldout>
<boldin>Upper motor neuron (UMN) weakness<boldout>
  • Results from disorders that affect the upper motor neurons or their axons in the cerebral cortex, internal capsule, brain stem or spinal cord

<boldin>Lower motor neuron (LMN) weakness<boldout>
  • Results from disorders of cell bodies of the lower motor neurons in the brain stem motor nuclei and anterior horn of the spinal cord or dysfunctions of axons of these neurons

<boldin>Myopathic weakness<boldout>
  • Results from disorders within the motor unit that affect the muscle fibers or neuromuscular junction

topics.title: Chapter 12: Malnutrition
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner:<boldout>
  • Asses for signs of malnutrition (i.e. Macronutrient + Micronutrients deficiency)
  • Asses for signs of micro nutrient deficiency (i.e. Iron, vitamin D, A, C, K … deficiency)
  • What do you see? (i.e. Any finding you see on the patient)
<boldin>• What is your finding?<boldout>
<boldin>• What is your conclusion?<boldout>
<boldin>• Differential diagnosis for your findings<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principles<boldout>
<boldin>Introduction <boldout>
  • Any condition caused by excess or deficient food energy or nutrient intake or by imbalance of nutrients
<boldin>• Classified into under nutrition and over-nutrition<boldout>
  • Traditionally we use the term malnutrition to mean undernutrition
  • Acute malnutrition is defined as a patient having sign and symptoms of malnutrition for a duration of /<3 months
  • Severe acute malnutrition is defined by the presence of bilateral pitting oedema or severe wasting (mid upper arm circumference (MUAC) /< 11.5 cm or a weight for height/length (WFH/L) /< -3 z-score [WHO standards]) in children 6-59 months’ old.
  • Nutrition can be assessed by the following four criteria: ABCD method
<boldin>• <boldin>A<boldout>nthropometric measurements<boldout>
<boldin>• <boldin>B<boldout>iochemical (investigation)<boldout>
<boldin>• <boldin>C<boldout>linical presentation<boldout>
<boldin>• <boldin>D<boldout>ietary history<boldout>
<boldin>Causes of malnutrition (under nutrition)<boldout>
  • Is a consequence of a deficiency in nutrient intake and/or absorption in the body
  • Can be Primary: due to inadequate food supply or access or Secondary: secondary to other diseases
  • The three layers of the determinants of nutritional status (UNICEF (6)) are immediate causes, underlying causes and basic causes.

<boldin>• Immediate Causes:<boldout>
<boldin>• Are causes which act on individuals<boldout>
  • Include: inadequate dietary intake and infection or disease
<boldin>• Underlying Causes<boldout>
<boldin>• Influence households and communities.<boldout>
  • Include shocks, such as Drought, Flooding …
<boldin>• Inadequate access to health services<boldout>
<boldin>• Household food insecurity …<boldout>
<boldin>• Basic causes:<boldout>
<boldin>• They influence communities and societies<boldout>
  • These include the country’s social, economic and political situation

<boldin>Question: Asses for signs of malnutrition<boldout>
<img:ch12_t01_02.png>
Both macro and micro nutrient deficiencies should be assessed
<boldin>General appearance<boldout>
<boldin>• Level of consciousness<boldout>
<boldin>• Health status (acute, healthy, chronic)<boldout>
  • Old man appearance or cachexic – skinny and bony appearance
<boldin>• Emaciated<boldout>
<boldin>• Edematous<boldout>
<boldin>Vital signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachycardia<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachypnea<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypo or hyperthermia<boldout>
<boldin>• Postural hypotension<boldout>
<boldin>Anthropometry<boldout>
<boldin>• Weight<boldout>
<boldin>• Height (length)<boldout>
<boldin>• Weight for Age (Wt/A)<boldout>
<boldin>• Height for Age (Ht/A)<boldout>
  • Weight for Height (Wt/Ht)                             depends on age /& sex
<boldin>• Mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC)<boldout>
<boldin>• Body mass index (BMI)<boldout>
<boldin>• Head circumference for Age (HC/A)<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note:<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Length  – For ages below 2 years and for bedridden patients<italicout>
  • <italicin>Height  – For ages 2 year and above <italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>MUAC  – For age ≥6 months<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>BMI      – For ages above 5 year <italicout>
<boldin>HEENT<boldout>
<boldin>Head<boldout>
<boldin>• Moon face (Kwashiorkor)<boldout>
<boldin>• Simian face (Marasmus)<boldout>
<boldin>• Hair<boldout>
  • Color – flag sign (alternating bands of light and normal color)
<img:ch12_t01_03.jpg>
<boldin>• Distribution – sparse<boldout>
<boldin>• Alopecia<boldout>
<boldin>• Pluckability or brittlity<boldout>
<boldin>• Broomstick eyelashes<boldout>
<boldin>• Craniotabes<boldout>
<boldin>• Caput quadratum<boldout>
<boldin>• Frontal bossing<boldout>
  • Fontanel size, closure /& surface                flag sign
<boldin>• Sutures – closed or not<boldout>
<boldin>Eye<boldout>
  • Sunkening of eyeball – due to loss of retroorbital fat
<boldin>• Pale conjunctiva<boldout>
<boldin>• Bitot’s spot<boldout>
<boldin>• Sclera – icteric or non-icteric<boldout>
<boldin>• Periorbital edema<boldout>
<boldin>• Discharge<boldout>
<boldin>Mouth and throat<boldout>
<boldin>• Buccal mucosa (pink, pale, wet, dry)<boldout>
<boldin>• Tongue atrophy<boldout>
<boldin>• Angular cheilities/ stomatitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Dentition – delayed eruption<boldout>
<boldin>• Gum bleeding /& swelling<boldout>
<boldin>• Oral ulcers, OHL, candidiasis<boldout>
<boldin>Respiratory system<boldout>
<boldin>• Costochondral beading<boldout>
<boldin>• Harrison groove<boldout>
<boldin>• Pigeon chest deformity<boldout>
<boldin>• Visible ribs<boldout>
<boldin>• Crepitation ,decreased air entry<boldout>
<boldin>• Clubbing<boldout>
<boldin>Cardiovascular system (CVS)<boldout>
<boldin>• Pounding pulse<boldout>
<boldin>• S3 gallop<boldout>
<boldin>• Distended vein<boldout>
<boldin>• Cold extremities<boldout>
<boldin>Abdomen<boldout>
  • Protruded abdomen (Pot belly appearance) – due to weakened abdominal muscles and gas from bacterial overgrowth of the upper gut may lead to a distended abdomen
  • Hepatomegaly – due to severe fatty infiltration (fatty liver)
<boldin>• Distension<boldout>
<boldin>• Ascites<boldout>
<boldin>Integumentary system<boldout>
<boldin>• Palmar and plantar pallor<boldout>
  • Skin - Wet, dry, pink or pale
<boldin>• Warm or cold extremities<boldout>
<boldin>• Loose and wrinkled skin (Marasmus)<boldout>
<boldin>• Shiny skin (Kwashiorkor)<boldout>
<boldin>• Skin lesions (nutritional dermatosis)<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyper or hypo pigmentation         Nutritional dermatosis<boldout>
  • Often involves the limbs, thighs, perineum, groin, and/or behind the ears
<boldin>• Has three grades:<boldout>
  • Grade I (Mild) → Discoloration or a few rough patches of skin
  • Grade II (Moderate) → Multiple patchy on arms and/or legs
  • Grade III (Severe) → Flaky paint appearance of skin (desquamation, fissures or ulceration)

<boldin>Musculoskeletal system<boldout>
  • Baggy pants – fold of skin over the buttocks
<boldin>• Wrist widening<boldout>
<boldin>• Double malleoli<boldout>
<boldin>• Bow leg<boldout>
<boldin>• Joint swelling                                                    Baggy pants<boldout>
  • Pitting leg or sacral edema - Grading See @ “edema”
<boldin>Central nervous system (CNS)<boldout>
<boldin>• Mental status<boldout>
<boldin>• Mentation – Conscious, lethargic, comatose<boldout>
<boldin>• Irritability<boldout>
<boldin>• Expression of misery and sadness<boldout>
<boldin>• Global developmental delay<boldout>
<boldin>Admission criteria to Stabilization center <boldout>
<boldin>For age 0-6 months<boldout>
• W/L/< -3 z score       <boldin>OR<boldout>
<boldin>• Presence of bilateral pitting edema<boldout>
<boldin>For age 6-59 months<boldout>
  • Grade III (<boldin>+++<boldout>) Bilateral pitting edema       <boldin>OR<boldout>
  • Any grade of bilateral pitting edema combined with severe wasting (WFL/H /< -3 Z score or MUAC /< 11.5 cm)         <boldin>OR<boldout>
  • Referred from the outpatient care according to the action protocol     <boldin>OR<boldout>
  • MUAC /< 11.5 cm       or               <boldin>Plus<boldout> any one of medical
  • WFL/H /< -3 Z score   or               complications or failed
  • Bilateral pitting edema <boldin>+, ++<boldout>        appetite test
<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypoglycemia (RBS /< 54 mg/dl)<boldout>
<boldin>• Dehydration<boldout>
  • Severe anemia (Hb /< 4 g/dl) within 48 hours of admission
<boldin>• Fever (T /> 39 °c)<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypothermia (Tº/< 35 ºc)<boldout>
<boldin>• Heart failure<boldout>
  • Infection – Lower respiratory tract infections (pneumonia, TB ...), Urinary tract infection
<boldin>• Septic shock<boldout>
  • Electrolyte disturbance – due to diarrhea, vomiting, poor absorption or intake
<boldin>• Intractable vomiting<boldout>
<boldin>• Persistent diarrhea<boldout>
<boldin>• Skin lesion (nutritional dermatosis)<boldout>
<boldin>• Renal failure<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
<boldin>• Random blood sugar<boldout>
<boldin>• Complete blood count– Leukocytosis or leucopenia<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemoglobin – Anemia<boldout>
  • Blood film- To rule out malaria as the cause of anemia
  • Peripheral morphology – To know the underlying cause of anemia
  • Provider-initiated testing and counselling (PITC) – HIV/AIDS is one of the differential diagnosis
  • Stool microscopy – To rule out hookworm as the cause of anemia
  • Urine analysis and culture – Urinary tract infection
<boldin>• Chest X-ray – Pneumonia, tuberculosis …<boldout>
  • Serum albumin and total protein - Reduced in kwashiorkor patients
  • Renal function test - To rule out renal failure as a complication and has to be done with indication
  • Serum electrolytes: K+  decrease, Mg++ decrease and Na+  increase
<boldin>• Renal function test<boldout>
<boldin>Management principles<boldout>
<boldin>• Treat life threatening conditions<boldout>
<boldin>• Phasic feeding, starter F-75<boldout>
<boldin>• Antibiotics<boldout>
<boldin>• Routine medications<boldout>
<boldin>• Nutritional rehabilitations<boldout>
<boldin>• Follow up<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 13: Effective Breast Feeding
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner:<boldout>
  • Comment on breast feeding of this baby
  • What are the criteria to say effective breast feeding?
  • What are the indicators of adequate breast feeding?
  • What are the optimal breast feeding practices?
  • What are the advantages of breast feeding?
<boldin>Introduction<boldout>
  • Breast milk is the best food for the newborn
  • It should be started immediately after birth within one hour of life
  • It should be given exclusively for the first six month of life even water need not to be added
  • It contains all the nutrients required by the infant
<boldin>Effective Breast Feeding<boldout>
<boldin>For effective breast feeding, there should be:<boldout>
<boldin>• Proper positioning<boldout>
<boldin>• Proper attachment and<boldout>
<boldin>• Effective suckling<boldout>
<boldin>• Proper positioning<boldout>
<boldin>• Supporting babies whole body<boldout>
  • Neck and back on same plane with neck slightly extended
<boldin>• Babies entire body facing mother<boldout>
<boldin>• Babies abdomen touching mothers’ abdomen<boldout>
<boldin>• Proper attachment<boldout>
<boldin>• Baby’s mouth wide open<boldout>
<boldin>• Lower lip turned outwards<boldout>
<boldin>• Chin touching nipple<boldout>
  • More areola visible on above than below
<boldin>• Effective suckling<boldout>
  • Slow, deep suckling with pause in between
<boldin>Causes of poor attachment<boldout>
<boldin>• Use of feeding bottles<boldout>
<boldin>• Inexperienced mother<boldout>
<boldin>• Lack of skilled support<boldout>
<boldin>• Inverted nipples<boldout>
<boldin>Consequences of poor attachment<boldout>
<boldin>• Pain to nipple or sore nipple<boldout>
<boldin>• Breast engorgement<boldout>
  • Poor milk supply hence the baby is not satisfied after sucking
  • Baby refuse to suck and poor weight gain
<boldin>Indicators of adequate breast feeding<boldout>
  • Baby passes urine six to eight times per day
  • Sleep for two to three hours after feeds
<boldin>• Gains weight around 20-30 gm/day<boldout>
<boldin>• Crosses birth weight by two weeks<boldout>
<boldin>Optimal breast feeding practices <boldout>
  • Have written breast feeding policy that is routinely communicated to all health care staff
  • Counsel mother and discuss about feeding of the newborn during pregnancy
  • Start breast feeding within one hour of birth
<boldin>• Teach mother on breast feeding practices<boldout>
  • Exclusive breast feeding for the first four to six months
  • Breast feeding should be given eight to twelve times per day including night feeds
  • It should be on demand and frequent
  • Empty one breast before switching to the other
<boldin>• Increase breast feeding during illness<boldout>
  • Continue breast feeding for a total of 24 months
  • Start complementary feeding at six months of life
  • No supply or advertizing of breast milk substitutes to mothers always tell about priority of breast milk
<boldin>• Don’t give artificial teats or pacifiers<boldout>
<boldin>Advantages of breast feeding <boldout>
<boldin>• Increases mother to infant bonding<boldout>
<boldin>• Protects infants from infection<boldout>
  • Lactational amenorrhea for the mother means of family planning
  • If started immediately after birth facilitates uterine contraction expulsion of placenta and prevent postpartum hemorrhage (PPH)
  • Natural food for full-term infants and is the appropriate milk for the first year of life
  • Always available at the proper temperature and requires no preparation time
  • Associated with fewer feeding difficulties incident to allergy and/or intolerance to bovine milk which include diarrhea, intestinal bleeding, occult melena, colic, and atopic eczema
  • Supply all the necessary nutrients except fluoride, vitamin K and vitamin D
  • Since the vitamin K content is low it may contribute to hemorrhagic disease of the newborn
  • Iron content is low, but most normal term infants have sufficient iron stores for the first four to six months of life. Human milk iron is well absorbed.
<boldin>• Fresh and free of contaminating bacteria<boldout>
<boldin>Some contraindications for breast feeding <boldout>
<boldin>• Human T-lymphotropic virus (HTLV) infection<boldout>
  • Herpes  simplex virus (HSV) active lesion on the breast
  • In case of maternal human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection, follow countries policy for breast feeding

topics.title: Chapter 14: Anemia
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner:<boldout>
<boldin>• Asses for signs of Anemia   OR<boldout>
  • What do you see? (i.e. If the patient is grossly pale)
  • What could be the cause of anemia?
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
  • How do you differentiate iron deficiency anemia Vs anemia of chronic illness Vs…?
<boldin>• Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>Introduction <boldout>
  • The threshold for defining anemia is a hemoglobin or hematocrit that is more than two standard deviations below the mean for the reference population
  • Normal hemoglobin values vary according to age, gender, race, and method of testing, such as capillary vs venous blood
  • Anemia may be defined as a reduction in RBC mass or hemoglobin concentration

<boldin>Physiologic adjustments of anemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Increased cardiac output<boldout>
  • Reduction of mixed venous oxygen tension (increased oxygen extraction by hypoxic tissue)
  • Shunting of blood flow toward vital organs and tissue
  • Increased 2,3-diphosphoglycerate (DPG) in the red blood cells (RBCs) causes reduced oxygen affinity, shifting the hemoglobin-O2 dissociation curve to the right and thereby enhancing oxygen release to the tissues
<boldin>• Stimulation of erythropoietin production<boldout>
<boldin>Classifications<boldout>
<boldin>Physiologic classification<boldout>
<boldin>Decreased production<boldout>
  • Deficiency states- Iron deficiency, Vitamin B12 deficiency…
<boldin>• Bone marrow failure<boldout>
<boldin>• Dyshematopoietic anemia<boldout>
<boldin>Increased destruction<boldout>
  • Corpuscular - membrane defects, enzyme defects, hemoglobin defects
<boldin>• Extra corpuscular- isoimmune, autoimmune, idiopathic<boldout>
Blood loss – hemorrhage, poorly tied umbilicus, hook worm infection

<boldin>Morphologic classifications<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>(<boldout>based on Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) values)
<boldin>• Normocytic anemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Anemia of chronic disease (70%)<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron deficiency (early)<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemolytic anemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Malignancy<boldout>
<boldin>• Renal failure<boldout>
<boldin>• Acute bleeding<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperspleenism<boldout>
  • Microangiopathy- Hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS), Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP), Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
  • Enzymopathies – Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD), Pyruvate kinase (PK) deficiencies
<boldin>• Microcytic anemia (LIST-C2)<boldout>
<boldin>• Lead poisoning<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron deficiency anemia (late)<boldout>
<boldin>• Sideroblastic anemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Thalasemia syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Anemia of chronic disease (30%)<boldout>
<boldin>• Copper deficiency<boldout>
<boldin>• Macrocytic anemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Vitamin B12 deficiency<boldout>
<boldin>• Folate deficiency<boldout>
<boldin>• Drug toxicity → Zidovudine, Methotrexate<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypothyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>• Acquired aplastic anemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital aplastic anemia<boldout>
<boldin>Common causes of anemia<boldout>
<boldin>Micro-nutrient deficiencies<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron deficiency<boldout>
<boldin>• Folic deficiency<boldout>
<boldin>• Vitamin B12 deficiency<boldout>
<boldin>Infectious diseases<boldout>
<boldin>• HIV<boldout>
<boldin>• Hookworm<boldout>
<boldin>• Malaria<boldout>
<boldin>• Trichuris trichuria<boldout>
<boldin>• Visceral leishmaniasis<boldout>
<boldin>• Schistosomiasis<boldout>
<boldin>Blood loss<boldout>
<boldin>Malignancy and chronic illness<boldout>
<boldin>Patient Evaluation<boldout>
<boldin>History<boldout>
<boldin>• Age and sex<boldout>
<boldin>• Perinatal history (prematurity)<boldout>
<boldin>• Dietary history<boldout>
  • Blood loss – acute or chronic blood loss
<boldin>• Underlying disease – malignancy, chronic illness<boldout>
<boldin>• Exposure to drugs<boldout>
<boldin>• Family history, Race<boldout>
<boldin>• Geographical location (residency), Travel history (malaria)<boldout>
<boldin>• Infection (hook worm, malaria, HIV, …)<boldout>
<boldin>• Symptoms of anemia and underlying disease<boldout>
  • Easy fatigability, tinnitus, vertigo, blurring of vision, …
<boldin>Physical examination<boldout>
<boldin>General appearance<boldout>
<boldin>• Nutritional status<boldout>
<boldin>Vital signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachycardia<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachypnea<boldout>
<boldin>• Postural hypotension<boldout>
<boldin>• Wide pulse pressure<boldout>
<boldin>HEENT<boldout>
<boldin>• Pale conjunctivae<boldout>
<boldin>• Icteric sclera (hemolysis)<boldout>
<boldin>• Mucosal pallor<boldout>
<boldin>• Angular stomatitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Atrophy of tongue papillae<boldout>
<boldin>• Glossitis<boldout>
<boldin>LGS<boldout>
  • Lymphadenopathy – malignancy, visceral leishmaniosis… as a cause of anemia
<boldin>CVS<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachycardia<boldout>
<boldin>• Bounding pulse<boldout>
<boldin>• S3 gallop<boldout>
<boldin>• Systolic ejection murmur<boldout>
<boldin>Abdomen<boldout>
  • Organomegaly – to find out the cause
  • Per rectal (PR) examination – occult blood
<boldin>Musculoskeletal system<boldout>
  • Bone tenderness – to find out the cause
  • Edema – congestive heart failure as a complication
<boldin>Integumentary<boldout>
<boldin>• Palmar pallor and palmar crease involvement<boldout>
<boldin>• Skin pallor<boldout>
<boldin>• Koilonychias – spooning of nails<boldout>
<boldin>• Petechiae, bruising, purpura<boldout>
<boldin>• Ulcers on lower extremities<boldout>
<boldin>CNS<boldout>
<boldin>• Mental status<boldout>
<boldin>• Fundoscopy for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Retinal hemorrhage (severe anemia, malaria)<boldout>
<boldin>• Optic atrophy (cobalamine deficiency)<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
<boldin>• Complete blood count:<boldout>
<boldin>• Red blood cell count<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemoglobin/hematocrit<boldout>
  • Red blood cell indices (MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW)
<boldin>• Reticulocyte count<boldout>
  • White blood cell (WBC) count with differential
<boldin>• Platelet count<boldout>
<boldin>• Peripheral smear:<boldout>
<boldin>• Size, Shape<boldout>
<boldin>• Chromicity of RBCs<boldout>
<boldin>• Rouloux formation<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypersegmentation of neutrophils<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron studies:<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum iron<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum transferrin<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum ferritin<boldout>
<boldin>• Total iron binding capacity (TIBC)<boldout>
<boldin>• Red cell enzyme studies<boldout>
<boldin>• Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G-6-PD)<boldout>
<boldin>• Pyruvate kinase<boldout>
<boldin>• Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy:<boldout>
<boldin>• Cellularity<boldout>
<boldin>• Red cell appearance<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron stain<boldout>
<boldin>• Culture<boldout>
<boldin>• Cytogenic studies<boldout>
  • RBC folate, Vitamin B12 and serum/urine methylmalonic acid
<boldin>• Bilirubin level/LDH - Hemolytic anemia<boldout>
  • Direct antiglobulin or Coombs test - Autoimmune hemolytic anemia
<boldin>• Hemoglobin electrophoresis – Hemoglobinopathies<boldout>
<boldin>• Osmotic fragility – Spherocytosis<boldout>
  • Blood typing and cross matching to assess possible isoimmune anemia in a neonate and to prepare for transfusion
  • BUN and creatinine levels - To assess renal function
<boldin>• Thyroxin (T4) or TSH – Hypothyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>• Stool microscopy<boldout>
<boldin>• Blood film<boldout>
<boldin>Note:<boldout>
  • Buccal mucosa, conjunctiva, palms and sole are the commonly used sites to check anemia because the vessels are superficial in these areas.
  • Palmar pallor usually noticed when hemoglobin level reaches to 7-8 g/dl
  • If it involves only the palm = Some palmar pallor
  • If it involves the palmar crease = Severe palmar pallor
<boldin>When we say severe anemia?<boldout>
  • Hb /< 7 g/dl or Hct /< 21%
  • Malnourished patients: Hb /< 4 g/dl or Hct /< 12%
  • Malaria: Hb /< 5 g/dl or Hct /< 15%
<boldin>WHO grading of anemia<boldout>
  • Mild = Hb: 10 g/dl -cutoff point
<boldin>• Moderate =Hgb:7-10 g/dl<boldout>
<boldin>• Severe =Hb: less than 7g/dl<boldout>
<boldin>Clinical grading<boldout>
  • Mild = Conjunctival pallor and/or mucous membrane
<boldin>• Moderate = Obvious skin pallor<boldout>
<boldin>• Severe = Palmar crease pallor<boldout>
<boldin>Management Principles<boldout>
<boldin>Supportive<boldout>
<boldin>• ABC of life<boldout>
<boldin>• Blood transfusion with indication<boldout>
<boldin>• Oxygen<boldout>
<boldin>• Bed rest<boldout>
<boldin>Specific<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron<boldout>
<boldin>• Folate and cobalamin<boldout>
<boldin>• Corticosteroids<boldout>
<boldin>• Recombinant erythropoietin<boldout>
<boldin>• Hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT)<boldout>
<boldin>• Immunosuppression<boldout>
<boldin>Key words<boldout>
Hematocrit (Hct)Fractional volume of whole blood sample occupied by RBC, expressed as percent
Hemoglobin (Hb)Measure of a concentration of the RBC pigment. Hb in whole blood, expressed as grams per 100 ml (dl) of whole blood
<boldin>Mean corpuscular concentration (MCV):<boldout>
  • The mean value of the volume of individual RBCs in the blood sample
<boldin>• Values vary with age<boldout>
<boldin>• Microcytic, normocytic or macrocytic<boldout>
<boldin>Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC):<boldout>
  • A calculated index (MCHC = Hb/Hct), yielding a value of grams of Hb per 100 ml of RBC
<boldin>• Values vary with age<boldout>
<boldin>• Normochromic or hypochromic<boldout>
<boldin>Red cell distribution width (RDW):<boldout>
  • A quantitative measure of the variability of RBC sizes in the sample (anisocytosis)
  • Generally normal value is 12–14%, may slightly vary with age
  • Helpful in differentiating iron deficiency anemia from thalassemia in microcytic anemia
  • Red cell distribution width (RDW) /> 20 is more likely iron deficiency anemia

topics.title: Chapter 15: Iron Deficiency Anemia
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Introduction<boldout>
  • Iron Deficiency Anemia (IDA) is the most common nutritional deficiency in children
<boldin>• Prevalence is higher in developing countries<boldout>
  • The development /& the rapidity of IDA is dependent upon the body’s iron store
  • Iron store in turn depends on: age, sex, rate of growth and balance between loss and absorption
  • Peak prevalence occurs during late infancy and early childhood when the following may occur
  • Rapid growth with exhaustion of gestational iron
<boldin>• Low levels of dietary iron<boldout>
  • Complicating effect of cow milk-induced exudative enteropathy

<boldin>Iron metabolism<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron is available in the body as:<boldout>
  • Functional iron - hemoglobin, myoglobin, cytochromes and catalase
<boldin>• Transport iron – transferrin<boldout>
<boldin>• Storage iron –ferritin and hemosiderin<boldout>
  • Dietary requirement of iron is about 8-10 g/dl/day of which 10% will be absorbed
  • Dietary iron absorption occurs in proximal duodenum
  • Intestinal iron absorption is a function of 3 principal factors
<boldin>• Body iron stores (transferrin and ferritin)<boldout>
<boldin>• Erythropoietic rate<boldout>
<boldin>• Bioavailability of dietary iron<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron absorption is;<boldout>
  • Facilitated by - citrates and ascorbic acid
  • Inhibited by - phytates, tannates, oxalates, phosphates, sulphates and antacids
<boldin>• Dietary iron exists in two states:<boldout>
  • Heme (10%) – found in animal proteins
  • Nonheme (90%) – vegetarians, found in the form of ferric state

<boldin>Causes of <boldout><boldin>IDA<boldout>
<boldin>Increased demand for iron and/or hematopoiesis<boldout>
<boldin>• Rapid growth in infancy or adolescence<boldout>
<boldin>• Pregnancy<boldout>
<boldin>• Erythropoietin therapy<boldout>
<boldin>Increased iron loss<boldout>
<boldin>• Chronic blood loss<boldout>
<boldin>• Menses<boldout>
<boldin>• Acute blood loss<boldout>
<boldin>• Phlebotomy as treatment for polycythemia vera<boldout>
<boldin>• Blood donation<boldout>
<boldin>Decreased iron intake or absorption<boldout>
<boldin>• Inadequate diet<boldout>
<boldin>• Malabsorption from disease (sprue, Crohn/'s disease)<boldout>
<boldin>• Malabsorption from surgery (post- gastrectomy)<boldout>
<boldin>• Unmodified cow milk<boldout>
In the first 3 – 6 months of life
<boldin>• Maternal iron deficiency<boldout>
<boldin>• Prematurity<boldout>
  • Administration of erythropoietin (EPO) for anemia of prematurity
<boldin>• Fetal – maternal hemorrhage<boldout>
<boldin>• Twin – twin transfusion syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Other Perinatal hemorrhagic events<boldout>
<boldin>Clinical Manifestations<boldout>
<boldin>Symptoms<boldout>
<boldin>• Anorexia<boldout>
  • Pica – the desire to ingest nonnutritive substances
  • Pagophagia – the desire to ingest ice
<boldin>• Fatigue<boldout>
  • Irritability when Hb level /< 5 gm/dl
  • Impaired psychomotor and/ mental development in infants
<boldin>• Cognitive impairment in adolescents<boldout>
<boldin>• Poor development<boldout>
<boldin>• Thrombosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Impaired exercise performance<boldout>
<boldin>Signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Pallor<boldout>
<boldin>• Blue sclera<boldout>
<boldin>• Angular cheilities<boldout>
<boldin>• Atrophic glossitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Koilonychias (spooning of nails)<boldout>
  • When the hemoglobin level falls below 5g/dl
<boldin>• Irritability and anorexia are prominent<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachycardia and cardiac dilation occur<boldout>
<boldin>• Systolic murmurs are often present<boldout>
  • Plummer–Vinson syndrome: Characterized by the combination of IDA, esophageal web and dysphagia

<boldin>Stages in the development of <boldout><boldin>IDA<boldout>
<boldin>• Depletion of iron stores<boldout>
<boldin>• Decreased level of serum ferritin<boldout>
  • Normal level of serum iron, total iron binding capacity (TIBC), % saturation and red blood cell (RBC) protoporphyrin level
<boldin>• Patient is asymptomatic<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron deficient erythropoiesis<boldout>
<boldin>• Decreased level of serum ferritin<boldout>
<boldin>• Reduced serum iron and % saturation<boldout>
<boldin>• Increased TIBC and RBC protoporphyrin level<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum transferrin receptor levels increases<boldout>
<boldin>• No change in hematocrit level<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron deficiency anemia<boldout>
  • Microcytic, hypochromic and aniso-poikilocytosis: cigar and pencil shaped RBCs
<boldin>• Increased RDW<boldout>
  • Elevated levels of Free erythrocyte protoporphyrin (FEP)
<boldin>Investigations <boldout>
<boldin>• Complete blood count<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemoglobin – decreased<boldout>
