<boldin>• RBC count – decreased<boldout>
<boldin>• Red cell distribution width (RDW)– increased<boldout>
<boldin>• Peripheral smear, RBCs are<boldout>
<boldin>• Small (microcytic)<boldout>
<boldin>• Pale (hypochromic)<boldout>
  • Poikilocytosis in the form of small elongated red cells (pencil cells) and anisocytosis

<boldin>• Iron studies<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum iron – decreased<boldout>
<boldin>• Total iron binding capacity – increased<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum ferritin – decreased<boldout>
<boldin>• Iron saturation – decreased<boldout>
<boldin>• Free erythrocyte protoporphyrin – elevated<boldout>
  • Bone marrow iron stain (<italicin>Prussian blue<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>stain)<italicout>
  • The disappearance of stainable iron from mononuclear phagocytic cells is a diagnostic finding
  • The most accurate method of diagnosing iron deficiency anemia but is invasive and expensive and usually unnecessary
  • Others investigations are based on the causes of IDA and differential diagnosis
<img:t16.png>
<italicin>*Taken from Nelson text book of pediatric 21st edition*  <italicout>
<boldin>Management Principle<boldout>
<boldin>Oral supplement<boldout>
  • A daily total dose of 3-6 mg/kg of elemental iron in one or two doses is adequate for 6–8 weeks after hemoglobin level and the RBC indices return to normal
  • For a total of at least 3 months
  • Ferrous sulfate is 20% elemental iron by weight and is ideally given between meals with vitamin C–containing juice
  • As effective, much less expensive and less toxic than parenteral iron
  • Tea is a significant inhibitor of iron absorption, try to avoid it
<boldin>Parenteral iron therapy; indications:<boldout>
<boldin>• Unable to tolerate oral iron<boldout>
  • Who need iron on an ongoing basis, usually due to persistent GI blood loss
<boldin>• Chronic hemorrhage<boldout>
<boldin>• Malabsorption<boldout>
<boldin>• Refractoriness to oral therapy<boldout>
<boldin>• Patient unwilling to take oral therapy<boldout>
<boldin>Blood transfusion<boldout>
  • When the anemia is severe or decompensated
<boldin>• Cardiovascular instability<boldout>
<boldin>• Continued and excessive blood loss<boldout>
<boldin>• Patients requiring immediate intervention<boldout>
<img:t17.png>
<boldin>Prevention<boldout>
<boldin>• Appropriate nutrition<boldout>
<boldin>• Health education<boldout>
<boldin>• Supplementation for those with increased demand<boldout>
<boldin>• Fortification of formulas, cereals…<boldout>
<boldin>• Diagnose and treat underlying conditions<boldout>
<boldin>• Quality antenatal and neonatal care<boldout>
  • Breastfeeding should be encouraged, with the addition of supplemental iron at four month of age
  • In breastfed preterm or low birth weight infants, elemental iron supplementation starting at one month of age and is continued until twelfth months of age
  • Universal screening by the age of 9-12 months during infancy

topics.title: Chapter 16: Possible Questions By The Examiner:
topics.content_html:
<boldin>• Asses for signs of rickets<boldout>
  • Other possible differential diagnosis for your findings
<boldin>• Causes/Risk factors for rickets<boldout>
<boldin>• What are complications of rickets?<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
<boldin>• Management principle<boldout>
<boldin>• Prevention mechanism<boldout>
<boldin>Introduction <boldout>
  • It is a disease of growing bone that is due to unmineralized matrix at the growth plates and occurs in children only before fusion of the epiphyses
  • Because growth plate cartilage and osteoid continue to expand but mineralization is inadequate, the growth plate thickens

<boldin>Question: Asses for signs of rickets <boldout>
<boldin>Look for:<boldout>
<boldin>• Craniotabes<boldout>
<boldin>• Frontal bossing<boldout>
<boldin>• Caput quadratum<boldout>
<boldin>• Fontanel size /& closure<boldout>
<boldin>• Dentition<boldout>
<boldin>• Rachitic rosary<boldout>
<boldin>• Harrison groove<boldout>
<boldin>• Pigeon chest deformity<boldout>
<boldin>• Protruding abdomen<boldout>
<boldin>• Wrist widening<boldout>
<boldin>• Double malleoli<boldout>
<boldin>• Bowlegs of knock knees<boldout>
<boldin>Causes<boldout>
<boldin>• Poor sun light exposure<boldout>
  • Skin pigmentation – will lead to poor ultra violet (UV) light absorption
<boldin>• Inadequate dietary intake<boldout>
<boldin>• Breast feeding – contains low calcium<boldout>
<boldin>• Malabsorption of vitamin D from intestine<boldout>
  • Defects in the metabolism of vitamin D
<boldin>What is adequate sunlight exposure?<boldout>
  • Expose in the morning with in 4:00 – 8:00 local time
<boldin>• For 20 – 30 minutes<boldout>
<boldin>• No ointment applied<boldout>
  • Expose all body parts except the eyes and genitalia
  • Starting from birth till he/she starts to ambulate. But we may say after 10 days as the mother can’t go out alone for the first 10 days in our culture (Ethiopian).
<boldin>• Daily exposure if possible<boldout>
<boldin>• Direct sunlight exposure<boldout>

<boldin>Clinical Features<boldout>
Usually appears towards the end of first year and during the second year of life, but
<boldin>• Mother with vitamin D deficiency<boldout>
  • Prematures and	                   <italicin>manifest as early as 2<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>months<italicout>
<boldin>• Infants on drugs like phenytoin<boldout>
<boldin>General<boldout>
<boldin>• Failure to thrive, Listlessness<boldout>
<boldin>• Protruding abdomen<boldout>
<boldin>• Muscle weakness<boldout>
<boldin>• Fracture<boldout>
<boldin>Head<boldout>
<boldin>• Craniotabes<boldout>
<boldin>• The first sign of rickets<boldout>
<boldin>• Softening of the cranial bones<boldout>
  • Detected by applying pressure at the temporoparietal or parietoccipital areas of the scalp
  • Sensation is similar to the feel of pressing into a Ping-Pong ball and then releasing
  • Frontal bossing - protuberant enlargement of the frontal bone
<boldin>• Caput quadratum – box shaped head<boldout>
<boldin>• Wide fontanel<boldout>
  • Delayed anterior fontanel closure (/> 2 years)
  • Delayed dentition - no single tooth at 13 months of age
<boldin>Chest<boldout>
<boldin>• Rachitic rosary (costochondral beading)<boldout>
<boldin>• Widening of the costochondral junctions<boldout>
  • Feels like the beads of a rosary as the examiner/'s fingers move along the costochondral junctions from rib to rib
  • Non tender and blunted, whereas scurvy is tender /& sharp
<boldin>• Harrison groove<boldout>
  • The horizontal depression along the lower anterior chest
  • Due to pulling of the softened ribs by the diaphragm during inspiration
<boldin>• Pigeon chest deformity
<img:ch16_t01_04.png><boldout>
<boldin>Abdomen<boldout>
<boldin>• Protruding abdomen<boldout>
(Potbelly abdomen)


<boldin>Potbelly abdomen and Harrison’s groove<boldout>
<boldin>Back<boldout>
<boldin>• Scoliosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Kyphosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Lordosis<boldout>
<boldin>Extremities<boldout><boldin>                                             <boldout>
<img:ch16_t01_05.png>
<boldin>• Enlargement of the wrists and ankles<boldout>
<boldin>• Due to growth plate widening<boldout>
<boldin>• Deformity of the pelvis<boldout>
<boldin>• Bowlegs of knock knees<boldout>
<boldin>• Double malleoli                                              Bowlegs of knock knees<boldout>
<boldin>• Greenstick fractures<boldout>
<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Bone deformity<boldout>
<boldin>• Fracture<boldout>
<boldin>• Infection – recurrent pneumonia<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypocalcaemia with tetany<boldout>
<boldin>• Corpulmonale<boldout>
<boldin>• Laryngeal spasm<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout><boldin>	<boldout><boldin>	<boldout><boldin>	<boldout><boldin>	<boldout><boldin>	<boldout><boldin>	<boldout>
  • Wrist x-ray→ Widening, cupping and fraying at the end of radius and ulna
  • Serum Ca level → Normal or low
<boldin>• Serum phosphorus level → Low<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum alkaline phosphatase → Universally elevated<boldout>
<boldin>• Urinary cAMP level → Elevated<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum 25-hydroxycholecalciferol → Low<boldout>
  • CBC – To screen for infections and concomitant anemia
  • CXR - To rule out complications: pneumonia, PTB…
<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout><boldin> <boldout>
<boldin>See at HEENT<boldout>
<boldin>Costochondral beading<boldout>
<boldin>• Rickets<boldout>
<boldin>• Scurvy<boldout>
<boldin>• Cytomegalic inclusion bodies<boldout>
<boldin>• Chondrodystrophy<boldout>
<boldin>• Copper deficiency<boldout>
<boldin>• Rubella, Syphilis                                   Costochondral beading<boldout>
<boldin>Pigeon shaped chest<boldout>
<boldin>• Rickets<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital<boldout>
<boldin>• Skeletal dysplasia<boldout>
<boldin>• Emphysema<boldout>
<boldin>• Marfan’s syndrome, Noonan syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>Management<boldout>
<boldin>• Vitamin D administration:<boldout>
  • Stoss dose of vitamin D 300,000 - 600,000 IU IM in two to four doses over one day (better) OR
  • Daily intake of 2000-5000 IU of Vit D for 4 to 6 weeks
  • Both should be accompanied by adequate administration of Calcium and Phosphorous
  • After treatment continue with the daily requirement (i.e. 400 IU of vitamin D daily for infants and 600 IU for children)
  • Have bilateral wrist x-ray before treatment and repeat it by three to four weeks after treatment

<boldin>Prevention<boldout>
<boldin>• Adequate sunlight exposure<boldout>
  • Universal administration of a daily multivitamin containing 400 IU of vitamin D to infants who are breast-fed and 600 IU for age greater than one year

topics.title: Chapter 17: Dehydration
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner:<boldout>
  • Assess for signs of dehydration (DHN) <boldin>or<boldout>
  • Evaluate this baby and tell me your finding
<boldin>• How do you classify dehydration?<boldout>
  • How do you assess dehydration in severe acute malnutrition patients?
  • How do you manage patients with dehydration?
<boldin>Introduction<boldout>
  • Dehydration refers to a deficit of total body water with an accompanying disruption of metabolic process
  • It occurs when free water loss exceeds free water intake
  • We should always consider the nutritional status of the patient before evaluating the patient for dehydration (i.e. we should identify weather the patient is well-nourished or malnourished)
  • Dehydration may be due to; Ongoing fluid loss, Third space fluid loss, Inadequate fluid intake, Defective intestinal absorption or Increased insensible loss
  • Nutritional status of the patient will affect our physical examination and management

<boldin>Clinical manifestations in well-nourished patients<boldout>
<boldin>Symptoms<boldout>
<boldin>• Thirst<boldout>
<boldin>• Restlessness<boldout>
<boldin>• Irritability<boldout>
<boldin>• Sunken eye ball<boldout>
<boldin>• Diminished level of consciousness<boldout>
<boldin>• Decreased urine volume and frequency<boldout>
<boldin>Signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Sunken eye ball<boldout>
<boldin>• Sunken fontanel(infants)<boldout>
<boldin>• Dry mucosal membrane<boldout>
<boldin>• Decreased skin turgor<boldout>
<boldin>• Low urine output<boldout>
<boldin>• Cool extremities<boldout>
<boldin>• Rapid and feeble pulse<boldout>
<boldin>Question: Asses for signs of dehydration<boldout>
Assess for signs of the following four important parameters
<boldin>• Mental status<boldout>
<boldin>• Eye ball<boldout>
<boldin>• Drinking ability<boldout>
<boldin>• Skin turgor<boldout>
<boldin>Classification<boldout>
<boldin>• No dehydration<boldout>
  • Some dehydration      	<italicin>≥2 of the above parameter signs are<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>needed<italicout>
<boldin>• Severe dehydration<boldout>
<img:t18.png>
<boldin>Skin<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>turgor<boldout>
  • Locate a site half way between the umbilicus and the sides of the abdomen
  • Use your thumb and index finger but avoid holding by the finger tips since it will be painful
  • Pinch the skin in a longitudinal (head to foot) direction
  • Pick up all layers of the skin and tissues underneath
  • Pinch for one second and release. If returns
<boldin>• Quickly 		– Skin pinch returns fast<boldout>
  • Within 2 seconds 	– Skin pinch returns slowly
  • Longer than 2 seconds – Skin pinch returns very slowly

<boldin>Sunken eyeball<boldout>
  • Ask the mother if the child’s eyes are sunken than the usual or if the skin around them are darker than the usual
  • Look at the eyeballs if unusually sunken in the socket
<boldin>How can we asses Dehydration in severe acute malnutrition (<boldout><boldin>SAM) <boldout><boldin>patients?<boldout>
  • Diagnosis is mainly based on the history
<boldin>• Ask the mother if the child has:<boldout>
  • History of recent fluid loss: Watery diarrhea/ vomiting, or recent increment in frequency/volume of fluid lost
  • History of recent change in child’s appearance: Irritability, sleepiness, loss of consciousness …
  • History of recent sunkening of the eyeball
  • No classification of dehydration in malnourished patients
<boldin><italicin>Note:<italicout><boldout>
<italicin>When a patient presented with diarrhea of more than two weeks with No signs of <italicout><italicin>dehydration<italicout><italicin> is <italicout><italicin>called <italicout><italicin>Persistent diarrhea and if it is with Signs of <italicout><italicin>dehydration is considered as <italicout><italicin>severe persistent diarrhea.<italicout>
<boldin>Why physical signs are not reliable to assess the degree of <boldout><boldin>dehydration <boldout><boldin>in malnutrition?<boldout>
  • Kwashiorkor patients will be <boldin>falsely<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>negative<boldout>. Skin turgor will be intact in the presence of dehydration due to the edema which accumulated in the subcutaneous tissue
  • Marasmic patients will be<boldin> falsely positive<boldout>. They already lost their protein, retro-orbital fat and subcutaneous fat which leads to sunkening of eyeball and slow return of skin pinch. Marasmic patients will have all signs of dehydration in the absence of dehydration.

<boldin>Management principles<boldout>
<boldin>Management of dehydration for well-nourished patients<boldout>
• <boldin>No Dehydration (plan<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>A)<boldout>
  • Loss is estimated to be /< 5% of body weight
<boldin>• Treat diarrhea at home<boldout>
<boldin>• Rules of 3F’S<boldout>
• Give extra <boldin>Fluid<boldout>
• Continue <boldin>Feeding<boldout>
• When to come for <boldin>Follow<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>up<boldout>
<boldin>• Supplementation of Zink<boldout>
<boldin>Fluid<boldout>: In addition to the usual fluid intake give:
  • More fluids than usual – Breast milk, food based fluids (soup, rise water, yoghurt) or clean water
<boldin>• Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS)<boldout>
  • 10 ml/kg	OR
  • 50-100 ml for those /< 2 years	       <italicin>per each bowl<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>motion<italicout>
  • 100-200 ml for those /> 2 years
<boldin>Feeding<boldout>
  • Depends on age, food preference, pre illness feeding pattern
<boldin>• Frequent small feeding is better tolerated<boldout>
  • Frequent breast feeding, cow’s milk or formula milk
<boldin>• Continue other foods if he/she started<boldout>
<boldin>Zink<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>supplementation<boldout>
  • 10 mg per day for infants /< 6 months of age and 20 mg per day for those age  ≥ 6 months for 10–14 days
  • Increase use of ORS and reduce inappropriate use of antimicrobials
<boldin>• Reduce duration and severity of diarrhea<boldout>
<boldin>Follow up<boldout>
<boldin>• See him in two days<boldout>
  • Advice on danger signs and come back immediately if the child
<boldin>• Starts to pass many watery stools<boldout>
<boldin>• Has repeated vomiting<boldout>
<boldin>• Eating and drinking poorly<boldout>
<boldin>• Develops a fever<boldout>
<boldin>• Has blood in the stool or<boldout>
• <boldin>Some Dehydration (Plan<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>B)<boldout>
  • Loss is estimated to be 5–10 % of body weight
<boldin>• Treat with ORS<boldout>
  • 75 ml/kg over four hours
<boldin>• Continue breast feeding<boldout>
<boldin>• If vomiting, wait for ten minutes<boldout>
<boldin>• Give zink based on age<boldout>
  • Asses frequently at least every hour for improvement or worsening and corrective measures should be taken accordingly
  • After four hours, reassess and classify dehydration
  • If no dehydration → send home the child with ORS packet for two days

• <boldin>Severe<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>Dehydration<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>(Plan<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>C)<boldout>
  • Loss is estimated to be />10% of body weight
<boldin>• Start intravenous (IV) fluid immediately<boldout>
  • If the patient can drink, give ORS by mouth till the drip is setup
  • Volume is 100ml/kg ringer lactate (RL) or normal saline (NS); divide in to two doses (30 ml/kg and 70 ml/kg).

<boldin>For infants (/< 12 months old)<boldout>
  • First give 30 ml/kg over 1 hour
  • Then give 70 ml/kg over 5 hours
<boldin>For children (≥ 12 months old)<boldout>
  • First give 30 ml/kg over 30 minutes
  • Then give 70 ml/kg over 2 ½ hours
<boldin>Monitoring<boldout>
  • If there is no response for the first bolus, repeat the bolus that should not be subtracted from the remaining 70ml/kg
  • Reassess the patient every one to two hours
  • After six hours (infant) or three hours (older) evaluate the patient and choose appropriate treatment plan (A, B or C)
  • If signs of severe dehydration are still present, repeat IV infusion as a child in treatment plan C
  • If still shows signs of some dehydration, discontinue the IV infusion and give ORS solution for four hours, as specified in Treatment Plan B
  • If no signs of dehydration; follow treatment plan A and observe the child for 6 hours before discharge

<boldin>Management of dehydration with <boldout><boldin>SAM<boldout>
<boldin>• For conscious and no signs of shock, we give Rehydration Solution for Malnutrition (ReSoMal) solution:<boldout>
  • 5 ml/kg every 30 minutes for the first 2 hours then
  • 5-10 ml/kg every hour for up to 10 hours
  • For unconscious child with no signs of shock, we give IV fluid
<boldin>• Ringer lactate with 5% glucose OR<boldout>
  • 45% NS with 5% glucose at 15 ml/kg for the first hour and reassess
  • If improving → give 15 ml/kg for the 2nd hour
  • If conscious → give ReSoMal by NG tube
<boldin>• If not improving → Septic shock<boldout>
  • Only rehydrate until the weight deficit is corrected
<boldin>• After rehydration<boldout>
<boldin>• Non-edematous children<boldout>
  • Less than two (/<2) years → Give 50-100 ml after each watery stool
  • Two (2) years and older → Give 100-200 ml after each watery stool
  • Edematous children → Give 30 ml after each watery stool
  • The most important determining factor for duration of rehydration in SAM patients is target weight of 3-5% from baseline so that ReSoMal for 10 hours always is not a rule.

<boldin>What is ReSoMal?<boldout>
  • ReSoMal is a rehydration solution for children with SAM
<boldin>• ReSoMal = Rehydration solution for malnutrition<boldout>
  • Contains less sodium, more sugar and more potassium than standard ORS
  • Is intended for severely malnourished children with diarrhea
  • It should be given by mouth or by NG tube
<boldin>Contents<boldout>
<boldin>• Water = 2L<boldout>
<boldin>• WHO ORS = one (1-litre) packet<boldout>
  • Mineral mix solution = 40 ml or one leveled scoop combined minerals and vitamins (CMV) or 2400mg (4 tab) KCl
<boldin>• Sugar = 50 gram<boldout>
<boldin>Preparation<boldout>
<boldin>• Wash hands<boldout>
  • Empty one 1-litre standard ORS packet into container that holds more than 2 liters
  • Measure and add 50 grams of sugar. (It is best to weigh the sugar on a dietary scale that weighs to 5 g.)
  • Measure 40 milliliters or one leveled scoop of CMV in a graduated medicine cup or syringe; add to other ingredients. In the absence of CMV add 4 tab (2400mg) KCl
  • Measure and add 2 liters of cooled boiled water
<boldin>• Stir until dissolved<boldout>
<boldin>• Use within 24 hours<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 18: Edema
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner<boldout>
<boldin>• Assess for signs of edema<boldout>
  • What is the Pathophysiology of edema formation?
<boldin>• Possible differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
<boldin>Introduction<boldout>
  • Edema is defined as a clinically apparent increase in the interstitial fluid volume or a swelling of tissues as a result of excess fluid accumulation
  • The following patho-physiologic processes result in edema formation
<boldin>• Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure<boldout>
<boldin>• Decreased capillary oncotic pressure<boldout>
<boldin>• Increase capillary permeability<boldout>
  • Impaired lymphatic transport - usually localized edema
Question = assess for signs of edema
<boldin>Sites/ locations<boldout>
  • Pedal	  – On bony prominence of dorsum of the foot
  • Ankle	  – On bony prominence of medial malleoli
  • Pretibial – Anteromedial shaft of the tibia, one-third below the tibial tuberosity
  • Sacral  	– For infants and bed ridden patients, on the sacral area
<boldin>Pitting Vs non pitting<boldout>
  • Gently compressing the area for at least 3 seconds with the thumb and release
  • If there is indentation, it is pitting edema
  • If there is no indentation, it is non pitting edema
<boldin>Grading of edema  <boldout>
<boldin>Based on location:<boldout>
For malnourished patients (Apply thumb pressure for 3 seconds and release)
  • Grade I (mild <boldin>+<boldout>)           → Both pedal / ankle
  • Grade II (moderate <boldin>++<boldout>) → Both feet + lower legs, hands or lower arm
  • Grade III (severe <boldin>+++<boldout>)  → Generalized bilateral pitting edema including both feet, legs, arms and face

<boldin>For well-nourished patients<boldout>
  • Grade I   → Pedal and pretibial edema
  • Grade II  → Leg and thigh edema
  • Grade III → Abdominal wall and sacral edema
<boldin>• Grade IV → Generalized body swelling<boldout>
<boldin>Based on time of refill <boldout>
By duration of refill of the indented skin (pitting edema)
  • Grade I    → Returns within 5 seconds
  • Grade II   → Returns within 5-10 seconds
  • Grade III  → Returns within 10-15 seconds
  • Grade IV  → Returns after 15 seconds
<boldin>Based on corresponding indenting depth<boldout>
<boldin>• Grade 0 → No edema<boldout>
<boldin>• Grade 1 → Barely discernible depression<boldout>
  • Grade 2 → Deeper depression (/<5 mm) accompanied by normal foot and leg contours
  • Grade 3 → Deep depression (5-10 mm) accompanied by foot and leg swelling
  • Grade 4 → Deeper depression (/>1 cm) accompanied by severe foot and leg swelling

<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout>- (age dependent)
<boldin>Pitting edema (Generalized body swelling)<boldout>
<boldin>• Kwashiorkor or edematous malnutrition<boldout>
<boldin>• Congestive heart failure
<img:ch18_t01_06.png><boldout>
<boldin>• Nephrotic syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Acute Glomerulonephritis<boldout>
<boldin>• Post Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Henoch-Schönlein purpura<boldout>
<boldin>• Tuberculosis pericarditis<boldout>
<boldin>• Protein losing enteropathy<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypoalbuminemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Fulminant hepatic failure<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital hepatic fibrosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Chronic kidney disease, Chronic liver disease<boldout>
<boldin>Non pitting edema<boldout>
<boldin>• Lymphatic obstruction<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
<boldin>• Random blood sugar<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum albumin, Total protein<boldout>
<boldin>• Urine analysis<boldout>
<boldin>• ECG, Echo, CXR<boldout>
<boldin>• Renal function test, Liver function test<boldout>
<boldin>• Viral markers<boldout>
<boldin>• Abdominal ultrasound<boldout>
<boldin>• Peritoneal fluid analysis<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 19: Possible Questions By The Examiner
topics.content_html:
<boldin>• Assess for Down syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Possible differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you diagnose Down syndrome?<boldout>
<boldin>• Complications of Down syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
<boldin>Introduction<boldout>
  • It is commonly due to meiotic nondisjunction resulting in Trisomy 21, translocation
<boldin>• The most common chromosomal anomaly<boldout>
  • The most common genetic etiology of moderate intellectual disability
  • The incidence in live births is approximately 1 in 733
  • Approximately 1% of persons with trisomy 21 are mosaics, with some cells having 46 chromosomes
  • The risk of having a child with trisomy 21 is highest in women who conceive after age of 35 year
  • Life expectancy for children with Down syndrome is reduced and is approximately 50-55 year
<boldin>• No racial predilection<boldout>
<boldin>Clinical features <boldout>
<boldin>Clinical features of Down syndrome is characterized by a variety of:<boldout>
<boldin>• Dysmorphic features<boldout>
<boldin>• Intellectual disability<boldout>
<boldin>• Heart diseases<boldout>
<boldin>• GI anomalies<boldout>
<boldin>• Growth abnormalities<boldout>
<boldin>• Eye problems<boldout>
<boldin>• Hearing loss<boldout>
<boldin>• Hematologic disorders<boldout>
<boldin>• Immune deficiency<boldout>
<boldin>• Endocrine disorders<boldout>
<boldin>• Atlantoaxial instability<boldout>
<boldin>• Sleep apnea<boldout>
<boldin>• Skin disorders<boldout>
<boldin>• Behavioral disorders<boldout>
<boldin>Question: Asses for Down<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>Look for “Hall’s Criteria”
<img:ch19_t01_07.jpg><boldout>
<boldin>HEENT<boldout>
<boldin>• Brachycephaly with flat occiput<boldout>
<boldin>• Wide fontanel<boldout>
<boldin>• Delayed fontanel closure<boldout>
<boldin>• Upward slanted palpebral fissures
<img:ch19_t01_08.jpg><boldout>
<boldin>• Epicanthal folds<boldout>
<boldin>• Flat nasal bridge<boldout>
<boldin>• Protruding tongue<boldout>
<boldin>• Open mouth<boldout>
<boldin>• High arched palate<boldout>
<boldin>• Small and low set ear<boldout>
<boldin>• Short neck, redundant skin<boldout>
<boldin>Cardiovascular system<boldout>
  • Endocardial Cushing defects (AVSD) - the most common
<boldin>• Ventricular septal defect<boldout>
<boldin>• Atrial septal defect<boldout>
<boldin>• Patent ductus arteriosus<boldout>
<boldin>• Aberrant subclavian artery<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary hypertension<boldout>
<boldin>Musculo-skeletal system<boldout>
<boldin>• Short broad hands<boldout>
<boldin>• Single transverse palmar creases (Simian crease)<boldout>
<boldin>• Fifth digit clinodactyly<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypoplastic mid phalanx of fifth finger<boldout>
<boldin>• Joint hyperflexibility<boldout>
<boldin>• Short metacarpals and phalanges<boldout>
  • Wide space between 1st and 2nd toes (Sandal gap deformity)
<boldin>• Short sternum
<img:ch19_t01_09.jpg><boldout>
<boldin>• Pelvic dysplasia<boldout>
<boldin>• Atlantoaxial instability                                                                             dal gap<boldout>
<boldin>Gastro-intestinal tract<boldout>
<boldin>• Duodenal atresia<boldout>
<boldin>• Annular pancreas<boldout>
<boldin>• Tracheoesophageal fistula<boldout>
<boldin>• Hirschsprung disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Imperforate anus<boldout>
<boldin>Central nervous system<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypotonia<boldout>
<boldin>• Mental retardation<boldout>
  • Developmental delay (motor delay is the universal finding)
<boldin>• Seizures<boldout>
<boldin>• Autism spectrum disorders<boldout>
<boldin>• Behavioral disorders (disruptive)<boldout>
<boldin>• Depression<boldout>
<boldin>• Alzheimer disease<boldout>
<boldin>Sensory<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital or acquired hearing loss<boldout>
<boldin>• Serous otitis media<boldout>
<boldin>• Refractive errors (myopia)<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital or acquired cataracts<boldout>
<boldin>• Nystagmus<boldout>
<boldin>• Strabismus<boldout>
<boldin>• Glaucoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Blocked tear ducts<boldout>
<boldin>Endocrine<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital or acquired hypothyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>• Diabetes mellitus<boldout>
<boldin>• Infertility<boldout>
<boldin>• Obesity<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperthyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>Hematologic<boldout>
<boldin>• Transient myeloproliferative syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Acute lymphocytic leukemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Acute myelogenous leukemia<boldout>
<boldin>Respiratory<boldout>
<boldin>• Obstructed sleep apnea<boldout>
<boldin>• Frequent infections (sinusitis, nasopharyngitis, pneumonia)<boldout>
<boldin>Cutaneous<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperkeratosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Seborrhea<boldout>
<boldin>• Xerosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Perigenital folliculitis<boldout>
<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Hearing and vision disorder<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital heart disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Failure to thrive<boldout>
<boldin>• Mental retardation<boldout>
<boldin>• Recurrent upper respiratory tract infections<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypothyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>• Acute lymphoblastic leukemia<boldout>
  • Megakaryoblastic form of acute myeloid leukemia, …
<boldin>• Risk of solid tumors are reduced<boldout>
<boldin>Diagnosis<boldout>
• <boldin>Clinical<boldout>
  • Neonatal period ≥ 8 Hall’s criteria should be full filled. If the criteria are /< 8, it is called Mosaic down syndrome
  • Beyond neonatal period ≥ 6 Hall’s criteria may be enough. If the criteria are /< 6, it is called Mosaic down syndrome

• <boldin>Antenatal screening<boldout>
<boldin>First trimester<boldout>
<boldin>• Fetal nuchal translucency (NT) thickness alone<boldout>
or in conjunction with<boldin><italicin>                                                               Triple screen<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Maternal beta hCG and<boldout>
<boldin>• Pregnancy associated plasma protein A (PAPP-A)<boldout>
  • NT alone can detect ≤ 70% of down syndrome pregnancies
  • NT + beta hCG + PAPP-A (<boldin>Triple screen<boldout>) = detects up to 87%

<boldin>Second trimester<boldout>
<boldin>• Beta hCG = ↑<boldout>
• Unconjugated estriol = ↑     <boldin><italicin>Quad screen<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Inhibin = ↑<boldout>
<boldin>• Alpha fetoprotein = ↓<boldout>
  • Can detect up to 80% of down syndrome pregnancies
  • If both the first and second trimester screens are combined (<boldin><italicin>integrated test<italicout><boldout>), detects up to 95%
  • Both first and second trimester tests have a 5% false positive rate

<boldin>Free cell fetal DNA test<boldout>
  • Blood sample is taken from the mother by venipuncture
  • Can be done starting from 10th week of gestational age
  • Has a high degree of accuracy (98% detection rate)
<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Patau syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Edward syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Congenital hypothyroidism<boldout>
<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
<boldin>• Random blood sugar<boldout>
<boldin>• Complete blood count<boldout>
<boldin>• Hearing screening<boldout>
<boldin>• Ophthalmoscopy<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest x-ray<boldout>
<boldin>• Electrocardiogram<boldout>
<boldin>• Echocardiography<boldout>
<boldin>• Thyroid function test<boldout>
<boldin>• Abdominal ultrasound, …<boldout>
<boldin>Management principle <boldout>
<boldin>• Therapy for specific problems<boldout>
<boldin>• Special education programs<boldout>
<boldin>• Parental participation in support groups<boldout>
<boldin>• Counseling<boldout>
<boldin>• Follow up<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 20: Burn
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner<boldout>
<boldin>• What do you see?<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you classify burn?<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you manage?<boldout>
<boldin>• What will be the complications?<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
<boldin>• Possible differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>Introduction <boldout>
  • A burn is defined as coagulative necrosis
  • Burns are a leading cause of unintentional injury in children, second only to motor vehicle crashes
  • The leading cause of burn in children 5-14 years old is flame injury
  • Scald burns account for 85% of total injuries and are most prevalent in children /<4 year old
  • Burn care involves a range of activities: prevention, acute care and resuscitation, wound management, pain relief, reconstruction, rehabilitation, and psychosocial adjustment

<boldin>Pathophysiology<boldout>
  • Burn is a coagulative necrosis of the epidermis and underlying tissues
  • The cutaneous injury is divided into three zones
  • Zone of Hyperemia: Inflammatory response of healthy tissue
  • Zone of stasis: Is characterized by a decreased tissue perfusion with sluggish blood flow. It can either survive or progress to coagulative necrosis.
  • Zone of coagulation: Irreversible tissue damage, no blood flow
<boldin>Types of burns<boldout>
<boldin>• Thermal<boldout>
  • Scaled burn – the most common type in children
  • Flame burn – the most common type in adults
  • Contact - damage from contact with hot or cold solid materials
  • Electrical - conduction of electrical current through tissues
  • Chemical – contact with noxious chemicals (acid, alkali)
<boldin>• Inhalational<boldout>
<boldin>• Radiation (e.g. Sunburn)<boldout>
<boldin>• Frostbite<boldout>
<boldin>Classifications<boldout>
Classified according to the depth of tissue injury
<boldin>• First degree (superficial or epidermal)<boldout>
<boldin>• Second degree (partial thickness)<boldout>
<boldin>• Third degree<boldin> <boldout>(full thickness)<boldout>
<boldin>First degree burn<boldout>
<boldin>• Involves only the epidermis<boldout>
<boldin>• Causes: Uv exposure, very short flash<boldout>
<boldin>• Characterized by
<img:ch20_t01_10.png><boldout>
<boldin>• Swelling, erythema, pain<boldout>
<boldin>• Can blench or bleed<boldout>
<boldin>• No blister<boldout>
<boldin>• Minimal or no edema<boldout>
<boldin>• Pain resolves within 48-72hrs<boldout>
  • Healed within 3-6 days with no scaring
<boldin>Second degree burn<boldout>
  • Involves the entire epidermis /& variable portion of dermal layer
<img:ch20_t01_11.png>
<boldin>• Characterized by:<boldout>
<boldin>• Blisters, bullae<boldout>
<boldin>• Serous fluid<boldout>
<boldin>• Can bleed<boldout>
<boldin>• Edematous<boldout>
  • Underlying tissue is mottled pink and white with fair capillary refill
  • Classified as Superficial and deep partial thickness burn
<boldin>Superficial second degree burn <boldout>
  • Injury to the epidermis and superficial dermis
  • Extremely painful due to large number of remaining viable nerve endings are exposed
  • Healed in 7-20 days as the epithelium regenerates in the absence of infection
<boldin>Deep second degree burn<boldout>
  • Injury through the epidermis and deep into the dermis
  • Pain is less severe than superficial burns due to fewer nerve endings remain viable
  • Healed in 21-35 days spontaneously with no infection
  • If infected, it would have converted to 3rd degree burn
<boldin>Third degree burn<boldout>
  • Full-thickness injury through the epidermis and dermis into the subcutaneous fat, fascia, muscle, and/or bone
  • Leaving no residual epidermal cells to repopulate the damaged area
<boldin>• The wound cannot epithelialize<boldout>
  • Can heal only by wound contraction or skin grafting
<boldin>• Characterized by:<boldout>
  • Absence of painful sensation and capillary filling due to loss of nerve and capillary elements
<boldin>• No blenching or bleeding<boldout>
<boldin>• White, yellow, brown leathery appearance<boldout>
<boldin>Estimation of Total Body Surface Area (<boldout><boldin>TBSA<boldout><boldin>)<boldout>
<boldin>Rule of nine<boldout>– for children /> 14 years old
  • Each leg represents = 18 % of TBSA
<boldin>• Each arm represents = 9 %<boldout>
  • Anterior and posterior trunk, each represents =18%
<boldin>• The head represents = 9 %<boldout>
<boldin>• Perineum represents = 1 %<boldout>
<boldin>• The head represents = 9 %<boldout>
<boldin>Rule of palm<boldout>–for small burn (BSA /< 10%)
  • Each of the patient palm <italicin>excluding <italicout>fingers represent = 1 %
<boldin><italicin>Lund and Brower chart <italicout><boldout><italicin>(age to body ratio) <italicout>– for children /< 14 years old
<img:ch20_t01_12.png>
<img:t19.png>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout>
<italicin>Subtract 1% from head for each year above one year of<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>age<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>and add ½% to each leg for each year over one year of<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>age.<italicout>
<boldin>Indications for admission<boldout>
<boldin>• Burns affecting />10% TBSA<boldout>
<boldin>• Burns />10-20% of TBSA<boldout>
<boldin>• Third degree burn<boldout>
<boldin>• High tension electrical burn or lightening<boldout>
<boldin>• Inhalational injury<boldout>
<boldin>• Chemical burn<boldout>
  • Burns to the face, hand, feet, perineum, genitalia or major joints
<boldin>• Suspected child abuse/social issues<boldout>
<boldin>• Inadequate home or social environment<boldout>
<boldin>• Associated injuries (fracture)<boldout>
<boldin>• Pregnancy<boldout>
<boldin>Management principles<boldout>
<boldin>Acute treatments of burns<boldout>
  • First aid, including washing of wounds and removal of devitalized tissue
<boldin>• Fluid resuscitation<boldout>
<boldin>• Provision of energy requirement<boldout>
<boldin>• Control of pain<boldout>
  • Control of bacterial wound flora = topical antibiotics
  • Use of dressing to close the wound
<boldin>• Reconstruction and rehabilitation<boldout>
<boldin>First aid measures<boldout>
  • Extinguish flames by roll, but don’t run
<boldin>• Check for ABC<boldout>
  • Cover the child with blanket, coat or carpet
<boldin>• Remove clothes, rings, bracelets<boldout>
<boldin>• Brush off any remaining chemical<boldout>
  • Cover the burned area with clean /& dry sheeting
  • Apply cold (not iced) wet compresses to small injuries
<boldin>• Administer analgesic medication<boldout>
<boldin>Fluid resuscitation<boldout>
  • Children with burns />15% of BSA require IV fluid resuscitation to maintain adequate perfusion and should be kept null per mouth (NPO) to avoid gastric distention
  • A Foley catheter should be inserted into the bladder to monitor urine output in all children who require IV fluid resuscitation
  • Decision on fluid resuscitation after 24 hours is determined by urine output

<boldin>Parkland formula (PF)<boldout>
  • Ringer lactate 4ml /kg/% BSA burnt = 4ml * weight (kg) * total surface area burnt (%)
  • Half of the fluid is given in the first 8 hours of post burn period
  • The remaining half is given over the next 16 hours
<boldin>• Used to replace fluid deficit<boldout>
  • Do not give if 24 hours past post burn
  • Do not give the first half of the fluid if the patient arrive after 8 hours of the event
  • Till the Parkland formula is calculated, 10-20ml/kg of fluid can be given over 1 hour

<boldin>Maintenance<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>fluid (MF)<boldout>
<boldin>• <italicin>See at shock<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>management<italicout><boldout>
  • Add 5% dextrose to maintenance fluid for children /< 5 years old or weight /< 20 kg to prevent hypoglycemia

<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>Acute complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Infection<boldout>
<boldin>• Renal failure<boldout>
<boldin>• Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)<boldout>
<boldin>• Dysrhythmia<boldout>
<boldin>• Loss of consciousness<boldout>
<boldin>• Motor paralysis etc.<boldout>
<boldin>Long term disability<boldout>
<boldin>Gastro intestinal tract<boldout>
<boldin>• Curling ulcer<boldout>
<boldin>Psychiatric and neurologic disabilities<boldout>
<boldin>• Sleep disorder<boldout>
<boldin>• Depression<boldout>
<boldin>• Post-traumatic stress syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Burn encephalopathy<boldout>
<boldin>Musculo-skeletal system<boldout>
<boldin>• Contracture
<img:ch20_t01_13.jpg><boldout>
<boldin>• Marjolin’s ulcer<boldout>
<boldin>• Alopecia<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypertrophic scar<boldout>
<boldin>• Amputations<boldout>
<boldin>• Osteoporosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Bed sores                   	                      Contracture<boldout>
<boldin>• Hetrotopic ossification<boldout>
<boldin>• Deep venous thrombosis (DVT)<boldout>
<boldin>Investigation <boldout>
<boldin>• Case dependent<boldout>
<boldin>• Random blood sugar<boldout>
<boldin>• Complete blood count<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum electrolytes<boldout>
<boldin>• Creatinine<boldout>
<boldin>• Blood urea nitrogen<boldout>
  • Urine analysis – may detect myoglobin showing muscle injury
<boldin>• X-ray<boldout>
<boldin>• Electrocardiography<boldout>
<boldin>• Carbon mono oxide level<boldout>
<boldin>• Wound culture<boldout>
<boldin>Differential diagnosis of burn<boldout>
<boldin>• Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN)<boldout>
<boldin>• Steven Johnson syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS)<boldout>
<boldin>Example<boldout>
  • A 16-year-old female known epileptic patient who sustained a scaled burn to her anterior chest, abdomen and both upper limbs presented to pediatric emergency department after 3 hours of the event. (Weight = 40 kg)
  • Total body surface area burnt is 36%
  • Anterior chest and abdomen = 9+9 = 18%
  • Both upper limb = 9+9 = 18%
<boldin>• What should be the fluid management?<boldout>
  • For the first 24 hours of post burn period; total fluid to be given =
  • PF + MF = (4ml x 40kg x 36%)/24hrs <boldin>+<boldout> 1900ml = 5,760ml/24hrs <boldin>+<boldout> 1900ml
<boldin>• Since the patient arrived after 3 hours:<boldout>
  • We should give half of the parkland fluid over the next 5 hours (i.e. ½PF = 2880ml to be run over 5 hour)
  • Total fluid to be given over 5 hours <boldin>=<boldout> ½PF <boldin>+<boldout> MF (for 5 hour) = 2880ml <boldin>+<boldout> 396ml = 3276 ml to be run over 5 hours at a rate of 218 drops per minute
<boldin>• For the next 16 hours;<boldout>
  • Fluid = ½PF + MF (of 16 hour) = 2880ml + 1267ml = 4147ml to be given in two divided doses. i.e. 4147/2 = 2073.5 ml to run over 8 hours at a rate of 86 drops per minute
  • For electrical burn start as 2-3x of the normal maintenance fluid with target urine output

topics.title: Chapter 21: Shock
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner:<boldout>
<boldin>• Assess for signs of shock? Or<boldout>
  • How do you approach a patient with shock?
  • What are the phases and classification of shock?
<boldin>• How do you manage?<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
<boldin>Introduction <boldout>
  • Shock is a physiologic state characterized by a significant systemic reduction tissue perfusion resulting in decrease tissue oxygen delivery
  • Shock may be present with normal or decreased BP
  • Physiologic parameters that the body can manipulate to compensate for compromised perfusion are: COP = SV x HR (SV = preload, cardiac contractility and after load) and systemic vascular resistance.

<boldin>Phases of shock<boldout>
• <boldin>Compensated<boldout>
  • The body’s homeostatic mechanism rapidly compensates for decreased perfusion
<boldin>• Characterized by<boldout>
<boldin>• Normal blood pressure and cardiac output<boldout>
<boldin>• Adequate tissue perfusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Vital organ functions are maintained<boldout>
  • Signs of peripheral vasoconstriction (cool skin, decreased pulse, oliguria)
  • Tachycardia with or without tachypnea. It may be the first or only sign of early compensated shock
• <boldin>Decompensated<boldout>
<boldin>• Compensatory mechanisms are overwhelmed<boldout>
<boldin>• Signs /& symptoms of organ dysfunction<boldout>
<boldin>• The child’s condition usually deteriorates rapidly<boldout>
<boldin>• Characterized by<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypotension<boldout>
<boldin>• Low cardiac output and<boldout>
<boldin>• Inadequate tissue perfusion<boldout>
• <boldin>Irreversible<boldout>
  • Characterize by cell death and is refractory to medical treatment
  • Multiorgan system dysfunction with end organ injury
<boldin>Criteria for Organ Dysfunction<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Cardiovascular system <italicout><boldout>
  • Despite administration of isotonic intravenous fluid bolus ≥60 ml/kg in 1 hour: decrease in blood pressure (hypotension) systolic BP /<90 mmHg, mean arterial pressure /<70 mmHg, /<5th percentile for age or systolic BP /<2 SD below normal for age  <boldin>Or<boldout>
  • Need for vasoactive drug to maintain BP in normal range (dopamine />5 µg/kg/min or dobutamine, epinephrine, or norepinephrine at any dose)        <boldin>Or<boldout>
<boldin>• Two of the following:<boldout>
  • Unexplained metabolic acidosis: base deficit />5.0 mEq/L
  • Increased arterial lactate: />1 mmol/L or />2× upper limit of normal
<boldin>• Oliguria: urine output /<0.5 ml/kg/hr<boldout>
<boldin>• Prolonged capillary refill: />5 sec<boldout>
<boldin>• Core-to-peripheral temperature gap: />3°C (5.4°F)<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Respiratory system <italicout><boldout>
  • PaO2 /FIO2 ratio /<300 in absence of cyanotic heart disease or preexisting lung disease <boldin>Or<boldout>
  • PaCO2 /> 65 torr or 20mmHg over baseline PaCO2 <boldin>Or<boldout>
  • Need for />50% FIO2 to maintain saturation ≥ 92% <boldin>Or<boldout>
  • Need for nonelective invasive or noninvasive mechanical ventilation

<boldin><italicin>Neurologic<italicout><boldout>
• GCS score ≤11 <boldin>Or<boldout>
  • Acute change in mental status with decrease in GCS score ≥3 points from abnormal baseline

<boldin><italicin>Renal<italicout><boldout>
  • Serum creatinine /> 0.5 mg/dl, ≥ 2× upper limit of normal for age or 2-fold increase in baseline creatinine value

<boldin><italicin>Hematologic <italicout><boldout>
  • Platelet count /<100,000/mm3 or decline of 50% in platelet count from highest value recorded over last 3 days (for patients with chronic hematologic or oncologic disorders) <boldin>Or<boldout>
• INR />1.5 <boldin>Or<boldout>
<boldin>• Activated prothrombin time /> 60 sec<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Hepatic <italicout><boldout>
  • Total bilirubin ≥ 4 mg/dl (not applicable for newborn)
  • Alanine transaminase level 2× upper limit of normal for age
<boldin>Classifications<boldout>
• <boldin>Hypovolemic<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>shock<boldout>
  • The most common cause of shock in children worldwide
  • Decreased preload secondary to internal or external losses
  • Caused by any condition that results in decreased circulating blood volume, such as hemorrhage or dehydration (e.g., from Acute gastro-enteritis)
  • The amount of volume loss determines the success of compensatory mechanisms, such as endogenous catecholamines, in maintaining blood pressure and cardiac output
  • Volume losses greater than 25% result in decompensated shock
<boldin>• Potential etiologies<boldout>
<boldin>• Blood loss → hemorrhage<boldout>
<boldin>• Plasma loss → burns, nephrotic syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Water/electrolyte loss → diarrhea, vomiting<boldout>
<boldin>• Clinical manifestations<boldout>
<boldin>• Often manifests initially as orthostatic hypotension<boldout>
<boldin>• Dry mucous membranes<boldout>
<boldin>• Poor skin turgor<boldout>
<boldin>• Decreased urine output<boldout>
<boldin>• Normal or slightly cool extremities<boldout>
  • Peripheral or even femoral pulse may be normal, decrease or absent
• <boldin>Distributive<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>shock<boldout>
  • Results from decreased SVR with abnormal distribution of blood flow within the microcirculation and inadequate tissue perfusion
  • Typically caused by anaphylactic or neurogenic shock, or as a result of medications or toxins

<boldin>Anaphylactic shock<boldout> is characterized by
<boldin>• Acute angioedema of the upper airway<boldout>
<boldin>• Bronchospasm<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary edema<boldout>
<boldin>• Urticaria and<boldout>
  • Hypotension because of extravasation of intravascular fluid from permeable capillaries

<boldin>Neurogenic<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>shock<boldout>
  • Characterized by a total loss of distal sympathetic cardiovascular tone with hypotension resulting from pooling of blood within the vascular bed
  • Typically, secondary to spinal cord transection or injury
• <boldin>Cardiogenic<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>shock<boldout>
  • Occurs when cardiac output is limited because of primary cardiac dysfunction
<boldin>• Potential etiologies<boldout>
<boldin>• Dysrhythmias (e.g., supraventricular tachycardia)<boldout>
  • Congenital heart disease (e.g., any lesion that impairs left ventricular outflow)
<boldin>• Cardiac dysfunction after cardiac surgery<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiomayopatis<boldout>
<boldin>• Ventricular fibrillation<boldout>
<boldin>• Clinical features<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachycardia<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachypnea<boldout>
<boldin>• Cool extremities<boldout>
<boldin>• Delayed capillary filling time<boldout>
<boldin>• Poor peripheral and/or central pulses<boldout>
<boldin>• Declining mental status<boldout>
<boldin>• Decreased urine output<boldout>
<boldin>• Signs of congestive heart failure<boldout>
• <boldin>Obstructive<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>shock<boldout>
  • Decreased cardiac output secondary to direct impediment to right- or left-sided heart outflow or restriction of all cardiac chambers
  • Stems from any lesion that creates a mechanical barrier that impedes adequate cardiac output
<boldin>• Potential etiologies<boldout>
<boldin>• Pericardial tamponade<boldout>
<boldin>• Tension pneumothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary embolism<boldout>
<boldin>• Anterior mediastinal masses<boldout>
<boldin>• Critical coarctation of aorta<boldout>
  • Often manifests as inadequate cardiac output due to a physical restriction of forward blood flow
  • The acute presentation may quickly progress to cardiac arrest
• <boldin>Septic<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>shock<boldout>
  • Occurs secondary to an inflammatory response to invading microorganisms and their toxins and results in abnormal blood distribution
  • Septic process involves more complex interaction of distributive, hypovolumic and cardiogenic shock
<boldin>• There are two clinical stages:<boldout>
<boldin>• <boldin>Hyperdynamic stage; <boldout>characterized by<boldout>
  • Normal or high cardiac output with bounding pulses
<boldin>• Warm extremities, and<boldout>
<boldin>• A wide pulse pressure<boldout>
• <boldin>Decompensated stage<boldout>
  • Follows the hyperdynamic stage if aggressive treatment has not been initiated
<boldin>• Characterized by<boldout>
<boldin>• Impaired mental status<boldout>
<boldin>• Cool extremities, and<boldout>
<boldin>• Diminished pulses<boldout>
<boldin>Approach to a patient<boldout>
<boldin>• ABCD of life<boldout>
<boldin>• Asses the circulatory problem<boldout>
<boldin>• Weather hands are warm or cold<boldout>
<boldin>• Capillary refill<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulse<boldout>
  • Asses the child weather malnourished or not
<boldin>Diagnosis of shock<boldout>
  • Recognition of shock may be difficult because of the presence of compensatory mechanisms that prevent hypotension until 25% of intravascular volume is lost
  • Therefore, blood pressure is never been a criteria to diagnose shock in children as its likely to be preserved early

<boldin>• <boldin>Historic features <boldout>that may suggest the presence of shock include:<boldout>
<boldin>• Severe vomiting and diarrhea<boldout>
<boldin>• Trauma with hemorrhage<boldout>
  • Febrile illness, especially in an immunocompromised patient
<boldin>• Symptoms of congestive heart failure<boldout>
<boldin>• Exposure to a known allergic antigen<boldout>
<boldin>• Spinal cord injury<boldout>
• <boldin>Physical<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>examination<boldout>
  • Blood pressure may be normal in the initial stages of hypovolemic and septic shock
  • Tachycardia almost always accompanies shock and occurs before blood pressure changes in children
  • Tachypnea may be present as a compensatory mechanism for severe metabolic acidosis
  • Mental status changes may indicate poor cerebral perfusion
  • Capillary refill may be prolonged (/>3 seconds) with cool and mottled extremities
  • Peripheral pulses may be bounding in early septic shock
<boldin>• Cold extremities<boldout>
<boldin>• <boldin>Laboratory studies<boldout> should include<boldout>
  • Random blood sugar → To rule out hypoglycemia
  • Complete blood count → To assess for blood loss /& infection
  • Electrolytes → To assess for metabolic acidosis /& electrolyte abnormalities
  • Blood urea nitrogen and creatinine → To evaluate renal function and perfusion
  • Coagulation factors → To evaluate for DIC, which may accompany shock
<boldin>• Electrocardiography<boldout>
<boldin>• Echocardiography<boldout>
<boldin>• Abdominal ultrasonography<boldout>
  • Toxicology screens → To evaluate for a poisoning, which could cause shock

<boldin>Management principles<boldout>
<boldin>• Aggressive treatment with in few minutes<boldout>
  • Initiate treatment immediately before cause of shock is identified
  • Asses response to each intervention and cause of shock while treating the child
  • Specific therapy must be initiated as soon as cause of shock is identified

<boldin>General measures<boldout>
  • If the child has any bleeding; stop by applying pressure
<boldin>• Give oxygen<boldout>
  • Make sure the child is warm- cover with blanket
  • Select appropriate site for administration of fluid
<boldin>• Draw blood for emergency laboratory<boldout>
<boldin>Shock with no <boldout><boldin>SAM<boldout>
  • Give 20ml/kg of IV fluid (RL or NS) as rapidly as possible
  • If no response → Give another 20 ml/kg as quickly as possible
  • Reassess again if no response → Give another 20ml/kg
<boldin>• If still no improvement<boldout>
  • Give 20ml/kg of blood<boldin> <boldout>over 30 minutes unless there is profuse watery diarrhea
  • If the patient has profuse watery diarrhea → repeat RL
  • If the patient has both shock and dehydration at the same time, give 30ml/kg fast
<boldin>Shock with <boldout><boldin>SAM<boldout>
<boldin>Shock:<boldout>
• Lethargic or unconscious <boldin>and<boldout>
• Cold hands <boldin>plus<boldout>
  • Either slow capillary refill (/>3 seconds) <boldin>or<boldout> weak fast pulse
<boldin>Key steps to manage shock in SAM patients<boldout>
  • Give oxygen (for infant 0.5-1L/min and older children 1-2L/min)
<boldin>• Give 10% glucose 5 ml/kg IV<boldout>
  • Give IV fluid at 15 ml/kg over one hour using ringer lactate with 5% glucose or 0.45% normal saline with 5% glucose
  • Measure and record pulse and respirations at the start and every 10 minute
<boldin>• Give antibiotics based on the guideline<boldout>
<boldin>After one hour of treatment<boldout>
  • If there are signs of improvement (pulse rate and respiratory rate fall); Repeat IV 15 ml/kg over one hour, then switch to oral or NGT rehydration with ReSoMal, 10 ml/kg/hour for up to 10 hours in alternate hours with F-75, then continue feeding with starter F-75
<boldin>• If there are no signs of improvement, assume septic shock /&:<boldout>
  • Give maintenance fluid IV (4 ml/kg/hour) while waiting for blood
  • When blood is available transfuse slowly with 10 ml/kg fresh whole blood over 3 hour
  • If signs of heart failure, give 5-7 ml/kg packed cells rather than whole blood
<boldin>• Then begin feeding with F-75<boldout>
  • If gets worse (breathing increases by 5 or more breaths/min and pulse increases by 25 or more beats/min) → Stop the infusion

<boldin>Maintenance Fluid (MF)<boldout>
  • Daily water needs are based upon insensible losses from the respiratory tract and skin, and sensible losses from urine and stool
  • Water requirements are estimated in direct relation to caloric energy expenditures, with approximately 100ml of exogenous water needed for every 100 kcal/kg of energy expended
  • Maintenance fluids usually contain D5W, 17 calories/100 ml, which provides approximately 20% of the normal caloric needs of the patient, prevents the development of starvation ketoacidosis and diminishes the protein degradation that would occur if the patient received no calories
  • We are using the <italicin>Hollyday Segar’s Formula<italicout> for the 1st , 2nd  and remaining weights above 20kg for hospitalized patients
<boldin>Goals of Maintenance Fluids <boldout>
<boldin>• Prevent dehydration<boldout>
<boldin>• Prevent electrolyte disorders<boldout>
<boldin>• Prevent ketoacidosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Prevent protein degradation<boldout>
<boldin>Hollyday Segar’s Formula<boldout>
<boldin>Method 1:<boldout>–Maintenance fluid per 24 hour period
<boldin>• Weight ≤ 10 kg<boldout>
  • 100 ml/kg
<boldin>• Weight between 10 and 20 kg<boldout>
  • 1000 ml for the first 10 kg
  • Plus 50 ml/kg for any increment of weight over 10 kg
<boldin>• Weight between 20 and 80 kg<boldout>
  • 1500 ml for the first 20 kg
  • Plus 20 ml/kg for any increment of weight over 20 kg
<boldin>• Maximum = 2400 ml/day<boldout>
<boldin>Method 2: <boldout>– Maintenance fluid per hourly basis
<boldin>• Weight ≤ 10 kg<boldout>
  • 4 ml/kg/hour
<boldin>• Weight between 10 and 20 kg<boldout>
  • 40 ml for the first 10 kg
  • Plus 2 ml/kg/hour for any increment of weight over 10 kg
<boldin>• Weight between 20 and 80 kg<boldout>
  • 60 ml for the first 20 kg
  • Plus 1 ml/kg/hour for any increment of weight over 20 kg
<boldin>• To a maximum of 100 ml/hour<boldout>
  • Up to a maximum of 100 ml/hour x 24 = 2400 ml/day
<boldin>How to prepare maintenance fluid (MF)?<boldout>
<boldin>MF = NS +  D10%<boldout>
  • Since we have no 10% DW in most of our set up (Ethiopia), we should prepare from the fluid that are available in our set.
  • Most commonly we found fluids in the form of 5% DW, 40% DW, 0.9% NS (0% DW) and RL (0% DW)
<boldin>• We can prepare 10% DW from 5% DW, 40% DW and NS as follows:<boldout>
<boldin>• D10% = 85% D5W + 15% D40%<boldout>
<boldin>• D10% =  NS +  D40%<boldout>
<boldin>• In general, we can prepare any % of DW from any given % of DW by using the following formula:<boldout>
<boldin>• Tv = Va + Vb<boldout>
  • 
  • 
<boldin>Where<boldout>
  • a = highest concentration of DW, b = lowest conc. of DW, x = conc. of DW wanted
  • Tv = total volume needed, Va = volume of “a”, Vb = volume of “b”
  • a /> x /> b
  • In the first 24 hours of neonatal age, MF is prepared from only D10% (i.e. MF= D10%)

How to calculate number of drops per minute?
  • 1 ml = 20 drops
  • 

<boldin>Example<boldout><boldin> 1<boldout>
Calculate MF and drops per minute of a 35kg boy to run over 8 hrs
<boldin>Solution<boldout>
<boldin>Calculate total volume of fluid to be needed for 24 hours and divide into 3 i.e.:<boldout>
  • (For the 1st 10kg = 100ml x 10 = 1000ml) + (For the 2nd 10kg = 50ml x 10 = 500ml) + (For the next 15kg = 20ml x 15 = 300ml) = 1800ml/day
  • Divide it in to 3 to run over 8 hours (i.e. 1800ml ÷ 3 = 600 ml to run over 8 hours)
  • = 25 dpm
<boldin>What about the preparation?<boldout>
<boldin>• NS =  600ml x   = 200ml<boldout>
  • D10% = 600ml – 200 ml = 400ml
<boldin>• If prepared from 5% DW and 40% DW:<boldout>
<boldin>• D5W = 400ml x  = 340ml<boldout>
  • D40% = 400ml – 340ml = 60ml
<boldin>• If prepared from NS and D40%:<boldout>
<boldin>• NS = 400ml x   = 300ml<boldout>
  • D40% = 400ml – 300ml = 100ml
<boldin>When we write on order sheet:<boldout>
  • Please put on MF 600 ml (200ml of NS <boldin>+<boldout> 340 ml of D5W <boldin>+<boldout> 60 ml of D40%) to be run over 8 hours at a rate of 25 drops per minute    <boldin>Or<boldout>
  • Please put on MF 600 ml (500ml of NS <boldin>+<boldout> 100ml of D40%) to run over 8 hours at a rate of 25 drops per minute

<boldin>Example<boldout><boldin> 2<boldout>
How to prepare a 5% DW from 40% DW and NS for a 10kg toddler?
<boldin>Solution<boldout>
<boldin>Given<boldout>
  • Total fluid needed (Tv D5W) is 100ml x 10 = 400ml
  • a = 40%, b = 0%, x = 5%
<boldin>Find<boldout>
<boldin>• Vb (volume of NS)?<boldout>
<boldin>• Va (volume of D40%)?<boldout>
<boldin>• Tv D5W = Va + Vb<boldout>
  • ml NS
<boldin>• D40%<boldout>
  • So to prepare 400ml D5W, we need 350ml NS /& 50ml D40%

<boldin>Management of septic shock<boldout>
<boldin>• Correct hypoglycemia and hypocalcaemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Broad spectrum antibiotics<boldout>
  • The choice of antibiotics depends on age of the patient, immune status and comorbid conditions

<boldin>General principles for antimicrobial coverage for sepsis<boldout>
  • All children with septic shock should receive coverage for MRSA
  • Coverage for enteric organisms should be added whenever clinical features are suggestive
  • Treatment for pseudomonas should be included for children who are immunosuppressed
  • monocytogen and HSV are important pathogens in infant’s ≤ 28 days

<boldin>Key words<boldout>
SIRS: Two out of four criteria, one of which must be abnormal temperature or abnormal leukocyte count
  • Core temperature />38.5 0c or /<36 0c
  • Mean heart rate />2 SD above normal for age
  • Respiratory rate />2 SD above normal for age
  • Leukocyte count elevated or depressed for age (not secondary to chemotherapy) or />10% immature neutrophils
Sepsis: SIRS plus a suspected or proven infection.
<boldin>Severe sepsis: Sepsis plus one of the following:<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiovascular organ dysfunction, defined as<boldout>
  • Despite />40 ml/kg of isotonic intravenous fluid in 1 hour
  • Hypotension /< 5th percentile for age or systolic blood pressure /<2 SD below normal for age OR
  • Need for vasoactive drug to maintain blood pressure OR
<boldin>• Two of the following<boldout>
<boldin>• Unexplained metabolic acidosis: base deficit />5mEq<boldout>
  • Increased arterial lactate />2x upper limit of normal
<boldin>• Oliguria: urine output /< 0.5 ml/kg/hour<boldout>
<boldin>• Prolonged capillary refill: />5 sec<boldout>
  • Core to peripheral temperature gap />3 0C
  • Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) as defined by the presence of a Pao2/Fio2 ratio ≤300 mm Hg, bilateral infiltrates on chest radiograph, and no evidence of left heart failure; OR
  • Sepsis plus 2 or more organ dysfunctions (respiratory, renal, neurologic, hematologic, or hepatic)

Septic shock: Sepsis plus cardiovascular organ dysfunction as defined above.
Multiple organ dysfunction syndromes (MODS: Presence of altered organ function such that homeostasis cannot be maintained without medical intervention.

topics.title: Chapter 22: Introduction
topics.content_html:
• Poisoning is exposure to a chemical or other agent that adversely affects functioning of an organism
  • It can be intentional or unintentional (the most common)
  • Poisoning can occur at two peak ages
<boldin>• Less than 6 years<boldout>
<boldin>• It is unintentional or accidental<boldout>
  • Occur because young children put everything to their mouth
  • Young children are hyperactive, they want to explore their environment
  • They do not know what is safe or dangerous
<boldin>• Teenagers (13 – 19 years old)<boldout>
  • It is intentional (suicidal, abuse, or misuse)
<boldin>• Results more severe toxicity<boldout>
  • Can be because of Challenging issue in their life, family problems like death of family members and psychiatric illness
<boldin>• Females are higher risk<boldout>
  • “All substances are poisons, the right dose separates poisons from remedy.” <italicin>Paracelsus<italicout>

<boldin>Routes of exposure<boldout>
<boldin>• Ingestion – the most common<boldout>
<boldin>• Dermal<boldout>
<boldin>• Inhalational<boldout>
<boldin>• Ophthalmic<boldout>
<boldin>• Parenteral<boldout>
<boldin>• Transplacental<boldout>
<boldin>Risk factors<boldout>
<boldin>Age<boldout>
  • Majority of poisonings involving young children are classified as unintentional
  • More than one-half of poisoning exposures involving teenagers are intentional
<boldin>Sex<boldout>
  • For preadolescents, poisoning occurs slightly more often in males than females
  • In teenagers, more than half of all poisonings occurring in females

<boldin>Developmental factors <boldout>
  • The normal developmental progression of young children, including exploration of their environment, places them at risk for poisoning
  • As children become mobile, they are able to maneuver through the home; they learn to open cabinets and to examine the contents
  • As children begin to walk, they may be able to grab items that were previously out of reach (eg, on countertops)
  • Improved fine motor skills enable toddlers to open simple screw-on caps or bottle tops
  • Normal curiosity and desire for oral stimulation may cause children to place new objects directly into the mouth for tasting or swallowing
  • Well-meaning preschoolers may try to /"help/" by using toxic cleaning products or by attempting to self-administer medication
  • The goal of independence and the tendency to believe that they are indestructible may cause adolescents to take undue risks in experimenting with illicit drugs or other substances of abuse.

<boldin>Environment factors<boldout>
  • Most homes contain numerous potentially toxic substances, particularly in the kitchen, bathrooms, laundry room, and garage
  • Look-alikes also pose a problem for small children
<boldin>• Some medications look identical to candy<boldout>
  • Some cleaning products may look like or be stored in containers that resemble those of food or juice
  • The availability of medications, drugs, or other substances of abuse at school or home may put vulnerable teenagers at further risk

<boldin>Common substances ingested<boldout>
<boldin>Drugs<boldout>
<boldin>• Analgesics<boldout>
<boldin>• Topical preparations<boldout>
<boldin>• Vitamins<boldout>
<boldin>• Minerals<boldout>
<boldin>Non drug<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>substance<boldout>
<boldin>• Cosmetics<boldout>
<boldin>• Cleaning solutions<boldout>
<boldin>• Insecticides<boldout>
<boldin>• Caustics<boldout>
<boldin>• Hydrocarbons<boldout>
<boldin>• Plants<boldout>
<boldin>• Foreign bodies<boldout>
<boldin>Effects of poisons <boldout>
  • Interfere with the transport or tissue utilization of oxygen
<boldin>• Depress or stimulate CNS<boldout>
<boldin>• Effects on the autonomic nervous system<boldout>
<boldin>• Effects on the lungs by aspiration<boldout>
<boldin>• Effects on the heart and vasculature<boldout>
<boldin>• Produce local damage<boldout>
<boldin>• Effects on the liver<boldout>
<boldin>Approach to a poisoned patient<boldout>
  • The initial approach is like any acutely sick child
<boldin>• We follow ABCD of life:<boldout>
<boldin>A<boldout>irway
<boldin>• Remove excess secretion<boldout>
  • Look for flaccid tongue or tongue fall
  • Look for any inhalational injury that worsen with time
<boldin>• Check signs of burn around mouth<boldout>
<boldin>• Consider intubation if severe airway compromise<boldout>
<boldin>B<boldout>reathing
<boldin>• Look for signs of respiratory distress<boldout>
<boldin>• Check oxygen saturation, ABG<boldout>
<boldin>• Auscultate lung fields<boldout>
<boldin>• Give oxygen if indicated<boldout>
<boldin>C<boldout>irculation
  • Check for signs of shock and treat accordingly
<boldin>• Delayed capillary refill<boldout>
<boldin>• Feeble and fast pulse<boldout>
<boldin>• Cold extremities<boldout>
<boldin>• Secure IV line early<boldout>
<boldin>• Continuous ECG monitoring<boldout>
<boldin>C<boldout>oma
  • Check whether a child is comatose or not
  • If comatose; determine RBS and give dextrose
<boldin>D<boldout>ehydration
  • Asses for signs of dehydration and treat accordingly
<boldin>When do we suspect poisoning?<boldout>
<boldin>History<boldout>
  • Apparently healthy child presented with sudden onset of loss of consciousness or respiratory distress
  • If any medication, caustic agent, pesticide, insecticide around the patient
  • Name specific ingredients and concentration of the toxin if known
<boldin>• If pills: - shape, color, quantity<boldout>
  • Where the child was found – at home, work place, laundry, bathroom, …
<boldin>• Timing of ingestion<boldout>
<boldin>• Progression of symptoms<boldout>
  • Medical history and concurrent drug therapy - underlying disease and medications may make the child more susceptible to the effects of the toxin
  • How much was ingested – count pills or measure the remaining volume of liquid
<boldin>• If inhalational, ocular or dermal<boldout>
<boldin>• Concentration of the agent<boldout>
<boldin>• Length of contact time<boldout>
<boldin>• History of psychiatric illness<boldout>
<boldin>• The child’s social environment<boldout>
<boldin>Physical examination<boldout>
<boldin>General<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>appearance<boldout>
<boldin>• Level of consciousness – depressed<boldout>
<boldin>• Acutely sick looking<boldout>
<boldin>Vital signs<boldout>
<boldin>Pulse rate<boldout>
<boldin>• Tachycardia–atropine, antidepressants, caffeine, …<boldout>
  • Bradycardia – beta blockers, calcium channel blockers…
<boldin>Respiratory rate<boldout>
  • Tachypnea – amphetamine, carbon mono oxide, caffeine
<boldin>• Bradypnea – opioids, alcohol, barbiturates, …<boldout>
<boldin>Blood pressure<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypertension – amphetamine, anticholinergics, …<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypotension – beta blocker, CCB, opioids<boldout>
<boldin>Temperature<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperthermia – selective serotonin inhibitors, lithium<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypothermia –morphine, heroin, …<boldout>
<boldin>HEENT<boldout>
<boldin>Eye<boldout>
<boldin>• Miosis – opioid, organophosphate<boldout>
<boldin>• Myadriasis – atropine, cocaine, amphetamine<boldout>
<boldin>• Lacrimation – organophosphate<boldout>
<boldin>Mouth and throat<boldout>
<boldin>• Salivation – organophosphate, ketamine<boldout>
<boldin>• Oral burn – corrosives<boldout>
<boldin>• Mouth odour<boldout>
<boldin>Respiratory system<boldout>
  • Signs of respiratory distress – opioids, alcohol, barbiturates
<boldin>• Wheeze<boldout>
<boldin>• Stridor<boldout>
<boldin>Cardiovascular system<boldout>
<boldin>• Arrythmia<boldout>
<boldin>Abdomen<boldout>
<boldin>• Borborygmi<boldout>
<boldin>Integumentary<boldout>
<boldin>• Diaphoresis – organophosphate, salicylates<boldout>
<boldin>• Alopecia – thallium, arsenic<boldout>
<boldin>• Erythema – CO, elemental mercury<boldout>
<boldin>• Cyanosis – amidarone<boldout>
<boldin>Musculoskeletal system<boldout>
<boldin>• Muscle weakness<boldout>
<boldin>• Tremor or fasciculation<boldout>
<boldin>Central nervous system<boldout>
<boldin>• Papillary size<boldout>
<boldin>• Check for gag/cough reflex<boldout>
<boldin>• Ataxia – alcohol, barbiturates, CO, anticonvulsants<boldout>
<boldin>• Coma – opioids, barbiturates<boldout>
<boldin>• Seizure – organophosphate, antidepressants,<boldout>
<boldin>• Delirium/psychosis – anticholinergics, lithium, steroids<boldout>
  • Peripheral neuropathy – organophosphate, lead, arsenic, mercury…

<boldin>Investigations<boldout>
<boldin>• Random blood sugar<boldout>
<boldin>• Complete blood count<boldout>
<boldin>• Serum electrolytes<boldout>
<boldin>• Liver function test<boldout>
<boldin>• Renal function test<boldout>
<boldin>• Electrocardiography<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest X- ray<boldout>
<boldin>Management<boldout>
<boldin>General management<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>principle<boldout>
<boldin>• Decontamination<boldout>
<boldin>• Ipecac induced vomiting<boldout>
<boldin>• Catharetics<boldout>
<boldin>• Gastric lavage<boldout>
<boldin>• Activated charcoal<boldout>
<boldin>• Whole bowl irrigation<boldout>
<boldin>• Enhanced elimination<boldout>
<boldin>• Multiple dose of activated charcoal<boldout>
<boldin>• Urinary alkalization<boldout>
<boldin>• Dialysis<boldout>
<boldin>• Giving antidote<boldout>
<boldin>• Supportive<boldout>
<boldin>Decontamination<boldout>
  • The goal is to prevent absorption of toxic substance
  • The specific methods depend on the properties of the toxin and route of exposure
  • Regardless of the methods used the efficacy decrease with increasing the time since exposure especially after one hour of ingestion
  • GI decontamination strategies are effective in the first hour after ingestion
<boldin>• GI absorption may be delayed after<boldout>
  • Ingestion of agents that slow GI motility
<boldin>• Massive pill ingestion sustained release preparation<boldout>
  • Ingestion of agents that can form pharmacologic bezoars
  • So GI decontamination after one hour of ingestion may be considered in patients ingesting toxins with the above properties

<boldin>GI decontamination<boldout>
• <boldin>Syrup of<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>Ipecac<boldout>
  • Contains two emetic alkaloids that work both in CNS and locally in GIT to produce vomiting
  • Currently it is not used because of multiple adverse effect
<boldin>• The only indications are:<boldout>
  • If there is delay of a child to reach emergency medical facility for greater than an hour after toxic ingestion
  • If there is substantial risk of serious toxicity to the patient
  • If there is no alternative therapy to decrease GI absorption
  • If there are no contra indications for it
  • If the use of it do not adversely affect definitive therapy that may be provided at hospital
  • All of these conditions must be fulfilled to use it
• <boldin>Gastric<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>lavage<boldout>
  • It involves placing a tube into stomach to aspirate contents followed by flushing with a liquid usually normal saline
  • Useful if it is given with one hour of ingestion of poison
  • However, it is time consuming and may delay administration of more definitive treatment
<boldin>• Can induce bradycardia<boldout>
  • Under best circumstance only removes fraction of gastric contents
  • In most clinical scenarios are no longer recommended
• <boldin>Single dose activated<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>charcoal<boldout>
  • Charcoal is activated through heating to extreme temperature creating an extreme network of pores that provide a very large absorptive surface area
  • Many substances are adsorbed on to its surface; these prevent absorption from GI tract
  • Effective when given with 1 hour of ingestion
  • Substance poorly adsorbed by activated charcoal: - alcohols, caustics, cyanides, heavy metals, hydrocarbons, iron, lithium
  • It should be avoided after ingestion of caustic substances
  • Before administration air way should be intact
  • Dose: Children = 1gm/kg and Adolescent = 50-100 gm
  • Amount of water 8-10 times amount of charcoal
  • If possible, give the whole amount at once but can be given divided doses if the child cannot tolerate it
  • Can be mixed with caffeine free diet, coca or juice
• <boldin>Whole bowl<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>irrigation<boldout>
  • It involves installing large volume of polyethylene glycol electrolyte solution to cleans entire gastro-intestinal tract
  • Dose: Children = 35ml/kg/hour and Adolescents = 1-2 L/hour
<boldin>• It may have some success after:<boldout>
<boldin>• Ingestion of slowly absorbed substance<boldout>
<boldin>• Substance not well absorbed by charcoal<boldout>
<boldin>• Trans dermal patches and drug packets<boldout>
<boldin>• Can be combined with activated charcoal<boldout>
<boldin>• Administered through NG tube<boldout>
  • Has advantage because of having balancing electrolytes
  • Asses airway and examine abdomen before initiating it
  • Complications – vomiting, abdominal pain, bezoars formation
<boldin>Skin decontamination<boldout>
<boldin>• Remove all closing and personal effect<boldout>
  • Thoroughly clean all exposed areas with large amount of water
  • Use soap and water for oily substances
<boldin>• Take care to protect your self<boldout>
<boldin>Eye decontamination<boldout>
  • Rinse eye for 10-15 minutes with clean running water or normal saline
  • Evert the eyelid and insure that all surface are rinsed
  • Examine eye for signs of corneal damage
  • If conjuctival or corneal damage – refer to ophthalmologist
<boldin>Decontamination of inhaled poison<boldout>
<boldin>• Remove from the source<boldout>
<boldin>• Give oxygen supplementation if<boldout>
<boldin>• Respiratory distress or cyanosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Oxygen saturation ≤ 90%<boldout>
<boldin>Enhanced elimination<boldout>
• <boldin>Multiple dose activated<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>charcoal<boldout>
  • It enhances elimination via two proposed mechanism
<boldin>• Interruption of enterohepatic recirculation<boldout>
  • GI dialysis uses intestinal mucosa as a dialysis membrane and pull toxin from the tissue into intra luminal space
  • Dose: 0.5 gm/kg every 4-6 hours (for ≤24 hours) and continue until there is significant clinical improvement
  • Consider in drug poisonings like Phenobarbital, carbamazepine, phenytoin, digoxin, salicylates, theophylline
  • Examine airway and abdomen before each dose
• <boldin>Urinary<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>alkalization<boldout>
  • It enhances elimination some drugs that are weak acids by forming charged particles
  • Accomplished with continuous infusion of sodium bicarbonate containing IV fluids with goal of urinary PH 7.5-8.0
  • Serum PH should be closely monitored because serum PH />7.55 is potentially dangerous for cellular function
  • Most useful in managing salicylates and methotrexate toxicity

• <boldin>Dialysis<boldout>
  • Few drugs or toxins are removed by dialysis
  • Toxins amenable for dialysis have the following properties
<boldin>• Low volume of distribution (/<1L/kg)<boldout>
<boldin>• Low molecular weight<boldout>
<boldin>• Low degree of protein binding<boldout>
<boldin>• High degree of water solubility<boldout>
  • Such toxins are – methanol and ethyl glycol
  • Can also use for large symptomatic ingestion of salicylates, theophylline, bromide and lithium
  • Correct severe electrolyte disturbance and acid base derangement
<boldin>Antidote<boldout>
<boldin>• Available for few toxins<boldout>
  • Early and appropriate use is key element in managing poisoned child
<img:t20.png>
<italicin>Taken from Nelson text book of pediatrics 21<italicout><italicin>st<italicout><italicin> edition<italicout>
<boldin>Supportive<boldout>
  • The goal is to support the vital function of the patient until the patient can eliminate the toxin from the body
  • In any poisoned patient excellent supportive care and frequent clinical assessment are the key to effective management and improved outcome
<boldin>• These include:<boldout>
<boldin>• Airway support<boldout>
<boldin>• Ventilator management<boldout>
  • Appropriate and timely management of seizure, dysrhythmia, conduction delay, electrolyte and metabolic derangement
<boldin>• Blood pressure support<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 23: Skin Lesions
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Possible questions by the examiner:
<img:ch23_t01_14.jpg><boldout>
<boldin>• What do you see? Or<boldout>
<boldin>• Describe the lesion? Or<boldout>
<boldin>• Do scalp examination?<boldout>
<boldin>• Differential diagnosis for your findings<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you manage?<boldout>
<boldin>Introduction <boldout>
  • Diagnosis of skin diseases relies on proper identifications of symptoms and signs.
  • A dermatologic history is similar to other fields of medicine i.e. it has all components of both the history and physical examination.
  • In this topic we are concentrating on physical examination of a skin lesion.
  • It has mainly two components: Inspection and palpation.
<boldin>How to characterize a skin lesion?<boldout>
Skin lesions can be characterized based on its morphology, configuration, distribution, texture, color, and particular signs of a specific disease.

<boldin>Morphology<boldout>
