  • Classified as primary and secondary skin lesions
  • Primary skin lesions: Are either the first visible lesion or involve the initial skin changes
<boldin>• Secondary skin lesions:<boldout>
  • Secondary lesions of the skin represent evolved changes from the skin disorder, due to secondary manipulation or as a result of infection
<boldin>• Modified primary lesion<boldout>
<boldin>Primary skin lesions<boldout>
<boldin>Macule<boldout>
  • Non palpable lesion that vary in pigmentation to the surrounding skin
<boldin>• No elevation or depression<boldout>
<boldin>• Less than 10mm in diameter<boldout>
<boldin>Patch<boldin>: <boldout>A macule greater than 10mm<boldout>
<boldin>Papule<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpable discrete lesions<boldout>
<boldin>• May be isolated or grouped<boldout>
<boldin>• Less than 1cm in diameter<boldout>
<boldin>• Solid elevations with no visible fluid<boldout>
  • May be rounded, conical, flat topped or umbilicated
Nodule: A papule greater than 1cm in diameter
<boldin>Tumor<boldout>
  • Large nodule that is suspected to be neoplastic in origin or
<boldin>• Any large deep seated swelling<boldout>
<boldin>Plaque<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpable lesions<boldout>
<boldin>• Flat surface at the top<boldout>
<boldin>• Greater than 1cm in diameter<boldout>
<boldin>Vesicle<boldout>
<boldin>• Raised fluid filled lesion<boldout>
  • ≤ 1cm in diameter
Bulla or Blister: Vesicle greater than 1cm in diameter
Pustule: Vesicle or bulla containing purulent material
<boldin>Urticaria<boldout>
<boldin>• Also known as wheal or hive<boldout>
  • Are irregularly elevated edematous skin areas that are often erythematous
  • The borders of a wheal are sharp but not stable
<boldin><italicin>  <italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin>	<italicout><boldout>Patches                   papules                               nodules                           plaque
<img:ch23_t01_15.png>

<img:ch23_t01_16.png>
<boldin>Vesicle                                 bulla                          pustules                           urticaria
<img:ch23_t01_17.jpg>

<img:ch23_t01_18.jpg>

<img:ch23_t01_19.png><boldout>

<boldin>Secondary skin lesions <boldout>
Scale<boldin>: <boldout>Visible flake of the upper part of the skin
Crust<boldin>: <boldout>Dried pus, serum, tissue fluid, cellular debris or blood
<boldin>Erosion<boldout>
<boldin>• Superficial loss of epidermal continuity<boldout>
<boldin>• Usually heal without scarring<boldout>
<boldin>Ulcer<boldout>
  • Focal loss of the epidermis that extending to the dermis
<boldin>• May heal with scarring<boldout>
<boldin>Excoriation<boldout>
  • Superficial, often linear, skin erosion caused by scratching
<boldin>• Usually involves only the epidermis<boldout>
Fissure: A linear deep skin split extending into the dermis
<boldin>Purpura<boldout>
  • As a result of bleeding into the skin /& have a red-purple color
<boldin>• May be flat or palpable<boldout>
<boldin>Petechiae: Small purpura /<2-3 mm<boldout>
<boldin>Atrophy<boldout>
  • Decreased skin thickness due to skin thinning
  • May appear dry and wrinkled, resembling cigarette paper
<boldin>Scar<boldout>
  • Abnormal fibrous tissue that replaces normal tissue after skin injury
  • Can be thin, depressed, and atrophic; raised and hypertrophic; or flat and pliable
  • If it is beyond the area of the original trauma – called keloid
Telangiectasia: A focus of small, permanently dilated blood vessels
Scale                                Crust                        Erosion                           ulcer                         Telangiectasia                  Excoriation                    Fissure                          Petechie
<img:ch23_t01_20.png>

<img:ch23_t01_21.jpg>

<img:ch23_t01_22.png>

<img:ch23_t01_23.jpg>

<boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout>
<boldin>Purpura                                     Atrophy                                   Scar (flat)                 Hypertrophic scar
<img:ch23_t01_24.jpg>

<img:ch23_t01_25.png><boldout>


<boldin>Configuration<boldout>
Linear<boldin>: <boldout>Lesions take on the shape of a straight line
Annular: A lesion appears in a ring shaped form with central clearing
<boldin>Nummular:<boldout>
  • Used for a lesion having a shape of a coin
<boldin>• Also called discoid lesion<boldout>
<boldin>• No central clearing<boldout>
Target or iris: Lesions appear as rings with central duskiness
Serpiginous: Lesions have linear, branched, and curving element
Reticulated: Lesions have a lacy or networked pattern
<boldin>Herpetiform: When lesions arranged in groups<boldout>
<boldin>Zosteriform:<boldout>
<boldin>• Used for lesions having dermatomal distribution<boldout>
  • A dermatome is area of the skin innervated by a single sensory trunk of spinal or cranial nerve

linear                    Annular                      Nummular                       Target or iris
<img:ch23_t01_26.jpg>

<img:ch23_t01_27.jpg>

<img:ch23_t01_28.png>

<boldin><italicin>Serpiginous                       Reticulated                    Herpetiform                    Zosteriform <italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin>	<italicout><boldout>
<img:ch23_t01_29.jpg>

<img:ch23_t01_30.png>
<boldin>Location and distribution<boldout>
Location: Are particular body parts affected (palms, soles, scalp, mucosal membranes, extensor or flexor)?
<boldin>Distribution<boldout>
<boldin>• Are lesions single or multiple?<boldout>
  • Is the distribution random or patterned, symmetric or asymmetric?
  • Are lesions on sun-exposed or protected skin?
  • Photodistributed – lesions confined to areas exposed to the sun

<boldin>Color<boldout>
<boldin>Erythematous<boldout>
<boldin>• For lesions having reddish appearance<boldout>
  • As a result of increased blood flow
<boldin>Hyperpigmentation<boldout>
<boldin>• Increased skin pigment<boldout>
  • The lesion appears darker than the normal skin
<boldin>Hypopigmentation<boldout>
<boldin>• Decreased skin pigment<boldout>
  • The lesion appears whiter than the normal skin
<boldin>Depigmentation<boldout>
<boldin>• Total loss of skin pigment<boldout>
<boldin>• The lesion appears chalky white<boldout>
<boldin>Erythematous lesion     Hyperpigmented           Hypopigmented               Depigmented
<img:ch23_t01_31.jpg>

<img:ch23_t01_32.jpg><boldout>

<boldin>Texture<boldout>
<boldin>Lichenification<boldout>
  • Increased skin markings and thickening with induration
  • Secondary to chronic inflammation caused by scratching or other irritation
Verrucous: Lesions have an irregular, pebbly, or rough surface
<boldin>Induration<boldout>
<boldin>• Deep thickening of the skin<boldout>
  • As a result of edema, inflammation, or infiltration by cancer
<boldin>Umbilicated: Lesions having a central indentation<boldout>
<boldin>Particular clinical signs<boldout>
Dermatographism: The appearance of an urticarial wheal after focal pressure.
Darier/'s sign: Refers to rapid swelling of a lesion when stroked.
Nikolsky/'s sign: Epidermal shearing that occurs with gentle lateral pressure on seemingly uninvolved skin.
<boldin>Auspitz sign:<boldout>
  • The appearance of pinpoint bleeding after scale is removed from plaques
  • A clinical reflection of elongated vessels in the dermal papillae
Koebner phenomenon: Describes the development of lesions within areas of trauma.

<boldin>Dermatographism<boldin>                       <boldout>Nikolsky/'s sign<boldout>
<img:ch23_t01_33.png>





<boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout>


Auspitz sign - Note point of bleeding after scale is removed

<boldin><boldout>
<boldin>Example 1<boldout>
<img:ch23_t01_34.jpg>
<boldin>• Describe the lesion?<boldout>
  • What could be the possible differential diagnosis?
<boldin>• What could be the etiology?<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you investigate?<boldout>
<boldin>• How do you manage?<boldout>
<boldin>Description<boldout>
  • There is a single whitish/grey scaly well demarcated circular patch of scalp alopecia over the parietoocipital area of the scalp
<boldin>• Most likely: Tinea capitis<boldout>
<boldin>Differential diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Tinea capitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Psoriasis<boldout>
<boldin>• Seborrheic dermatitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Bacterial scalp infection<boldout>
<boldin>• Alopecia areata<boldout>
<boldin>• Trichotillomania<boldout>
<boldin>Tinea Capitis <boldout>
<boldin>Introduction <boldout>
  • A superficial dermatophyte infection of the scalp
<boldin>• Caused by:<boldout>
<boldin>• Trichophyton tonsurans – the most common<boldout>
<boldin>• Microsporum canis<boldout>
<boldin>• Microsporum audouinii<boldout>
<boldin>• Trichophyton schoenleinii<boldout>
<boldin>• Microsporum gypseum…<boldout>
  • Particularly common in black children age 4-14 years
<boldin>• Transmission could be from:<boldout>
  • Human to human; e.g. T. tonsurans - acquired by contact with infected hairs
  • Animal to human; e.g. M. canis - acquired from cats and dogs
  • After an incubation period of several days, the fungal hyphae grow in to the hair shaft and follicle forming ectothrix or endothrix
  • This cause broken hair, scaling and host inflammatory response

<boldin>Clinical manifestation <boldout>
<boldin>• It varies with the infecting organism<boldout>
  • four different types of manifestations
• <boldin>Grey patch<boldout>
<boldin>• Occurs in epidemic and endemic forms<boldout>
<boldin>• Patches of scalp alopecia with scale<boldout>
  • The infection begins with an erythematous, scaling, well- demarcated patch on the scalp that spreads centrifugally
  • One or several lesions may be present
<boldin>• Common in M. canis<boldout>
<boldin>• Florence bright green under Woods Light<boldout>
• <boldin>Black dot <boldout>
  • Characterized by many small circular patches of alopecia in which hairs are broken of close to the hair follicle
  • Hairs within the patches break off flush with the scalp; detritus within the follicular opening formerly occupied by the hair appears as a black dot
  • One or several lesions may be present
<boldin>• Common in T. tonsurans<boldout>
  • Do not fluoresce green under Woods Light
• <boldin>Kerion<boldout>
<boldin>• A severe inflammatory response<boldout>
  • Boggy, purulent, inflamed nodules and plaques extremely painful
  • Fever, pain and regional adenopathy are common
  • May result in permanent scarring and alopecia
  • canis or Microsporum gypsum may also cause keroin
<boldin>• Common in T. tonsurance<boldout>
• <boldin>Favus <boldout>
<boldin>• A chronic form of tinea capitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Caused by the fungus Trichophyton schoenleinii<boldout>
  • It starts as yellowish red papules at the opening of hair follicles
  • Papules expand /& coalesce to form cup-shaped yellowish crusted
  • Fluoresce dull green under a Wood lamp
<boldin>Diagnosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Wood lamp<boldout>
<boldin>• KOH preparation<boldout>
<boldin>• Culture<boldout>
<boldin>Management <boldout>
<boldin>Oral griseofulvin microcrystalline<boldout>
  • 20-25 mg/kg/day with a maximum daily dose of 1000 mg or
  • 10-15 mg/kg/day with a maximum daily dose of 750 mg if the ultramicrosize form is used
  • The recommended treatment for all forms of tinea capitis
  • Absorption of griseofulvin is enhanced by the ingestion of a fatty meal
  • Treatment for one month after a negative culture result minimizes the risk of recurrence
  • A minimum of 8 week of treatment is usually required
<boldin>Oral terbinafine<boldout>
  • Effective at a dosage of 3-6 mg/kg/24 hr
<boldin>• For 4-6 week<boldout>
<boldin>• Has limited activity against M. canis<boldout>
<boldin>Oral itraconazole<boldout>
  • Useful in instances of griseofulvin resistance, intolerance or allergy
  • Given at a dosage of 3-5 mg/kg/24 hr with food
<boldin>• For 4-6 week<boldout>
  • Itraconazole is not approved by the FDA for treatment of dermatophyte infections in the pediatric population
  • Capsules are preferable to the syrup, which may cause diarrhea
<boldin>Topical therapy<boldout>
  • 5% selenium sulfide, zinc pyrithione or ketoconazole shampoo
<boldin>• Alone is ineffective<boldout>
  • But it may be an important adjunct because it may decrease the shedding of spores and should be recommended in all patients
  • Treat both the patient and potential carriers with a sporicidal shampoo
  • It is not necessary to shave the scalp

topics.title: Chapter 24: Prescription Writing
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Introduction <boldout><boldin>	<boldout>
  • It is the prescribers order to prepare or dispense a specific treatment usually medication for a specific diagnosis
  • It is writing a safe, appropriate and effective drug for the diagnosis to the right patient and providing information to the patient how, when, how long to use the drug, about the drug side effects and its effectiveness
  • It is a legal document for both the prescriber and the pharmacist
  • A number of drugs that can be purchased without a prescription are termed as over the counter (OTC) drugs. These drugs are safer and can be self-medicated. It includes: antacids, antidiarrheal, antipyretics, and antiseptics
  • A drug that requires a prescription from a licensed prescriber to be dispended by a pharmacist is termed legend drug

<boldin>Rational steps of prescription writing<boldout>
<boldin>• Make a specific diagnosis<boldout>
  • Consider the Pathophysiologic implications of the diagnosis
<boldin>• Select the specific therapeutic objective<boldout>
<boldin>• Select the drug of choice<boldout>
<boldin>• Determine the appropriate dose of regimen<boldout>
  • Device a plan for monitoring the drug action and determine the end point for therapy
  • Plan a program of patient education – at least the following information should be addressed to the patient
<boldin>Effects of the drug<boldout>
<boldin>• Why the drug is needed<boldout>
  • Which symptoms will disappear and which will not?
  • When the effect is expected to start and what will happen if the drug is taken incorrectly or not at all

<boldin>Side effects<boldout>
<boldin>• Which side effects may occur<boldout>
<boldin>• How to recognize them<boldout>
<boldin>• How long they will continue<boldout>
<boldin>• How serious they are<boldout>
<boldin>• What action to be taken<boldout>
<boldin>Instructions<boldout>
<boldin>• How the drug should be taken?<boldout>
<boldin>• When it should be taken<boldout>
<boldin>• How long the treatment should continue?<boldout>
<boldin>• How the drug should be stored?<boldout>
  • What to do with left over drugs
<boldin>Warnings<boldout>
  • When the drug should not be taken?
<boldin>• What is the maximum dose?<boldout>
  • Why the full treatment course should be taken?
<boldin>Future consultations<boldout>
<boldin>• When to come back<boldout>
  • In what circumstance the patient should come earlier
  • What information the physician will need at the next appointment

<boldin>Everything clear? <boldout>
  • Ask the patient whether everything is understood
  • Ask the patient to repeat the most important information
  • Ask whether the patient has any more questions
<boldin>Elements of prescription<boldout>
A prescription paper has 3 parts with its own elements
<boldin>• Heading<boldout>
  • Patients status: Full name, sex, age, weight, card No., region, zone, woreda, kebele, house No. Tel no.,
<boldin>• Emergency, inpatient, Outpatient, ward<boldout>
<boldin>• Diagnosis<boldout>
  • Body: Drug name, strength, dosage form, dose, frequency, duration, quantity, how to use and other information
<boldin>• Closing<boldout>
  • Prescribers: - name, qualification, registration, signature, date
  • Dispensers (Evaluator’s, cashier’s, counselor’s): - name, qualification, registration, signature, date

<boldin>Prescribing errors <boldout>
<boldin>• Omission of information<boldout>
  • Poor prescription writing: illegible handwriting or misplaced or ambiguous decimal point
<boldin>• Inappropriate drug prescription: due to<boldout>
  • Failure to recognize contraindications imposed by other diseases the patient may have
  • Failure to obtain information about the drugs that the patient is taking
  • Failure to recognize possible incompatibility between the drugs that may react each other

<boldin>Example <boldout>
This is Amen Adonay Andualem a 4-year-old male patient from Gondar kebele 03 presented with high grade intermittent fever of one week duration associated with ear discharge on his right ear and ear pooling. On physical examination he is febrile, tachycardic, active discharge through his right ear, tragus tenderness and normal anthropometric measurements with weight of 16 kg.
What will be the management?  (NB. For patient’s age less than 5-year-old, it is better to prescribe po medication in syrup form).

<boldin>When we write on the chart:<boldout>
<boldin>Ass’t<boldout> – Acute Otitis Media
<boldin>Plan<boldout>
<boldin>• Cold compression when needed<boldout>
  • Paracetamol syrup 6.5ml (120mg/5ml) Po QID for #2 days
  • Amoxilin syrup 8.5ml (250mg/5ml) Po TID or 12.5 ml Po BID for #10 days

<boldin>Sign ……………….<boldout>
<boldin>Name ………………<boldout>
<boldin>How to calculate the volume of the syrup and the dose of the drug?<boldout>
  • First calculate the dose needed for the patient per dose for the given kg and then try to know the possible preparation of the dose per the given volume. For the above example
<boldin>• Dose:<boldout>
  • Paracetamol/dose = 10 – 15 mg per Kg per dose; If we take 10mg per kg per dose = (10mg x 16)/dose = 160mg/dose
  • Amoxilin per day = 80mg x weight(kg) = (80mg x 16)/day = 1280mg/day
  • = 1280mg ÷ 3 = 426.7mg/dose
<boldin>• Preparation<boldout>
<boldin>• Paracetamol: 250mg/5ml, 120mg/5ml<boldout>
<boldin>• Amoxilin: 250mg/5ml, 125mg/5ml<boldout>
<boldin>• Which preparation should be used?<boldout>
  • We should approximate the calculated dose to the nearby preparation
  • g. the calculated dose of PCM is 160 mg: 250 – 160 = 90; 160 – 120 = 40; i.e. 160 ~ 120; so we use the preparation of 120mg/5ml.
<boldin>• How to convert?<boldout>
  • 
  • = 6.67ml ~ 6.5ml
  • = 8.53ml ~ 8.5ml

<boldin>Common abbreviations used during prescription writing<boldout>
<boldin>Ac		Before a meal<boldout>
<boldin>BID 	 	Twice per day<boldout>
<boldin>Im 		Intramuscular<boldout>
<boldin>IV  		Intravenous<boldout>
<boldin>PC 		After a meal<boldout>
<boldin>PO  		By mouth<boldout>
<boldin>PR  		Per rectum<boldout>
<boldin>PRN 		When needed<boldout>
<boldin>Q 		Every<boldout>
<boldin>Qam 		Every morning<boldout>
<boldin>QH 		Every hour<boldout>
<boldin>Qhs 	 	Every night at bed time<boldout>
<boldin>QID 		Four times per day<boldout>
<boldin>QOD 		Every other day<boldout>
<boldin>Rx 		Take<boldout>
<boldin>SC, SQ  	Subcutaneous<boldout>
<boldin>Stat 	  	At once<boldout>
<boldin>Sup, supp  	Suppository<boldout>
<boldin>Tab 		Tablet<boldout>
<boldin>Tbsp 		Table spoon<boldout>
<boldin>TID 		Three times per day<boldout>
<boldin>Tsp 		Tea spoon<boldout>
<boldin>VAG 		Vaginal<boldout>




<boldin>Section – two<boldout>
<boldin>Common Pediatrics<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>Procedures<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 25: Peripheral Iv Line Insertion
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Indications<boldout>
<boldin>• Administration of fluids and electrolytes<boldout>
<boldin>• Administration of intravenous medications<boldout>
<boldin>• Administration of blood and blood products<boldout>
<boldin>• Blood sampling<boldout>
<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
<boldin>Absolute<boldout>
<boldin>• Infected site<boldout>
<boldin>• Burnt site<boldout>
<boldin>• Injured site<boldout>
<boldin>Relative<boldout>
<boldin>• Paralyzed extremity<boldout>
<boldin>• Massive edematous extremity<boldout>
  • Distal to an injured organ – Don’t use lower extremities when treating abdominal injuries

<boldin>Equipments<boldout><boldin>	<boldout>
<boldin>• Gloves<boldout>
<boldin>• Tourniquet or rubber band<boldout>
<boldin>• Tape and occlusive transparent dressing<boldout>
<boldin>• Alcohol wipes<boldout>
<boldin>• Povidone or chlorhexidine<boldout>
<boldin>• Syringe filled with injectable saline<boldout>
<boldin>• Gauze pads<boldout>
  • IV device: catheter or butterfly of appropriate size to fit the patient /& the task
<boldin>• Topical anesthetic cream<boldout>
  • Ultrasound guiding equipment (if available /& if trained in its use)
<boldin>Common sites<boldout>
<boldin>• Dorsum of the hand<boldout>
  • Radial side of the hand (cephalic vein)
  • Antecubital fossa (cephalic vein – common site for age />2 months)
  • Saphenous vein at the medial aspect of the ankle
  • Dorsum of the feet (dorsal Veneous plexus)
<boldin>• External jugular vein<boldout>
  • Scalp veins: The frontal superficial, temporal posterior, auricular, supra-orbital and posterior facial veins can be used. Often for ages /<2 years but best in young infants.

<boldin>Procedures<boldout>
<boldin>• Wash your hands thoroughly<boldout>
<boldin>• Assemble the equipment at the bedside<boldout>
<boldin>• Observe universal precautions<boldout>
<boldin>• Follow the patient’s specific isolation instructions<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Upper and Lower Extremities<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Apply tourniquet<boldout>
  • Identify the blood vessel by palpation, visualization, trans illumination, or ultrasound
<boldin>• Release the tourniquet, cleanse the site<boldout>
  • Inspect the integrity of the catheter/stylet assembly
  • Flush the catheter and the connecting tube with saline (omit this step if you intend to draw blood through this catheter)
<boldin>• Reapply the tourniquet<boldout>
  • Use your non dominant hand to apply traction on the skin linearly or circumferentially in order to stabilize the vein
  • Enter the skin at a 30- to 45-degree angle proximal to or alongside the vein
  • Reduce the angle as you advance the catheter and enter the vein
  • Watch for blood flashback in the hub of the catheter
  • Stabilize the catheter with the thumb and middle finger of your dominant hand and advance the catheter over the stylet using the tip of your index finger
<boldin>• Remove the stylet<boldout>
  • Do not reinsert the stylet once it has been removed; it may damage the catheter
<boldin>• Release the tourniquet<boldout>
  • Connect the extension tubing and saline-filled syringe to the catheter
  • Gently flush the catheter; observe for swelling, mottling, or color changes in the extremity
  • Secure the IV with occlusive transparent dressing and tape
  • Make a small loop in the IV tubing and tape it across
  • Attach the line to an IV infusion assembly and turn the pump on
  • Dispose of all sharp instruments in the proper secure container
<boldin><italicin>External Jugular Vein<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Bundle infant or child<boldout>
  • Have an assistant position the patient in the Trendelenburg position with the head toward you
  • Turn the patient’s head away from the jugular vein you intend to use
  • Elevate the patient’s shoulders and neck (15 – 30o )
  • Apply traction with your non dominant hand to the skin over the jugular vein
  • Nick the skin with a large bore needle at a shallow angle below or alongside the vein in order to facilitate the catheter’s entry
  • Insert the catheter through the puncture and advance subcutaneously a few millimeters before entering the vein
  • Synchronize your entry with the child’s breathing
  • Enter the vein during exhalation in a spontaneously breathing patient to avoid air embolus
  • Enter the vein during a positive pressure breath if the patient is on positive pressure ventilation
  • Watch for blood flashback in the hub
  • Stabilize the catheter assembly and advance the catheter over the stylet
  • Withdraw the stylet and occlude the catheter with your gloved thumb to avoid air embolus
  • Connect the tubing and saline-filled syringe, draw back and flush
  • Secure the line with occlusive transparent dressing and tape
  • Reposition the child and retest the line
  • The jugular vein has a number of valves that can obstruct the catheter when the neck position is altered
  • Traction may be needed or the catheter may need to be withdrawn slightly to ensure proper function

<boldin><italicin>Scalp IV<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Find a suitable scalp veins<boldout>
  • Shave the area and clean with antiseptic
  • Palpate for pulse in order to avoid inadvertent arterial cannulation
  • Apply traction with your non dominant hand
  • Fill the butterfly set tubing with saline
  • Disconnect the syringe and leave the end of the tubing open
  • Introduce the butterfly needle as described above.
  • Blood flowing back slowly through the tubing indicates that the needle is in the vein
  • Secure the IV with transparent occlusive dressing and tape
<boldin>• Attach to an infusion assembly<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout><italicin>:<italicout>
<italicin>Failed peripheral IV securing is defined as no establishment after 90 seconds of trial, so that other means of IV access should be searched.<italicout>

<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Infection<boldout>
<boldin>• Hematoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Extravasation<boldout>
<boldin>• Compartment syndrome (6p’s)<boldout>
<boldin>• Pain<boldout>
<boldin>• Pallor<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulselessness<boldout>
<boldin>• Paresthesia<boldout>
<boldin>• Paralysis and<boldout>
<boldin>• Pershingly cool<boldout>
  • Severe vasoconstriction: If vasoactive medications are infused through a peripheral IV and extravasate
<boldin>• Venous thrombosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Embolization of air or catheter fragment<boldout>
<boldin>• Local ischemia<boldout>
<boldin>Monitoring<boldout>
<boldin>Peripheral IV in Extremity<boldout>
  • Compare extremity’s color and temperature; watch for congestion and swelling
<boldin>• Watch for signs of occlusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpate pulses<boldout>
<boldin>• Ensure skin integrity<boldout>
<boldin>• Check tightness of dressing<boldout>
  • Inspect IV tubing for blood or precipitation
  • Monitor IV pump for increased resistance that may indicate clotting
<boldin>• Watch for pump malfunction<boldout>
<boldin>Jugular Vein<boldout>
<boldin>• Monitor for swelling<boldout>
<boldin>• Assess for signs of occlusion<boldout>
<boldin>Scalp IV<boldout>
<boldin>• Monitor for swelling and blanching<boldout>
<boldin>• Watch for signs of occlusion<boldout>
<boldin>• Assess skin integrity<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 26: Indications
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Diagnostic<boldout>
<boldin>• Suspected CNS infections<boldout>
<boldin>• Suspected subarachnoid haemorrhage<boldout>
<boldin>• Metabolic studies<boldout>
<boldin>• Aminoacidopathies<boldout>
<boldin>• Neurotransmitter disorders<boldout>
<boldin>• Undiagnosed movement disorders<boldout>
<boldin>• Undiagnosed infantile or pediatric epilepsy<boldout>
<boldin>• Demyelinating disorders – <italicin>multiple sclerosis<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Contrast media instillation<boldout>
<boldin>Therapeutic<boldout>
<boldin>• Instillation of chemotherapy or spinal anesthesia<boldout>
  • Removal of cerebro-spinal fluid (CSF) in the treatment of intracranial hypertension

<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
<boldin>Absolute<boldout>
  • Midline shift on imaging is the real contraindication
  • Raised intracranial pressure (with a pressure gradient across the CNS compartments, papilledema)
<boldin>• Focal neurologic deficit<boldout>
<boldin>• Local developmental abnormality, e.g., Myelomeningocele<boldout>
<boldin>• Platelet count /< 20,000<boldout>
<boldin>Relative<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiopulmonary instability<boldout>
<boldin>• Bleeding diathesis<boldout>
<boldin>• International normalized ratio (INR) />1.4<boldout>
<boldin>• Local skin infection<boldout>
Children with the following conditions should have CT scan before Lumbar Puncture (LP)
<boldin>• Altered mental status<boldout>
<boldin>• Focal neurologic signs<boldout>
<boldin>• Recent seizure<boldout>
  • Risk of brain abscess (immunocompromised, CHD with right to left shunt)
<boldin>• Papilledema<boldout>
<boldin>Equipments<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile gloves<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile drapes<boldout>
<boldin>• Povodine – iodine solution<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile sponges<boldout>
  • Manometer (typically used in patients />2 years old)
  • Lidocaine 1% without epinephrine and topical anaesthetic
<boldin>• Syringe<boldout>
  • 22 gauge spinal needle: 0.5 inch for neonate, 1.5 inches /<2 years old, 2.5 inches for 2-12 years old, 3,5 inches for /> 12 years old
  • Manometer ( for children older than 2 years)
  • Sterile collection tubes (sufficient number for studies): There should be at least three tubes – for biochemical, culture and cytology
  • 3-way stopcock
<boldin>• Flexible tubing<boldout>
<boldin>Patient preparation<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile technique<boldout>
<boldin>• Povidone-iodine preparation<boldout>
  • Sterile drape with fenestration over mid lumbar spine
<boldin>• Sedation, if needed<boldout>
  • Connect 3-way stopcock to ﬂexible tubing and manometer at 90 degrees from each other
  • Free end of tubing will connect to hub of needle
<boldin>Positioning<boldout>
<boldin>Lateral recumbent<boldout>
<boldin>• Back arched in extreme lordosis<boldout>
  • Spine should be as perfectly horizontal as possible
  • Sacral plane should be as vertical as possible
<boldin>Sitting<boldout>
  • For children who have the potential for developing respiratory compromise because of hyper flexion of the neck in the lateral recumbent position
  • Does not permit accurate measurement of opening pressure
<boldin>Procedures<boldout>
<boldin>• Palpate iliac crests as landmarks<boldout>
  • Place lumbar puncture needle between interspaces L4–L5
  • Angle the needle tip approximately 15–30 degrees from perpendicular to plane of back in rostral direction, aiming toward umbilicus
  • Needle remains fixed in horizontal plane to back
  • Advance needle slowly until light resistance (a pop) is felt
  • Remove stylet and check for CSF flow
  • If no CSF flows, continue to advance the needle slowly
  • If CSF flows, connect flexible tubing to hub of lumbar puncture needle
  • Allow CSF to flow through tubing into manometer
  • Hold base of manometer and stopcock at level of heart
<boldin>• Straighten the patient’s back and legs<boldout>
  • When CSF stops advancing along manometer, measure opening pressure at meniscus
<boldin>• Collect CSF for studies<boldout>
<boldin>• Measure closing pressure, if needed.<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout><italicin>: LP can be done at L2/L3, L3/L4, and L4/L5 interspaces depending on the age<italicout>
<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Post puncture headache<boldout>
<boldin>• Infection<boldout>
<boldin>• Cerebral herniation<boldout>
<boldin>• Spinal Hematoma<boldout>
<boldin>• Epidermoid tumor<boldout>
<boldin>• Back pain<boldout>
<boldin>Follow up<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile dressing<boldout>
  • Older adolescents should rest in bed for 1-3 hours
<boldin>CSF Analysis<boldout>
<boldin>• Opening pressure<boldout>
  • Direct measure of intrathecal pressure and indirect measure of intracranial pressure
  • Patient must be relaxed and back and legs extended
<boldin>• Appearance<boldout>
<boldin>• Crystal clear – normal, viral infection<boldout>
  • Hemorrhagic – local trauma, SAH, hemorrhagic infection, HSV meningitis…
  • Xanthocromia – considered as hemorrhage till proven, can be due to raised protein
  • Turbid - when WBC count is />200–400/mm3
<boldin>• Cell count and differentials<boldout>
<boldin>• Normal<boldout>
  • Neonate: ≤ 20-30 cells with lymphocyte dominant
  • Infants: /< 8 cells with lymphocyte dominant
<boldin>• Elevations suggest inflammatory process (often infective)<boldout>
  • Elevations of polymorphonuclear leukocytes suggest - acute bacterial infection or early viral infection
  • Elevations of lymphocytes suggest TB, viral, fungal or protozoal infection
  • Pleocytosis may be absent in severe overwhelming sepsis and meningitis
<boldin>• Erythrocytes (normal is 0): if elevated<boldout>
  • Hemorrhage (not etiologically specific, acute or subacute)
<boldin>• Hemorrhagic infection (herpes)<boldout>
  • Evaluate spun sample for hemochromatosis (subacute hemorrhage)
  • Protein (normal values are based on age)
  • Elevations suggest - inflammatory or degenerative process
<boldin>• Bacterial meningitis is usually significantly elevated<boldout>
  • Newborns may have elevations of protein at baseline
<boldin>• Glucose<boldout>
  • Decrease in CSF glucose is highly suggestive of - active bacterial infection because metabolism rate is increased and bacteria uses for itself
  • Decrease in CSF glucose also seen in glucose transporter defect (epilepsy)
  • Normal or slight decrement in viral infection, since viruses have no metabolic machinery – they cannot use it
<boldin>• Gram stain<boldout>
<boldin>• AFB<boldout>
  • Culture - usually as high as 10ml of CSF is recommended
<boldin>• Studies are selected on clinical suspicion and indication; examples include the following:<boldout>
<boldin>• Herpes polymerase chain reaction<boldout>
<boldin>• India ink stains<boldout>
<boldin>• CSF neurotransmitters (movement disorders and epilepsy)<boldout>
<boldin>• Amino acids (for aminoacidopathies, especially glutaricaciduria)<boldout>
<boldin>• Lactate (mitochondrial and energy metabolism disorders)<boldout>
<boldin>• Myelin basic protein (demyelinating diseases)<boldout>
<boldin>• Oligoclonal bands (demyelinating diseases)<boldout>
<boldin>• Cytology (neoplasm, usually requires larger volume)<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 27: Bone Marrow Aspiration And Biopsy
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Indications<boldout>
<boldin>• Pancytopenia<boldout>
<boldin>• Unexplained anemia and/leucopoenia, or thrombocytopenia<boldout>
<boldin>• Acute or chronic leukemia (aspiration only)<boldout>
<boldin>• Fever of unknown origin<boldout>
<boldin>• Myelodysplasia<boldout>
<boldin>• Myeloproliferative disease<boldout>
<boldin>• Non-Hodgkin or Hodgkin lymphoma<boldout>
  • Childhood solid tumors (including sarcoma, Wilms tumour, neuroblastoma, germ cell tumour)
  • Bone marrow failure (including acquired aplastic anemia, Fanconi anemia, Diamond- Blackfan syndrome)
  • Monitoring during chemotherapy or following stem cell transplantation (aspiration only)
<boldin>• Storage disease<boldout>
<boldin>Contraindications <boldout>
<boldin>Relative<boldout>
  • Congenital factor deﬁciency or acquired coagulation defect
<boldin>• Anticoagulation with warfarin or heparin<boldout>
  • Severe thrombocytopenia: Isolated severe thrombocytopenia usually is not a contraindication
  • Infection/fracture or prior radiation at sample site
<boldin>Common sites<boldout>
<boldin>• Posterior superior iliac spine<boldout>
<boldin>• Anterior iliac crest in obese patients<boldout>
  • Anterio-medial surface of the tibia in infants /< 3 mon of age
<boldin>Equipments<boldout>
<boldin>Site Preparation<boldout>
  • 10% povidone-iodine
<boldin>• Alcohol preparation pads or swabs<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile gloves, gown, and drape<boldout>
  • Spinal and subcutaneous needles, 20 to 26 gauge
  • 1% lidocaine hydrochloride, injection
  • 4% sodium bicarbonate, injection, USP
<boldin>Marrow Aspiration and Biopsy<boldout>
  • Sodium heparin, injection, 1000 USP units/ml, preservative free
  • Bone marrow aspiration needles (15 /& 18 gauge, adjustable lengths)
  • Bone marrow biopsy needles (11 /& 13 gauge, 4 or 2inches in length)
<boldin>• Sterile syringes, 10 to 20 ml<boldout>
  • Container with ﬁxative for trephine biopsy specimen
  • Vacutainers; one for sodium heparin and one for ethylenediaminetetra acetic acid (EDTA)
<boldin>• Gauze sponges<boldout>
<boldin>• Bandages<boldout>
  • Slides – at least 3 slides are needed
<boldin>Patient positioning<boldout>
  • If the posterior iliac crest is used, the patient is placed in the right or left
  • Decubitus position, with the hips flexed and the knees drawn up
  • If the anterior iliac crest is used, the patient is placed in the supine position with the hips and knees flexed
  • Occasionally, thin patients who do not receive general anesthesia may be placed in the prone position
<boldin>Procedures<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Bone marrow aspiration<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Clean the site with povidone-iodine followed by alcohol<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>swab<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Place sterile<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>drape<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Inject buffered lidocaine intradermally with a subcutaneous needle to produce a small<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>wheal<italicout>
  • <italicin>Use a larger bore needle to push through the skin and subcutaneous tissue and inject 2-3 ml (maximum 3g/kg/dose along the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>periosteum<italicout>
  • <italicin>Hold the bone aspirate needle horizontally using the index <italicout>ﬁ<italicin>nger near the tip of the needle for<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>control<italicout>
  • <italicin>Advance the needle through the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and the surface of the cortical bone with steady pressure and a twisting<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>motion<italicout>
  • <italicin>An abrupt decrease in resistance occurs when the needle penetrates the cortex and enters the spongy marrow<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>cavity<italicout>
  • <italicin>Advance the needle 1 cm more before the stylet is<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>removed<italicout>
  • <italicin>Attach a 10-mL or 20-ml syringe to the end of the needle and pull the plunger back quickly to aspirate approximately 0.25 ml of bone<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>marrow<italicout>
  • <italicin>If an aspirate is not obtained, replace the stylet and advance or reposition the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>needle<italicout>
  • <italicin>This <italicout>ﬁ<italicin>rst pull contains the marrow particles or spicules that should be used for preparing initial<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>smears<italicout>
  • <italicin>A heparinized, larger syringe (30 ml) may be used to obtain<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>additional marrow for cytogenetic analysis, <italicout>ﬂ<italicin>ow cytometry, and other<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>studies.<italicout>

<boldin><italicin>Bone marrow<italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin>biopsy<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>The trephine biopsy is the preferred method to evaluate cellularity and detect bone marrow metastasis in lymphoma and many childhood solid tumors<italicout>
  • <italicin>Biopsy specimen is obtained through the same incision site<italicout>
  • <italicin>Hold the biopsy needle in the same manner as the aspiration needle but angle it to sample a different area from the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>aspiration<italicout>
  • <italicin>Advance the needle with steady pressure to the periosteum and twist into the surface of the cortical<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>bone<italicout>
  • <italicin>Remove the obturator and push the needle through the cortex using a rotating, twisting motion until decreased resistance is<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>met<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Advance the needle another 1–2<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>cm<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Reinsert the obturator until resistance is met to gauge the length of<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>the specimen<italicout>
  • <italicin>Rotate the needle 360 degrees vigorously several times while moving it back and forth vertically and horizontally to break the biopsy core off the surrounding<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>bone<italicout>
  • <italicin>Carefully remove the needle and insert a separate blunt obturator into the distal end of the needle to force the core out through the hub onto a glass<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>slide<italicout>
  • <italicin>Touch preparations of the core biopsy should be<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>performed<italicout>
  • <italicin>Specimen should be at least 1.5 to 2 cm in length for optimal<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>processing<italicout>
  • <italicin>If the specimen is inadequate or consists mostly of cartilage or cortical bone rather than core marrow, which appears dark red with a fine, white trabecular network, attempt additional<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>biopsies<italicout>
  • <italicin>The specimen should be placed in an appropriate<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>fixative<italicout>
  • <italicin>Apply direct pressure to the site for at least 5 minutes once the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>procedure is completed and the needle<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>removed<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Place a pressure<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>dressing.<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>How do we know when we reach at the site?<boldout>
<boldin>• Pop sound<boldout>
  • Foamy blood - foaminess implies that it is a marrow content
<boldin>Complications<boldout>
  • Bleeding at any site, with or without development of a hematoma, is rare if adequate pressure is applied
  • Bleeding risk has been reported to be increased in adults with osteoporosis or extensive bony involvement by disease, such as multiple myeloma
  • Retroperitoneal hemorrhage, osteomyelitis and needle breakage have also been rarely described
<boldin>• Infection (rare)<boldout>

<boldin>Follow up<boldout>
  • Multiple evaluations are often required to monitor efficacy and recovery following chemotherapy or stem cell transplantation
  • Patients should lie on their backs for an additional 15–20 minutes for procedures performed on the posterior iliac crest
  • Patients should be reminded that a dull ache may be felt for several days following the procedure
  • Routine post-procedural monitoring should be performed when heavy sedation or general anesthesia is administered

<boldin>Interpretations and monitoring<boldout>
  • Prepare the initial aspirate at the bedside immediately
  • Various techniques for spreading the film have been described, including the traditional wedge technique, particle crush method, and cover slip preparation
  • Slides are then dried, fixed in methanol and stained by the Wright-Giemsa technique
  • Systematic analysis of the aspirate slides includes assessment of the adequacy of spicules, cellularity, megakaryocyte count as well as maturation and morphologic features of other cell lineages
  • The biopsy specimen, once fixed, is decalcified
  • Further processing includes hematoxylin and eosin (H/&E), reticulin, and immunohistochemical staining when necessary
  • Comprehensive analysis of the biopsy specimen should include evaluation of the specimen’s adequacy, cellularity, and bone structure and detection of focal lesions or metastatic disease.

topics.title: Chapter 28: Intraosseous Infusion
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Indications<boldout>
<boldin>• Emergent temporary vascular access during cardiopulmonary resuscitation or during the treatment of uncompensated shock when unable to insert an intravenous line (i.e if peripheral IV line establishment is failed 3x, i.e total of 90 second trials each trial being 30 seconds). Anything can be given with the intraosseous route with same efficacy like the IV line; Such as:<boldout>
<boldin>• Volume resuscitation<boldout>
<boldin>• Administration of blood and blood products<boldout>
<boldin>• Administration of fluids and electrolytes<boldout>
<boldin>• Administration of medications<boldout>
<boldin>• Infusion of inotropes and pressors<boldout>
<boldin>• Sampling of blood and bone marrow<boldout>
  • The method is safe if the needle is left in place no longer than 6-8 hours

<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
<boldin>Absolute<boldout>
  • Recently fractured bone/ prior failed trial site
<boldin>• Infected site or burn<boldout>
<boldin>• Osteogenesis imperfect<boldout>
<boldin>• Osteopetrosis<boldout>
<boldin>Relative<boldout>
<boldin>• Osteoporosis or osteopenia<boldout>
<boldin>• Cystic bones<boldout>
<boldin>Equipments<boldout>
  • Intraosseous needle (18 or 20 gauge) or bone marrow aspiration needle
<boldin>• Povidone, chlorhexidine, and alcohol wipes<boldout>
<boldin>• Gauze<boldout>
<boldin>• Tape<boldout>
<boldin>• T-connector<boldout>
  • Syringe (5ml syringes (2) filled with NS)
<boldin>• Iv infusion equipments<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile gloves<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout><italicin>:<italicout><italicin> All Equipments should be latex free<italicout>
<boldin>Common sites<boldout>
<boldin>Infants and children<boldout>
  • Antero-medial surface of tibia 1-2 cm below tibial tuberosity: Direct the needle caudally to avoid the growth plate
<boldin>• Distal tibia<boldout>
<boldin>Adolescents<boldout>
  • Antero-medial surface of tibia 2 cm below tibial tuberosity
  • Distal femur 2 cm above the lateral condyle
<boldin>• Distal tibia proximal to medial malleolus<boldout>
  • Proximal humerus approximately 2cm below acromion process
<boldin>• Anterior superior iliac spine<boldout>
<boldin>• Posterior superior iliac spine<boldout>
<boldin>• Iliac crest<boldout>
<boldin>Patient preparation and positioning<boldout>
<boldin>• Choose the most appropriate site<boldout>
  • Inject local anaesthetics if the patient is conscious
  • Support the site of insertion over a ﬁrm surface
  • Hold the extremity above and below the insertion site
  • Position the patient with the selected site closest to where you are standing

<boldin>Procedure<boldout>
  • <italicin>Sanitize or wash your hands thoroughly and done<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>gloves<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Cleanse the site with antiseptic<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>solution<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Support the leg on a <italicout>ﬁ<italicin>rm surface and have an assistant support the leg above and below the insertion<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>site<italicout>
  • <italicin>Ensure no hand is under the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>site<italicout>
  • <italicin>Administer local anesthetic if the patient is<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>conscious<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Align needle bevel and<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>stylet<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Insert needle assembly through the skin and advance to bone<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>cortex<italicout>
  • <italicin>Hold the needle slightly angled (10 to 15 degrees) the bone, and direct it away from the nearest<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>joint<italicout>
  • <italicin>Use a twisting motion when advancing the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>needle<italicout>
  • <italicin>Stop advancing the needle when you feel a sudden decrease in<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>resistance<italicout>
  • <italicin>Unscrew the cap, remove the stylet, and attempt to aspirate bone marrow (looks like blood)<italicout>
  • <italicin>The unsupported needle should remain upright if properly<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>placed<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Flush the line with normal<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>saline<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Watch for increased resistance to the <italicout>ﬂ<italicin>ush or swelling of the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>extremity<italicout>
  • <italicin>If no marrow is aspirated but you think you are in the bone<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>marrow, attempt to<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>flush<italicout>
  • <italicin>If you encounter resistance, advance needle assembly and reattempt aspiration<italicout>
  • <italicin>Fluid should <italicout>ﬂ<italicin>ow freely through the needle and the line should flush without<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>resistance<italicout>
  • <italicin>Use tape and gauze to secure the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>line<italicout>


<boldin>Monitoring<boldout>
  • Watch for signs of compartment syndrome (6p’s): Pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis and pershingly cool
  • Look for swelling, redness, blanching, and leakage
<boldin>• Assess for vasoconstriction<boldout>
  • Stop the intraosseous infusion as soon as venous access is available

<boldin>Complications<boldout>
  • Extravasations of fluids or medications into subcutaneous tissue
<boldin>• Compartment syndrome<boldout>
<boldin>• Infection: subcutaneous abscess, osteomyelitis and bacteraemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Epiphysis injury and fracture<boldout>
<boldin>• Fat embolus<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 29: Femoral Veneous Catheterization
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Indications<boldout>
  • Any situation that requires central venous access or venous access that cannot be obtained peripherally
  • An emergency resuscitation requiring administration of large amounts of fluids
  • The need for central venous pressure monitoring
<boldin>• Placement of a pulmonary artery catheter<boldout>
<boldin>• The need for frequent blood draws<boldout>
  • Infusion of hyper alimentation, concentrated solutions (i.e. KCl, dextrose concentrations greater than 12.5%, chemotherapeutic agents, hyperosmolar saline)
  • Infusion of vasoactive substances (i.e. dopamine and norepinephrine) that can extravasate and cause soft-tissue necrosis
<boldin>• The need for hemodialysis<boldout>
<boldin>Advantages of Catheter Placement at Femoral Site<boldout>
  • It does not interfere with procedures or monitoring involving the head, neck, or chest (such as cardiopulmonary resuscitation)
  • Pressure can be applied easily in the event of femoral artery puncture or catheterization
  • It leaves the patient’s neck free of devices
<boldin>Disadvantages of Catheter Placement at Femoral Site<boldout>
  • It is a relatively “dirty” area (though this can be managed with good sterile technique and dressing changes)
  • It can be challenging to place a pulmonary artery catheter through a femoral venous catheter
  • Placement of a long line is required for central venous pressure monitoring

<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
<boldin>Absolute<boldout>
  • Severe abdominal trauma (provided that adequate venous access can be obtained elsewhere)
<boldin>Relative<boldout>
  • A patient with distorted anatomy or landmarks
  • Risk factors for excessive bleeding, such as thrombocytopenia, coagulopathy, and anticoagulant or thrombolytic therapy
  • Skin lesions (such as cellulitis, burns, abrasions, or dermatitis)
  • Conditions that predispose the patient to sclerosis or thrombosis (such as vasculitis)
<boldin>• Known thrombus of the femoral vein<boldout>
<boldin>Equipments<boldout>
<boldin>• The catheter<boldout>
  • An appropriate size guidewire (at least 2 times the length of the catheter)
<boldin>• An appropriate size introducer needle<boldout>
  • A tissue dilator if the catheter is larger than 3F
  • Two or three 3- to 5-ml syringes
  • 1% lidocaine and a 26-gauge needle to inject the lidocaine
  • Skin preparation solution (either 2% chlorhexidine-based preparation for patients older than 2 months or 10%povidone-iodine)
<boldin>• Sterile drapes<boldout>
<boldin>• Scalpel blade<boldout>
  • Suture (i.e., 3.0 silk) and Sterile gauze pads
<boldin>Patient preparation and<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>positioning<boldout>
  • Place the patient in the supine position
  • Raise the hips slightly to flatten the inguinal area
  • Position the patient with his or her legs extended or with the hips and knees slightly flexed in the “frog” position
  • In the case of a child whose respiratory status is compromised in the supine position, it may be necessary to obtain definitive airway control (i.e., intubate the patient)prior to the procedure
  • Children who can comfortably lie supine may require conscious sedation so that they remain still throughout the procedure
  • To avoid aspiration during intubation or conscious sedation, the procedure should be delayed 6 hours after the ingestion of solid food and 4 hours after the ingestion of clear liquids, unless central access is needed emergently

<boldin>Anatomy review<boldout>
  • The femoral artery and vein run in parallel with the artery lateral to the vein
  • The inguinal ligament runs from the anterior superior iliac spine to the pubic tubercle
  • Remember the mnemonic “NAVEL” (nerve, artery, vein, empty space, lymph), which describes the structures’ anatomic location from lateral to medial

<boldin><italicin>Note:<italicout><boldout>
<italicin>Femoral artery, vein and femoral canal are found inside the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>femoral sheath, but the nerve found outside the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>sheath.<italicout>

<boldin>Procedures<boldout>
  • <italicin>The operator should wear a cap and mask, be scrubbed, and use a sterile gown and gloves for this<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>procedure<italicout>
  • <italicin>Prepare the area using either 2% chlorhexidine-based preparation for patients older than 2 months of age or 10%<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>povidone-iodine<italicout>
  • <italicin>Using the non-dominant hand, palpate the femoral artery in 2 to 3 places to get a sense for the path of the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>artery<italicout><italicin>. <italicout><italicin>The femoral vein lies just medial to the artery and typically follows in parallel<italicout>
  • <italicin>Inject a local anesthetic (1% lidocaine) in the area of<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>the venipuncture site and the tissues deep to the venipuncture<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>site<italicout>
  • <italicin>Always withdraw the plunger slightly before injecting lidocaine to avoid injecting<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>intravascularly<italicout>
  • <italicin>Flush all ports of the catheter with normal saline or heparinized saline prior to the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>procedure<italicout>
  • <italicin>The technique for placing the catheter is called the <italicout>Seldinger technique
  • <italicin>Attach the insertion needle to a syringe partially filled with<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>saline<italicout>
  • <italicin>With the bevel of the insertion needle facing up (toward the ceiling) hold the needle and syringe at a 30- to 45-degreeangle directing the needle toward the patient’s<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>umbilicus<italicout>
  • <italicin>Puncture the skin approximately 1–2 cm distal to the inguinal ligament in the location of the femoral vein (just medial to the palpated femoral<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>artery)<italicout>
  • <italicin>As the needle is slowly advanced, gently draw back on<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>the syringe<italicout>
  • <italicin>When a free flow of blood appears remove the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>syringe<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Insert the guidewire into the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>needle<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>The guidewire should pass easily through the needle into the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>vessel<italicout>
  • <italicin>Leave the distal end of the guidewire<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>exposed<italicout>
  • <italicin>If resistance is met, redirect the needle or remove the guidewire and needle, <italicout><italicin>apply pressure until the bleeding stops and begin the process over<italicout>
  • Do not force the guidewire into place
  • <italicin>Using the scalpel, make a small (approximately 2 mm) incision at the venipuncture<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>site<italicout>
  • <italicin>Apply gentle pressure to the venipuncture site and remove the needle, leaving the guidewire in<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>place<italicout>
  • <italicin>If the catheter is larger than 3F, thread the tissue Dilator over the wire and advance it into the venipuncture<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>site<italicout>
  • <italicin>It may be necessary to use a twisting action to <italicout><italicin>advance the tissue dilator<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Remove the tissue<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>dilator<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>The guidewire should be exposed and visible<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>continuously<italicout>
  • <italicin>Hold the guidewire with the nondominant hand and thread the catheter over the guidewire until the guideline protrudes from the distal end of the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>catheter<italicout>
  • <italicin>It may be necessary to hold a little pressure at the insertion site<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>if there is a significant amount of<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>bleeding<italicout>
  • <italicin>Take the end of the guidewire with the non-dominant hand and pass the catheter over the wire and into the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>vessel<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Remove the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>guidewire<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>There should be good blood flow from all<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>ports<italicout>
  • <italicin>Flush <italicout><italicin>each port with attention to removing any air<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>bubbles before flushing<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Suture the catheter in<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>place<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Cap off the catheter or attach it to IV<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>tubing<italicout>
<boldin>Monitoring<boldout>
  • If the catheter is correctly placed in the femoral vein, blood flow from the catheter should be steady, but not pulsatile
  • Verify correct placement by obtaining a venous gas and documenting an appropriate venous saturation
  • However, remember that placement confirmation using a blood gas can be unreliable in patients who have significant cardiopulmonary disease whose arterial oxygen saturation may be abnormally low or in hyper oxygenated patients whose venous oxygen saturation may be abnormally elevated
  • A more reliable method for verifying catheter placement is to transduce the catheter and confirm a venous wave form and pressure

<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>During Catheter Placement<boldout>
  • Bleeding, the main complication, often results from inadvertent artery puncture
<boldin>• Local hematomas<boldout>
<boldin>• Bowel or bladder perforation<boldout>
<boldin>• Air embolus<boldout>
<boldin>• Catheter embolus<boldout>
<boldin>• Creation of an arteriovenous fistula<boldout>
<boldin>With Catheter in Place<boldout>
<boldin>• Infection<boldout>
<boldin>• Current recommendations for limiting infectious complications include the following:<boldout>
  • Strict adherence to sterile technique during catheter placement
  • Use of 2% chlorhexidine-based preparation in patients older than 2 months of age
  • Use of a catheter with the fewest number of lumens that is essential for treatment
  • Swelling of the lower extremity, resulting from impaired venous return
  • The swelling can often be managed by elevating the leg
  • Care should be taken to confirm palpable distal pulses in such cases
  • Deep venous thrombosis or inferior vena cava thrombosis
  • Catheter malposition (i.e., insertion into the lumbar venous plexus, a potentially lethal complication)
<boldin>• Catheter knotting<boldout>
<boldin>Follow up<boldout>
  • The catheter should be removed as soon as it is not needed
  • When catheter is no longer needed, remove the sutures and pull the catheter slowly and carefully
  • Apply pressure to the insertion site until the bleeding stops

topics.title: Chapter 30: Umbilical Vein Catheterization
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Indications<boldout>
  • Emergency vascular access for ﬂuid and medications when IV access is failed
  • Administration of high glucose concentration and total parenteral nutrition
<boldin>• Central venous pressure monitoring<boldout>
<boldin>• Exchange transfusion<boldout>
  • Usually it is safest for age ≤10 days
<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
<boldin>• Omphalitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Omphalocele<boldout>
<boldin>• Gastroschisis<boldout>
<boldin>• Necrotizing enterocolitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Umbilical surgery<boldout>
<boldin>• Peritonitis<boldout>
<boldin>Equipments<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile catheter<boldout>
  • Use 3.5F catheter for patients weighing /< 1500 g
  • Use 5F catheter for patients weighing /> 1500 g
<boldin>• Sterile drapes<boldout>
<boldin>• Povidone-iodine swabs<boldout>
<boldin>• Umbilical tie<boldout>
<boldin>• Toothed iris forceps<boldout>
  • 2 curved non-toothed haemostats
<boldin>• Suture scissors<boldout>
<boldin>• Small needle holder<boldout>
  • 3-0 silk suture on small curved needle
  • 3-way stopcock with Luer-Lok
  • 3-mL and 1-mL syringes with needles
  • 2 × 2 gauze
  • 4 × 4 gauze
<boldin>• Saline solution with heparin 1 unit/ml<boldout>
<boldin>Patient preparation and positioning<boldout>
  • Place the infant in the supine position and secure the upper and lower extremities
  • Place chest leads for continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring and a sensor for pulse oximetry monitoring throughout the procedure
  • Place the infant on a radiant warmer
<boldin>Length of the tube inserted<boldout>
  • 2/3 of the distance from tip of the right shoulder to umbilicus <boldin>or<boldout>
• Approximately 5-7 cm <boldin>or<boldout>
  • For preterm you can advance 2-3 cm and for terms you can advance 3-4 cm then see  for blood to come <boldin>or<boldout>
  • Until the blood comes through the tube
<boldin>Procedures<boldout>
  • <italicin>Carefully clean the cord and surrounding skin with povidone-iodine and alcohol<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>solutions<italicout>
  • <italicin>Place an umbilical tie at the base of the umbilicus to control<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>bleeding<italicout>
  • <italicin>Drape the infant with sterile drapes with head and feet<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>visible<italicout>
  • <italicin>Cut the cord horizontally with a scalpel, approximately 1-2 cm above the skin<italicout>
  • <italicin>Identify vessels- usually 2 arteries and 1 vein. The vein is a large, thin- walled gaping vessel lying<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>superiorly<italicout>
  • <italicin>Grasp the umbilical stump on either side with the curved<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>haemostats<italicout>
  • <italicin>Remove visible clots in the lumen with some forceps<italicout>
  • <italicin>Gently insert the tip of the iris forceps into the lumen of the vein and dilate as needed. In general, minimal dilation<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>is<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>needed<italicout>
  • <italicin>Insert the heparinized saline- <italicout>ﬁ<italicin>lled catheter attached to a stopcock and syringe into the vessel while applying gentle traction on the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>cord<italicout>
  • <italicin>Advance in a cephalad direction to the estimated catheter<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>length<italicout>
  • <italicin>Aspirate gently. If there is smooth blood <italicout>ﬂ<italicin>ow, secure in place and obtain chest and abdominal radiographs to verify<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>position<italicout>
  • <italicin>If the catheter meets resistance before achieving its estimated distance, it has most likely entered the portal system or an intrahepatic branch of the umbilical vein<italicout>

<boldin>Monitoring<boldout>
  • The ideal location of the tip of the umbilical catheter is T9-10, just above the right hemi diaphragm and below the heart
  • On a radiograph, the catheter will lie to the right of the vertebral column in the inferior vena cava

<boldin>Complications<boldout>
  • Hemorrhage: From displacement of catheter or perforation of the umbilical artery
<boldin>• Infection: Especially portal vein thrombophlebitis<boldout>
  • Cardiac arrhythmias, tamponade, perforation, or thrombotic endocarditis: Due to catheter malpositioned in heart or great vessels
  • Hepatic necrosis: Due to catheter malpositioned in the portal system, especially if hypertonic solutions are infused into the liver tissue
  • Air embolism: If the catheter is inadvertently opened to the atmosphere
<img:t21.png>

topics.title: Chapter 31: Exchange Transfusion Of New Born
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Indications<boldout>
<boldin>• Partial volume exchange transfusion: Polycythemia; When:<boldout>
<boldin>• Hematocrit ≥ 65% in symptomatic infants<boldout>
<boldin>• Hematocrit ≥ 70% in asymptomatic infants<boldout>
  • Volume to volume exchange transfusion: Severe anemia
<boldin>• Double volume exchange transfusion: Hyperbilirubinemia<boldout>
  • If phototherapy fails to lower unconjugated bilirubin
  • If risk of kernicterus exceeds risk of procedure
<boldin>• If newborn has sign of kernicterus<boldout>
  • When unconjugated bilirubin is reached to level of exchange transfusion

<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
  • When patient is unstable and the risk of the procedure outweighs the possible benefit

<boldin>Types of exchange transfusion<boldout>
• <boldin>Partial volume<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>exchange<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>transfusion<boldout>
  • Total volume of blood removed is replaced by same volume of NS
<boldin>• Volume(ml) to be exchanged: V<boldout>
  • The desired hematocrit should be 50 to 55%
  • (85ml/kg * weight(kg)) = blood volume of the neonate
• <boldin>Volume to volume<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>exchange<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>transfusion <boldout>
  • Total volume of blood removed is replaced by same volume of Packed RBCs
• <boldin>Double volume<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>exchange<boldout><boldin> <boldout><boldin>transfusion <boldout>
  • Twice the total blood volume of the neonate is removed and replaced by donor blood
  • 𝑽𝒐𝒍𝒖𝒎𝒆(𝒎𝒍) 𝒕𝒐 𝒃𝒆 𝒆𝒙𝒄𝒉𝒂𝒏𝒈𝒆𝒅 = 𝟐 * 𝒃𝒍𝒐𝒐𝒅 𝒗𝒐𝒍𝒖𝒎𝒆 𝒐𝒇 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝒏𝒆𝒐𝒏𝒂𝒕𝒆
<boldin>• Replaces 85% of circulating newborn RBC<boldout>
  • Reduces bilirubin level by half of the previous pre-exchange value
<boldin>• Besides removing bilirubin corrects anemia<boldout>
<boldin>Patient preparations<boldout>
<boldin>• Discuss with the family<boldout>
<boldin>• Stop feedings before the procedure<boldout>
  • Modiﬁed whole blood (red cells and plasma) that is type O- cytomegalovirus negative, irradiated, cross-matched against the mother and compatible with the infant. It should be two times the infant’s blood volumes (blood volume in a term infant is 85 ml/kg) as well as enough volume to prime the tubing and blood warmer is needed.
  • As cross-matching to both the mother and infant may require additional time, blood should be ordered before the infant meets criteria for the procedure
  • Place infant on warmer with total accessibility and controlled environment
  • Restrain infant suitably. Sedation and pain relief are not usually required
  • Connect physiologic monitors and establish baseline values (temperature, respiratory and heart rates, oxygenation)
<boldin>• Empty infant/'s stomach<boldout>
  • Start IV line for glucose and medication infusion
  • Stabilize infant prior to starting exchange procedure
<boldin>Precautions<boldout>
  • <italicin>Do not start exchange procedure until personnel are available for monitoring and as backup for other<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>emergencies<italicout>
  • <italicin>Use blood product appropriate to clinical indication. Use freshest blood available, preferably /<5 to 7<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>days<italicout>
  • <italicin>Check potassium level of donor blood if patient has hyperkalemia<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>or renal<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>compromise<italicout>
  • <italicin>Monitor infant closely during and after<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>procedure<italicout>
  • <italicin>Do not rush<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>procedure<italicout> - <italicin>usually takes upto 90 minutes<italicout>
  • <italicin>Use only thermostatically controlled blood-warming device that has passed quality control for temperature and alarms. Be sure to review operating and safety procedures for specific blood warmer. Do not overheat blood, i.e., beyond<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>38°C<italicout>
  • <italicin>Do not apply excessive suction if it becomes difficult to draw blood<italicout> <italicin>from line. Reposition line or replace syringes, stopcocks, and any adapters connected to<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>line<italicout>
  • <italicin>Leave anticoagulated, banked blood in line or clear line<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>with heparinized saline if the procedure is<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>interrupted<italicout>
  • <italicin>Clear line with heparinized saline if administering<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>calcium<italicout>
<boldin>Procedures<boldout>
Push-pull technique:<boldin> <boldout>Central access usually through umbilical venous catheter
<boldin>Isovolumetric exchange:<boldout>
  • With simultaneous infusion of donor blood through venous line and removal of baby/'s blood through arterial line
  • This technique may be better tolerated in sick or unstable neonates because there is less fluctuation of blood pressure and cerebral hemodynamic
  • The technique is also favoured when only peripheral vascular access is available or preferred for various reasons
  • Infusion of donor blood may be through umbilical venous catheter or peripheral intravenous catheter
  • Removal of baby/'s blood may be from umbilical arterial or venous catheter, or peripheral arterial catheter, usually a radial arterial line.

<boldin>Technique<boldout>
  • Scrub as for major procedure. Wear mask, head cover, sterile gown, and gloves
  • Open preassembled equipment tray, using aseptic technique
  • Identify positions on special stopcock in clockwise rotation
  • Have an assistant document all vital signs, volumes, and other data on the exchange record
  • Check peripheral glucose levels every 30 to 60 minutes. Monitor cardiorespiratory status, continuous pulse oximeter. Determine blood gases as often as indicated by pre-existing clinical condition and stability
<boldin>• Draw blood for diagnostic studies<boldout>
  • Usual rate of removal and replacement of blood during the ET is aliquots of approximately 5 ml/kg over a 2- to 4-minute cycle
  • If infant is hypovolemic or has low CVP, start exchange with transfusion of aliquot (5 ml/kg) into catheter. If infant is hypervolemic or has high CVP, start by withdrawing precalculated aliquot
  • Remeasure CVP if indicated. Expect rise as plasma oncotic pressure increases, if CVP is low at start
  • Ensure that the stages of drawing and infusing blood from and into the infant are done slowly, taking at least a minute each to avoid fluctuations in blood pressure
  • Gently agitate the blood bag every 10 to 15 minutes to prevent red cell sedimentation, which may lead to exchange with relatively anaemic blood towards the end of the exchange
  • Perform calculated number of passes, until desired volume has been exchanged
  • Be sure there is adequate volume of donor blood remaining to infuse after last withdrawal, if a positive intravascular balance is desired
  • Clear umbilical line of banked blood and withdraw amount of infant/'s blood needed for laboratory testing, including re-cross-matching
  • Infuse IV fluids with 0.5 to 1 U heparin/ml of fluid through UVC if further ET is anticipated
  • Total duration for double-volume ET: 90 to 120 minutes
<boldin>• Document procedure in patient/'s hospital record.<boldout>
<boldin>Post-exchange<boldout>
  • Continue to monitor vital signs closely for at least 4 to 6 hours
  • Rewrite orders; adjust any drug dosages as needed to compensate for removal by exchange
  • Keep infant NPO for at least 4 hours. Restart feeds cautiously if clinically stable. Monitor abdominal girth and bowel sounds every 3 to 4 hours for next 24 hours if exchange has been performed using umbilical vascular lines. Observe for signs of feeding intolerance
  • Monitor serum glucose levels every 2 to 4 hours for 24 hours
  • Repeat blood gases as often as clinically indicated
  • Measure serum ionized calcium levels, serum level of medications and platelet counts in sick infants immediately after the ET and then as indicated
  • Repeat haemoglobin, hematocrit, and bilirubin measurements approximately 4 hours after exchange, and further as clinically indicated
  • Antibiotics for 48 hours if not on medications already
<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>Immediate<boldout>
<boldin>• Cardiovascular instability – arrhythmia<boldout>
<boldin>• Air embolism<boldout>
<boldin>• Hypoglycemia, Hypocalcaemia, Hypomagnesaemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Acidosis, alkalosis<boldout>
<boldin>• Hyperkalemia<boldout>
<boldin>• Anemia/polycythemia, Thrombocytopenia<boldout>
<boldin>• Infection<boldout>
<boldin>Long term<boldout>
<boldin>• Necrotizing enterocolitis<boldout>
<boldin>• Portal and hepatic vein thrombosis<boldout>
  • Blood-born infection → HIV, Hepatitis B, C, CMV…

topics.title: Chapter 32: Subdural Tap
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Indications<boldout>
  • Evacuations of subdural blood or fluid in young infants, when such collections cause symptoms (i.e., seizures, unilateral paresis) from increased intracranial pressure
  • To sample convexity subdural collection for hematologic, microbiologic, and biochemical studies
  • To diagnose acute subdural collection over the cerebral convexities (haemorrhage, effusion, empyema)

<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
  • Clinical instability when risk exceeds potential benefit
<boldin>• Uncorrected thrombocytopenia or bleeding diathesis<boldout>
  • Infection in the skin or underlying tissue at or near the puncture site

<boldin>Equipment’s<boldout>
  • Subdural or spinal needle (19 or 20 gauge)
  • 10 ml syringe
<boldin>• Razor blade<boldout>
<boldin>• Povidone–iodine solution<boldout>
  • 70% alcohol
<boldin>• Sterile gauze<boldout>
  • 1% lidocaine with epinephrine
<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Intracranial haemorrhage<boldout>
<boldin>• Contusion of the cerebral cortex<boldout>
<boldin>• Subgaleal collection of fluid or blood<boldout>
• Infection<boldin> <boldout>
<boldin>Procedures <boldout>
  • <italicin>Prepare the infant for the procedure in the supine<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>position<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Head should be face<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>up<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Shave the scalp in an area around the lateral boundaries of the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>anterior fontanel.<italicout>
  • <italicin>Wearing sterile gloves, palpate the coronal suture at the lateral aspect<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>of the anterior fontanel. If the fontanel opening is extremely small, move several millimeters farther laterally in the coronal suture. Inject local anesthetic (i.e., 1% lidocaine with epinephrine) in the conscious<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>child<italicout>
  • <italicin>Grasp a 19- or 20-gauge subdural or spinal needle by the hub and check<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>its patency. Hold it between the thumb and index finger, and rest the heel of the hand against the infant/'s<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>scalp<italicout>
  • <italicin>Puncture the skin at a right angle to surface, stretching it slightly to obtain a Z-track. Advance the needle through the puncture site between the edges of the coronal suture until the feeling of resistance<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>lessens<italicout>
  • <italicin>Then, remove the stylet to allow fluid or blood to drain; 10 to 15 ml can be safely evacuated from each<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>side<italicout>
  • <italicin>Normally, the needle is not advanced more than 5 to 8 mm below the scalp/'s surface. Some infants may require bilateral<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>taps<italicout>

  •

topics.title: Chapter 33: Indications
topics.content_html:
• Therapeutic drainage of pleural effusion in patient with respiratory compromise
  • Diagnostic evaluation of pleural effusion of unknown etiology
<boldin>• Therapeutic removal of small pneumothorax<boldout>
<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
<boldin>Relative<boldout>
  • Skin infection (eg, herpes zoster) at site of insertion
<boldin>• Bleeding diathesis<boldout>
<boldin>• Anticoagulant therapy<boldout>
<boldin>• Mechanical ventilation<boldout>
<boldin>Equipments<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile gloves, mask, and gown<boldout>
<boldin>• Iodinated skin preparation with sterile sponges<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile towels<boldout>
<boldin>• Local anesthetic (1%lidocaine without epinephrine)<boldout>
  • 5-ml syringe with 25-gauge needle
  • 18-gauge 2-inch needle
  • 18-20 gauge angiocatheter
<boldin>• Collection basin<boldout>
  • 3-way stopcock
  • 20-60-ml syringe
<boldin>Common sites<boldout>
  • Pleural effusion: At 6th or 7th intercostal space just distal to the scapular tip in the midscapular line or posterior axillary line
<boldin>• Pneumothorax: Usually for tension pneumothorax with cardiorespiratory compromise:<boldout>
  • At 2nd intercostal space over the mid clavicular line or
  • At 4th intercostal space over anterior axillary line
<boldin>Patient preparation<boldout>
  • Explain procedure in a developmentally appropriate manner before and during procedure
<boldin>• Patient should have intravenous access<boldout>
<boldin>• Oxygen should be available<boldout>
<boldin>• Monitor oxygen saturation with pulse oximetry<boldout>
  • Younger patients may need sedation for procedure
<boldin>Patient positioning<boldout>
<boldin>• Pleural effusion:<boldout>
  • Sitting upright with arms supported on table in front of patient
  • Lying in lateral decubitus position with effusion side down
  • Pneumothorax: Supine with head of bed up 30 degrees
<boldin>Procedures<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Locate effusion<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Chest radiograph<boldout>
  • Manual percussion to find onset of dullness. Ideal location is 1-2 cm (about 1 intercostal space) below onset of dullness
<boldin>• Ultra-sonogram marked location<boldout>
  • Mark location of effusion with the patient in the same position as necessary for procedure
  • If possible, do not move patient after marking the location because the ﬂuid may shift
<boldin><italicin>Prepare sterile field<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Cleanse area in sterile fashion<boldout>
<boldin>• Drape surrounding area with sterile towels<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Numb the area<italicout><boldout>
  • Use a 25-gauge needle and 5-ml syringe to inﬁltrate the skin and make a wheal under the skin
  • Change needle to 18 gauge with 2-inch needle
  • Going over top of sixth rib, inﬁltrate through wheal, overtop of rib to anesthetize the periosteum, and into pleural space
  • Be sure to aspirate ﬁrst, and know when you are in the pleural space
  • The parietal pleura needs to be anesthetized, but, to avoid a puncture of the lung, do not advance the needle further
  • When in the pleural space, a ‘pop’ may be felt and ﬂuid or air will enter syringe

<boldin><italicin>Removal of Pleural Effusion for Diagnostic Evaluation<italicout><boldout>
  • Remove lidocaine syringe and needle to outside the pleural space, with needle still inserted but outside the pleural space; replace syringe with empty 20-60 ml syringe
