  • Reinsert needle into pleural space while applying gentle negative pressure on syringe
  • When in pleural space, a ‘pop’ may be felt and ﬂuid or air will enter syringe
<boldin>• Remove effusion into syringe<boldout>
  • Remove needle and apply bandage to area
<boldin><italicin>Therapeutic removal of pleural effusion<italicout><boldout>
  • Completely remove needle and syringe filled with lidocaine
  • Insert angiocatheter into same track and enter pleural space while applying gentle negative pressure. When in pleural space, a “pop” may be felt and fluid or air will enter syringe
  • Remove inner needle, leaving catheter in place. Ensure that the stopcock is closed to pleural space and chest wall or place a finger over the end of catheter to avoid introducing air into chest wall and creating a pneumothorax
<boldin>• Withdraw fluid:<boldout>
  • Withdraw syringe full of fluid, close stopcock to chest wall and pleural space and drain into collection basin
  • Repeat withdrawal of fluid until desired amount has been removed
  • Remove angiocatheter and apply bandage to area
<boldin>Interpretations<boldout>
  • See “pleural fluid analysis” on “respiratory             system examinations”
<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Pneumothorax<boldout>
  • Bleeding: from intercostal vessel creating subcutaneous hematoma or Hemothorax
<boldin>• Hypoxia<boldout>
<boldin>• Pulmonary edema<boldout>
<boldin>• Puncture of lung, liver or spleen<boldout>
<boldin>• Infection<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Note<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Insert needle over top of the rib since the neurovascular<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>bundle is on the lower caudal edge of the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>rib.<italicout>
  • <italicin>The normal pleural effusion found in the pleural space is ≤<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>1ml.<italicout>

topics.title: Chapter 34: Chest Tube Insertion
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Indications<boldout>
<boldin>• Pneumothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Hemothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Chylothorax<boldout>
<boldin>• Empyema<boldout>
<boldin>• For pleurodesis<boldout>
<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
<boldin>Relative<boldout>
<boldin>• Bleeding diathesis<boldout>
<boldin>• Mechanical ventilation<boldout>
<boldin>• Presence of adhesions: may require pleurodesis<boldout>
<boldin>• Skin infection over the insertion site<boldout>
<boldin>Insertion site<boldout>
  • At 5th intercostal space over anterior axillary line
  • At 2nd intercostal space over midclavicular line for small pneumothorax or
<boldin>• At safe triangle. Boundaries of safe trangle:<boldout>
<boldin>• Posterior border of pectoralies major<boldout>
  • Superior border of the 5th rib and
<boldin>• Anterior border of latissimus dorsi<boldout>
<boldin>Equipments<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile gloves, mask, and gown<boldout>
<boldin>• Iodinated skin preparation with sterile sponges<boldout>
<boldin>• Sterile towels<boldout>
<boldin>• Local anesthetic (1%lidocaine without epinephrine)<boldout>
  • 5-mL syringe with 25-gauge needle
  • 18-gauge 2-inch needle
  • #10 scalpel with handle
  • Chest tube and Kelly clamp for large bore insertion
  • Pleurevac or other drainage system, including all connectors necessary to connect to chest tube and to suction
<boldin>• Suction<boldout>
<boldin>• Needle holder<boldout>
<boldin>• Suture scissors<boldout>
  • 2-0 silk suture
  • 4 × 4 gauze and Transparent occlusive dressing
<boldin>Patient preparation<boldout>
<boldin>• Patient should have intravenous access<boldout>
<boldin>• Oxygen should be available<boldout>
<boldin>• Monitor oxygen saturation with pulse oximetry<boldout>
  • Younger patients may need sedation or anesthesia for procedure, especially with large bore chest tube insertion
  • Explain procedure in a developmentally appropriate manner before and during procedure

<boldin>Patient positioning<boldout>
  • Patient lying on bed with head of table elevated 30 degrees with arm above head

<boldin>Procedures<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Prepare Sterile Field<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Cleanse area in sterile fashion<boldout>
<boldin>• Drape surrounding area with sterile towels.<boldout>
<boldin><italicin>Numb the<italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin>Area<italicout><boldout>
  • Use 25-gauge needle and 5-mL syringe to infiltrate skin and make wheal under skin
  • Change needle to 18 gauge with 2-inch needle
  • Infiltrate through wheal, over top of rib, to anesthetize the periosteum, and into pleural space
  • The pleura needs to be anesthetized, but, to avoid a puncture of the lung, do not advance the needle further
  • Be sure to aspirate first, and know when you are in the pleural space
<boldin><italicin>Insertion of Chest Tube by Seldinger Technique<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• Remove syringe from needle<boldout>
  • Pass guidewire through needle into pleural space
  • Remove needle (while always maintaining a hold on the guide wire)
  • Make small incision at site of insertion (large enough to pass chest tube)
  • Starting with smallest dilator, insert dilator over guide wire using a twisting motion (while always maintaining a hold on the guide wire)
  • Repeat with larger dilators over guide wire until track is large enough to easily pass chest tube (while always maintaining a hold on the guidewire)
  • Insert chest tube over guidewire until all port holes are within the pleural space and then remove guidewire
<boldin>• Suture chest tube to chest wall<boldout>
  • Connect tube to drainage device with suction at 15–20 cmH2O
  • Apply sterile 4 x 4 dressing and transparent occlusive dressing
<boldin><italicin>Insertion of Chest Tube by Blunt Dissection<italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin> <italicout><boldout><boldin><italicin>Technique<italicout><boldout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Remove needle used for local<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>anesthesia<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Using scalpel make ~1–2-cm incision through the skin and subcutaneous tissue (large enough to pass chest<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>tube)<italicout>
  • <italicin>Insert Kelly clamp and tunnel up 2 intercostal spaces I the subcutaneous space<italicout>
  • <italicin>Push through the intercostal muscle superior to the rib with the Kelly clamp and enter the pleural space; air or fluid may rush<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>out<italicout>
  • <italicin>Spread the clamp to widen the area to allow for the chest<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>tube<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Remove<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>clamp<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Insert gloved finger into tract and ensure correct location and lyse any adhesions<italicout>
  • <italicin>Using the Kelly clamp attached to the chest tube as a guide, insert the chest tube into the pleural<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>space<italicout>
  • <italicin>If to drain air, guide anterior and superior (turning the clamp so that the curve is upward will assist with this<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>guidance)<italicout>
  • <italicin>If to drain fluid, guide posterior and inferior (turning the clamp so that the curve is down will assist with this<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>guidance)<italicout>
  • <italicin>Advance chest tube until all ports are within the pleural<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>space<italicout>
<boldin>• <italicin>Suture chest tube to chest<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>wall<italicout><boldout>
  • <italicin>Connect tube to drainage device with suction at 15–20<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>cmH<italicout><italicin>2<italicout><italicin>O<italicout>
  • <italicin>Apply sterile 4 x 4 dressing and transparent occlusive<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>dressing<italicout>
<boldin>To check functionality<boldout>
  • Effusion = Continuous bubbling in the bottle
<boldin>• Pneumothorax = Oscillations in the bottle<boldout>
<boldin>When we remove chest tube?<boldout>
<boldin>• Effusion:<boldout>
<boldin>• When the lung is fully expanded<boldout>
  • When fluid output is less than 200 ml/day
<boldin>• Pneumothorax:<boldout>
  • When no visible air leak is present and air does not accumulate when suction is removed
<boldin>• When the lung is fully expanded<boldout>
<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Improper position<boldout>
<boldin>• Subcutaneous emphysema<boldout>
  • Hemorrhage – due to intercostal vessel injury
<boldin>• Puncture of lung, liver or spleen<boldout>
<boldin>• Infection<boldout>
<boldin>• Intercostal neurovascular injury<boldout>
<boldin>Follow up<boldout>
  • Obtain chest radiograph to ensure correct placement
  • While on chest tube, the child should have adequate analgesics to control pain so that full expansion of the chest will occur.

topics.title: Chapter 35: Pericardiocentesis
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Indications<boldout>
<boldin>• Therapeutic: Impending cardiac tamponade<boldout>
  • Diagnostic: Ideally any pericardial fluid has diagnostic pericardiocentesis indications
  • Compromise in the patient’s hemodynamic status/ tamponade effect

<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
<boldin>Relative<boldout>
  • Blood dyscrasia: May have a signiﬁcant bleeding
<boldin>• Site infection<boldout>
<boldin>• Effusion due to aortic dissection<boldout>
  • Signiﬁcantly elevated diaphragm  may change the standard landmarks
  • Grossly enlarged liver		   of needle insertion under such
  • Profound ascites	             circumstances, use the intercostal approach
<boldin>Equipments<boldout>
<boldin>• Povidone-iodine or equivalent sterilization substrate<boldout>
  • 1% or 2% lidocaine or xylocaine
  • 25-gauge, 1.5-inch-long needle
  • 16 or 18-gauge needle ≥ 1.5 inch
  • Floppy tip wire that can be introduced through the needle
  • Pigtail catheter with multiple side holes as well as an end hole
<boldin>• Scalpel<boldout>
  • 3-way stopcock
  • 30 ml or 60 ml syringe and suture kit
  • ECG monitor, pulse oximeter, and blood pressure cuff
<boldin>Patient preparation<boldout>
  • Prepare and drape the subxiphoid area in the usual sterile fashion
  • If the subxiphoid approach might be difficult, consider preparing the left sternal border
  • All equipment should be readily available and an assistant should be available to help with manipulation of needles, wires, and catheters

<boldin>Patient position<boldout>
  • Supine position, with 30-45 degrees of reverse Trendelenburg
  • Occasionally, the partially sitting position may be required or beneﬁcial
  • May allow for most of the pericardial ﬂuid to position inferiorly or closer to the drainage site
<boldin>• Makes an orthopneic patient more comfortable<boldout>
<boldin>Procedures <boldout>
  • <italicin>Prepare the subxiphoid area and left sternal<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>border<italicout>
  • <italicin>Administer 1% or 2%lidocaine approximately 0.5-1 cm below the left costoxiphoid angle using a 25- or 27-gauge 1.5-inch-long<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>needle<italicout>
  • <italicin>In<italicout>ﬁ<italicin>ltration of the lidocaine should be super<italicout>ﬁ<italicin>cial as well as deep, pushing the needle superiorly, posteriorly, and<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>leftward<italicout>
  • <italicin>Withdraw <italicout>ﬂ<italicin>uid each time the needle is passed deeper within the skin and subcutaneous<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>tissues<italicout>
  • <italicin>To allow for easier passage of the needle, precut the skin with the scalpel before introducing the 16- or 18-gauge1.5-inch to 2.5-inch<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>needle<italicout>
  • <italicin>Insert the larger needle at an approximate 30-45-degree angle with the abdomen with constant negative pressure on the syringe. Depth of the needle in children is<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>4-5cm<italicout>
  • <italicin>Monitor ECG very carefully for evidence of dysrhythmias or ST segment changes (evidence of coronary or myocardial<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>injury)<italicout>
  • <italicin>Slowly insert the needle until <italicout>ﬂ<italicin>uid is<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>withdrawn<italicout>
  • <italicin>Suspect cardiac perforation is the fluid is grossly<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>bloody<italicout>
  • <italicin>Serous fluid confirms that the needle has passed into<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>the pericardial<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>space<italicout>
  • <italicin>It is not unusual to feel the needle pass through the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>pericardium<italicout>
  • <italicin>Fix the needle into position once pericardial <italicout>ﬂ<italicin>uid is<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>extracted<italicout>
  • <italicin>Pass the <italicout>ﬂ<italicin>oppy tip wire through the needle with the intent of passing <italicout><italicin>the wire deep within the pericardium and into the posterior pericardial space<italicout>
  • <italicin>Because the wire may irritate the epicardium, ventricular ectopy is not uncommon<italicout>
  • <italicin>Once the wire is secured deep within the pericardium, remove the<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>needle<italicout>
  • <italicin>Make a larger incision in the skin adjacent to the wires so that the catheter can be<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>inserted<italicout>
  • <italicin>Insert the soft-tipped multiple side hole or pigtail catheter over the wire and secure it in the posterior pericardial<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>space<italicout>
  • <italicin>Connect the catheter to a 3-way stopcock, and <italicout>ﬂ<italicin>uid should be extracted slowly, monitoring for blood pressure and<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>ectopy<italicout>
  • <italicin>Continuous echocardiographic monitoring is useful for location of<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>the wire and catheter as well as for monitoring adequate extraction of pericardial<italicout><italicin> <italicout>ﬂ<italicin>uid<italicout>
  • <italicin>Once catheter position is con<italicout>ﬁ<italicin>rmed, the catheter should be secured with sutures and the entire site covered sterilely to minimize<italicout><italicin> <italicout><italicin>infection<italicout>

<boldin>Interpretations<boldout>
  • Send pericardial fluid for cell count, protein, glucose, lactic dehydrogenase, cytology as well as all other studies for infectious agents
  • Normal pericardial fluid is clear to straw colored, scant (/< 50 ml) with /< 500 white blood cells/mcl
  • An elevated white blood cell count suggests either an infectious or inflammatory process
  • Protein, glucose, and lactic dehydrogenase can be helpful in differentiating a transudate from an exudate
  • Metastasis to the pericardial space or pericardial tumors are frequently exudative, with abnormalities seen on cytology

<boldin>Monitoring<boldout>
  • Monitor the patient closely for rhythm disturbances and unstable blood pressure
  • Patients may require fluid resuscitation if large amounts of fluid are extracted from the pericardial space
<boldin>• Remove the fluid slowly<boldout>
<boldin>• Replace with isotonic fluid, if possible<boldout>
  • Large children with nephrotic syndrome can have as much as 1–2L of pericardial fluid and be relatively asymptomatic

<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Compromise in the hemodynamic status<boldout>
<boldin>• Bleeding<boldout>
  • Can be superﬁcial and easily controlled with pressure
  • Deeper bleeding from either a liver or splenic injury may be less obvious and more difficult to control.
<boldin>• A coronary injury is rare but potentially catastrophic (as is a cardiac perforation):<boldout>
  • Requires emergent intervention by a cardiac surgeon
<boldin>• Arrhythmias are typically transient<boldout>
  • Can be managed by repositioning the needle or catheter
  • Occasionally, more persistent rhythm disturbances occur that require antiarrhythmic therapies
  • Vasovagal response after successful pericardial decompression or left ventricular dysfunction can occur
  • Pneumoperitoneum or small pneumothorax: Requires careful monitoring but can be self-limited
  • Pneumopericardium: Should resolve as long as the pericardiocentesis catheter is secure, in proper position, and connected to negative pressure

topics.title: Chapter 36: Nasogastric Tube Insertion
topics.content_html:
<boldin>Indications<boldout>
<boldin>• Decompression of upper GIT<boldout>
  • Gastric lavage - for poisoning or overdose of medications
  • Enteral feeding – for pts unable to eat by mouth or swallow sufficient diet
  • Administration of medications – for pts unable to swallow medications
<boldin>• To obtain specimen from gastric contents<boldout>
  • After bowel surgery - to promote healing
  • To administer radiographic contrast media to GIT imaging
<boldin>Contraindications<boldout>
<boldin>Absolute<boldout>
<boldin>• Unstable airway<boldout>
<boldin>• Intestinal perforation<boldout>
<boldin>• Cervical spine trauma<boldout>
<boldin>• Severe mid facial trauma<boldout>
<boldin>• Recent nasal surgery<boldout>
<boldin>Relative<boldout>
<boldin>• Coagulopathy (prothrombin time /> 18 seconds)<boldout>
<boldin>• Thrombocytopenia (platelet count /< 100,000/mcl)<boldout>
  • Recent intestinal tract surgery (/< 1 month ago)
<boldin>• Esophageal stricture<boldout>
<boldin>Equipments<boldout>
<boldin>• Lubricant gel<boldout>
<boldin>• Nasogastric (NG) tube:<boldout>
  • Larger diameter, polyethylene NG tube for suction and decompression
  • Smaller diameter, silicone NG tube for enteral feeding
  • Water or normal saline at room temperature
<boldin>• Drainage bag or feeding pump<boldout>
  • 60-ml catheter tip syringe
<boldin>• Stethoscope<boldout>
<boldin>Patient preparation and position<boldout>
  • Explain indication and risks to the patient and parents
  • Inform the patient of the intention of the procedure
<boldin>• Patient should be sitting<boldout>
<boldin>Procedure<boldout>
  • Measure the length of insertion from the nares to the ear and to the epigastrium - mark it on the tube with an indelible pen
<boldin>• Lubricate tube with gel<boldout>
<boldin>• Insert the tube through the nose<boldout>
  • Ask the patient to cooperate by swallowing while the tube is being inserted
  • Advance the tube to the length mark
  • Secure tube to the face with tape
<boldin>Length of the tube inserted<boldout>
  • Measure length of tube insertion by positioning the tube from the nares or mouth to the ear, then to the umbilicus

<boldin>How to check the tube correct placement?<boldout>
  • Aspirate the tube with 50-ml syringe: Gastric aspirate (pH = 1–3) confirms positioning in stomach
  • Insert small amount of air (20–30 ml) via NG tube while listening at epigastric area with stethoscope → gurgling sound in the epigastrium → tube is in the stomach
<boldin>• Chest x-ray<boldout>
<boldin>Monitoring<boldout>
<boldin>• Monitor intake and output volume<boldout>
<boldin>• Evaluate tube position<boldout>
<boldin>• Patient symptoms<boldout>
<boldin>Complications<boldout>
<boldin>• Discomfort<boldout>
  • Sinusitis (caused by long-term NG tube feeding)
<boldin>• Bleeding<boldout>
<boldin>• GERD<boldout>
<boldin>• Esophageal<boldout>
<boldin>• Malposition (respiratory tree insertion)<boldout>
<boldin>Follow up<boldout>
Follow for the following sign and symptoms
<boldin>• Fever<boldout>
<boldin>• Nausea and vomiting<boldout>
<boldin>• Melanotic stool or bright red hematemesis<boldout>
<boldin>• Persistent abdominal pain<boldout>
<boldin>• Abdominal distention<boldout>
<boldin>• Chest pain<boldout>

topics.title: Chapter 37: TRIADS
topics.content_html:
<boldin>CHF in infants<boldout>
    • Tachycardia
    • Cardiomegaly
    • Tachypnea
<boldin>Sydenham chorea<boldout>
    • Chorea
    • Hypotonia
    • Emotional liability
<boldin>Cardiac tamponade (Beck's triad)<boldout>
    • Decreased arterial BP
    • Raised JVP
    • Distant heart sound
<boldin>PCP<boldout>
    • Dyspnea
    • Fever
    • Dry cough
<boldin>HUS<boldout>
    • MAHA
    • Renal insufficiency
    • Thrombocytopenia
<boldin>Pheochromocytoma<boldout>
    • Episodic headache
    • Sweating
    • Tachycardia
<boldin>Cloacal exstrophy<boldout>
    • Omphalocele
    • Bladder exstrophy
    • Imperforate anus
<boldin>Croup<boldout>
    • Barking cough
    • Hoarseness of voice
    • Stridor
<boldin>Epiglottitis = 3D's<boldout>
    • Drooling
    • Dysphagia
    • Dyspnea
<boldin>Meningitis in infants<boldout>
    • Fever
    • Irritability
    • Vomiting
<boldin>Cushing triad - late signs of herniation<boldout>
    • Bradycardia
    • Hypertension
    • Irregular breathing pattern
<boldin>Brain abscess<boldout>
    • Headache
    • Fever
    • Focal neurologic deficit
<boldin>Infectious mononucleosis<boldout>
    • Fever
    • Pharyngitis
    • Lymphadenopathy
<boldin>Pellagra = 4D's<boldout>
    • Dermatitis
    • Diarrhoea
    • Dementia
    • Death if untreated
<boldin>Congenital toxoplasmosis<boldout>
    • Chorioretinities
    • Hydrocephalus
    • Intracranial calcification
<boldin>Congenital rubella<boldout>
    • Microcephaly
    • PDA
    • Cataract
<boldin>Intussusception<boldout>
    • Abdominal pain
    • Palpable abdominal mass
    • Blood in the stool
<boldin>Febrile UTI<boldout>
    • Fever
    • Vomiting
    • Flank pain
<boldin>Measles<boldout>
    • Fever
    • Maculopapular rash
    • One of the 3'C' s
    • Conjuctivitis
    • Coryza
    • Cough
